Imagine a **corporate office** where the CEO (output layer) makes a wrong business decision. The CEO must now figure out **whose fault it is** — was it the middle manager (hidden layer 2) or the junior executive (hidden layer 1) who passed along flawed data?

- The **forward pass** is the decision being made, top-down.
- The **loss function** is the size of the financial loss.
- The **backward pass** is the post-mortem meeting where **blame (gradient)** is distributed proportionally to each person's contribution to the mistake.
- The **weight update** is the policy correction — those who caused more of the error get stricter "retraining" (larger weight adjustments).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *"Forward to predict, Backward to blame, Update to improve."* This three-step mantra is how KTU examiners expect you to describe the backprop cycle.

### 1.3 Why Backpropagation Matters in Modern AI

Backpropagation is the **computational engine** that powers:
- **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** for image recognition
- **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** and **LSTMs** for sequence modeling
- **Transformers** (via backpropagation through time and self-attention gradients)
- **Generative models** (GANs, VAEs, Diffusion models)

Without efficient gradient computation, training deep networks with millions of parameters would be **computationally intractable**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Phases

#### **Phase 1: Forward Pass (Inference)**
1. Input vector $\mathbf{x}$ is fed into the input layer.
2. Each neuron in layer $l$ computes a **pre-activation** $z^{(l)}_j = \sum_i W^{(l)}_{ji} a^{(l-1)}_i + b^{(l)}_j$.
3. An **activation function** $f(\cdot)$ is applied: $a^{(l)}_j = f(z^{(l)}_j)$.
4. The process continues layer-by-layer until the output $\hat{y} = a^{(L)}$ is produced.
5. The **loss** $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ is computed.

#### **Phase 2: Backward Pass (Credit Assignment)**
1. Compute the **error signal** at the output layer: $\delta^{(L)}$.
2. Propagate the error **backwards** using the chain rule: $\delta^{(l)} = ((W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)}) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$.
3. Compute weight gradients: $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$.
4. Update weights using gradient descent: $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$.

### 2.2 The Chain Rule — The Mathematical Heart ❤️

The **multivariate chain rule** is the engine that makes backpropagation possible. For a composite function $L = f(g(h(x)))$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial g} \cdot \frac{\partial g}{\partial h} \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

Each layer of the neural network is a **nested function**, so the total gradient is simply the **product of local gradients** along the path from the loss back to the weight.

### 2.3 Common Activation Functions & Their Derivatives

| Function | Formula $f(z)$ | Derivative $f'(z)$ | Range |
|----------|---------------|--------------------|-------|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | $(-1, 1)$ |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | $0 \text{ if } z \le 0, \; 1 \text{ if } z > 0$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z)$, $\alpha = 0.01$ | $\alpha \text{ if } z \le 0, \; 1 \text{ if } z > 0$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vanishing Gradient Warning:** For Sigmoid and Tanh, the derivative $f'(z)$ is always $\le 0.25$ and $\le 1$ respectively. When multiplied across many layers, gradients **shrink exponentially**, causing slow learning in deep networks. This is why **ReLU** is the default in modern deep learning.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Definition** |
|------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Loss (cost) function | e.g., MSE: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ |
| $W^{(l)}$ | Weight matrix of layer $l$ | Shape: $(\text{neurons}_l \times \text{neurons}_{l-1})$ |
| $b^{(l)}$ | Bias vector of layer $l$ | Shape: $(\text{neurons}_l, 1)$ |
| $z^{(l)}$ | Pre-activation at layer $l$ | $z^{(l)} = W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ |
| $a^{(l)}$ | Post-activation (output) at layer $l$ | $a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$ |
| $\delta^{(l)}$ | Error signal at layer $l$ | $\delta^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$ |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate (hyperparameter) | Typical range: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $W^{(l)}_{\text{new}}$ | Updated weight | $W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ |
| $\delta^{(L)}$ | Output-layer error (MSE + Sigmoid) | $(\hat{y} - y) \odot f'(z^{(L)})$ |
| $\delta^{(l)}$ | Hidden-layer error (general) | $(W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot f'(z^{(l)})$ |
| $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ | Weight gradient | $\delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$ |
| $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$ | Bias gradient | $\delta^{(l)}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** The symbol $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**. The symbol $\vert$ is reserved for absolute value or determinant — never confuse them.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Backpropagation-trained CNNs detect tumors in MRI scans with superhuman accuracy.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** End-to-end self-driving models (NVIDIA's PilotNet) use backprop to map camera pixels directly to steering angles.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT are trained via backpropagation over billions of parameters.
- **Financial Forecasting:** Time-series models for stock prediction rely on backpropagation through time (BPTT).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Problem Setup — A 2-Layer Neural Network

Consider the simplest non-trivial network: **1 input → 1 hidden layer (2 neurons) → 1 output neuron**.

- Input: $x \in \mathbb{R}$
- Hidden layer pre-activations: $z^{(1)}_1, z^{(1)}_2$
- Hidden layer activations: $a^{(1)}_1 = \sigma(z^{(1)}_1), \; a^{(1)}_2 = \sigma(z^{(1)}_2)$
- Output: $\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)})$
- Loss: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$ (Mean Squared Error, single sample)

The architecture is:

$$x \xrightarrow{W^{(1)}, b^{(1)}} z^{(1)} \xrightarrow{\sigma} a^{(1)} \xrightarrow{W^{(2)}, b^{(2)}} z^{(2)} \xrightarrow{\sigma} \hat{y} \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} \text{Loss}$$

### 3.2 Forward Pass — Complete Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute pre-activations of the hidden layer.

$$z^{(1)}_1 = w^{(1)}_{11} x + b^{(1)}_1$$

$$z^{(1)}_2 = w^{(1)}_{21} x + b^{(1)}_2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the sigmoid activation.

$$a^{(1)}_1 = \sigma(z^{(1)}_1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(1)}_1}}$$

$$a^{(1)}_2 = \sigma(z^{(1)}_2) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(1)}_2}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the output pre-activation.

$$z^{(2)} = w^{(2)}_1 a^{(1)}_1 + w^{(2)}_2 a^{(1)}_2 + b^{(2)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply sigmoid to obtain the prediction.

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(2)}}}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the scalar loss.

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor $\frac{1}{2}$?** It cancels with the exponent 2 during differentiation, yielding $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = (\hat{y} - y)$ instead of $2(\hat{y} - y)$. This is a **standard convention** expected in KTU derivations.

### 3.3 Backward Pass — Derivation of Output-Layer Gradients

We begin by computing the gradient of the loss with respect to $\hat{y}$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}} \left[ \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2(y - \hat{y}) \cdot (-1) = \hat{y} - y$$

**Next**, the gradient with respect to the output pre-activation $z^{(2)}$ uses the chain rule.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}}$$

Since $\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)})$, we use the famous identity $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

Therefore, the **error signal at the output layer** is:

$$\delta^{(2)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

**Now**, the gradients with respect to the output-layer weights $w^{(2)}_1, w^{(2)}_2$ and bias $b^{(2)}$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(2)}_1} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial w^{(2)}_1} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)}_1$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(2)}_2} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial w^{(2)}_2} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)}_2$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)}$$

### 3.4 Backward Pass — Derivation of Hidden-Layer Gradients

This is the **most important step** for examination purposes. We must propagate the error **back** to the hidden layer. The chain rule gives:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(1)}_j} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial a^{(1)}_j} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot w^{(2)}_j$$

Then for the hidden-layer pre-activation:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(1)}_j} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(1)}_j} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(1)}_j}{\partial z^{(1)}_j} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot w^{(2)}_j \cdot a^{(1)}_j (1 - a^{(1)}_j)$$

Therefore, the **error signal at the hidden layer** (neuron $j$) is:

$$\delta^{(1)}_j = \delta^{(2)} \cdot w^{(2)}_j \cdot a^{(1)}_j (1 - a^{(1)}_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization:** For a network with $L$ layers, the recursive formula is:
>
> $$\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot f'(z^{(l)})$$
>
> This is the **single equation** that defines the backward pass.

**Finally**, the gradients for the input-to-hidden weights and biases are:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(1)}_{j1}} = \delta^{(1)}_j \cdot x$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}_j} = \delta^{(1)}_j$$

### 3.5 The Weight Update Rule

For each weight, we apply the **Vanilla Gradient Descent** update:

$$W^{(l)}_{ij} \leftarrow W^{(l)}_{ij} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}_{ij}}$$

Concretely, for our 2-layer network:

$$w^{(2)}_1 \leftarrow w^{(2)}_1 - \eta \cdot \delta^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)}_1$$

$$w^{(1)}_{11} \leftarrow w^{(1)}_{11} - \eta \cdot \delta^{(1)}_1 \cdot x$$

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — Single Training Step

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **typical KTU numerical problem**. Practice this until you can reproduce it from memory.

**Given:**
- Input: $x = 1.0$, True label: $y = 0$
- Initial weights: $w^{(1)}_{11} = 0.5, \; w^{(1)}_{21} = -0.3, \; w^{(2)}_1 = 0.8, \; w^{(2)}_2 = -0.4$
- All biases: $b = 0$
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.5$

**Forward Pass:**

$$z^{(1)}_1 = 0.5(1.0) + 0 = 0.5 \Rightarrow a^{(1)}_1 = \sigma(0.5) = 0.6225$$

$$z^{(1)}_2 = -0.3(1.0) + 0 = -0.3 \Rightarrow a^{(1)}_2 = \sigma(-0.3) = 0.4256$$

$$z^{(2)} = 0.8(0.6225) + (-0.4)(0.4256) + 0 = 0.4980 - 0.1702 = 0.3278$$

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(0.3278) = 0.5812$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(0 - 0.5812)^2 = 0.1689$$

**Backward Pass:**

$$\delta^{(2)} = (0.5812 - 0)(0.5812)(1 - 0.5812) = (0.5812)(0.2432) = 0.1414$$

$$\delta^{(1)}_1 = 0.1414 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.6225(1 - 0.6225) = 0.1414 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.2350 = 0.0266$$

$$\delta^{(1)}_2 = 0.1414 \cdot (-0.4) \cdot 0.4256(1 - 0.4256) = 0.1414 \cdot (-0.4) \cdot 0.2444 = -0.0138$$

**Weight Updates:**

$$w^{(2)}_1 \leftarrow 0.8 - 0.5 \cdot 0.1414 \cdot 0.6225 = 0.8 - 0.0440 = 0.7560$$

$$w^{(2)}_2 \leftarrow -0.4 - 0.5 \cdot 0.1414 \cdot 0.4256 = -0.4 - 0.0301 = -0.4301$$

$$w^{(1)}_{11} \leftarrow 0.5 - 0.5 \cdot 0.0266 \cdot 1.0 = 0.4867$$

$$w^{(1)}_{21} \leftarrow -0.3 - 0.5 \cdot (-0.0138) \cdot 1.0 = -0.2931$$

After this single step, the loss **decreases** — proof that the algorithm is working correctly.

### 3.7 Variants of Gradient Descent Used with Backpropagation

| **Variant** | **Update Rule** | **Key Property** |
|-------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Batch GD** | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_{\text{full}}$ | Stable but slow on large data |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_{x_i}$ | Noisy but fast convergence |
| **Mini-batch GD** | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}}$ | Best of both worlds (industry standard) |
| **SGD + Momentum** | $v \leftarrow \beta v + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$, $W \leftarrow W - v$ | Escapes local minima; $\beta \approx 0.9$ |
| **RMSProp** | Adaptive per-parameter learning rate | Handles sparse gradients well |
| **Adam** | Combines Momentum + RMSProp | Default optimizer in deep learning |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Multi-Layer Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1[Input x1] --> h1n1[Hidden Node h11]
    inp2[Input x2] --> h1n1
    inp3[Input x3] --> h1n1
    inp1 --> h1n2[Hidden Node h12]
    inp2 --> h1n2
    inp3 --> h1n2
    inp1 --> h1n3[Hidden Node h13]
    inp2 --> h1n3
    inp3 --> h1n3
    inp1 --> h1n4[Hidden Node h14]
    inp2 --> h1n4
    inp3 --> h1n4
    h1n1 --> h2n1[Hidden Node h21]
    h1n2 --> h2n1
    h1n3 --> h2n1
    h1n4 --> h2n1
    h1n1 --> h2n2[Hidden Node h22]
    h1n2 --> h2n2
    h1n3 --> h2n2
    h1n4 --> h2n2
    h1n1 --> h2n3[Hidden Node h23]
    h1n2 --> h2n3
    h1n3 --> h2n3
    h1n4 --> h2n3
    h2n1 --> out1[Output y1]
    h2n2 --> out1
    h2n3 --> out1
    h2n1 --> out2[Output y2]
    h2n2 --> out2
    h2n3 --> out2
```

### 4.2 Forward Pass & Backward Pass Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Vector x] --> B[Layer 1: z1 = W1 x + b1]
    B --> C[Activation a1 = sigmoid of z1]
    C --> D[Layer 2: z2 = W2 a1 + b2]
    D --> E[Activation a2 = sigmoid of z2]
    E --> F[Output yhat]
    F --> G[Loss L = half times y minus yhat squared]
    G --> H[Compute delta2: error at output layer]
    H --> I[Compute delta1: propagate error to hidden layer]
    I --> J[Compute weight gradients]
    J --> K[Update weights using gradient descent]
    K --> A
```

### 4.3 Mathematical Signal Flow — The Computational Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L[Loss L] --> dLdyhat[dL by dyhat equals dyhat minus y]
    dLdyhat --> dLdz2[dL by dz2 equals delta2]
    dLdz2 --> dLdw21[dL by dw21 equals delta2 times a1_1]
    dLdz2 --> dLdw22[dL by dw22 equals delta2 times a1_2]
    dLdz2 --> dLda1[dL by da1 equals delta2 times W2]
    dLda1 --> dLdz1[dL by dz1 equals delta1]
    dLdz1 --> dLdw11[dL by dw11 equals delta1 times x]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a "computational graph" in the exam, this left-to-right forward / right-to-left backward visualization is the most commonly accepted KTU format.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define backpropagation. State the role of the chain rule in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Backpropagation is a supervised learning algorithm used to train multi-layer neural networks by computing the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight through a backward pass from output to input.

The **chain rule** of calculus plays a central role: it allows the gradient at any layer to be expressed as a product of local gradients along the path from the loss to that layer. Formally, for a composite function $L = f(g(h(x)))$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial g} \cdot \frac{\partial g}{\partial h} \cdot \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

Without the chain rule, we would need to compute the gradient analytically for every weight — a computationally infeasible task for deep networks. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is the vanishing gradient problem? How does ReLU help mitigate it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs when gradients shrink exponentially as they propagate from the output layer to earlier layers during backpropagation, causing the weights of early layers to update negligibly.

This happens because activation functions like **Sigmoid** and **Tanh** have derivatives bounded by $0.25$ and $1$ respectively. When these small values are multiplied across many layers, the gradient approaches zero.

**ReLU** mitigates this because its derivative is exactly $1$ for all positive inputs. Therefore, gradients flow backward through ReLU activations without being multiplied by a small factor, preserving signal strength in deep networks. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model — Sub-Question Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the weight update rule for a 2-layer feedforward neural network with sigmoid activation and Mean Squared Error loss. Show all steps of the forward and backward pass clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a network with one hidden layer of 2 neurons, input $x = 0.5$, true output $y = 1.0$, learning rate $\eta = 0.1$, and initial weights as below, perform **one complete iteration** of backpropagation and show the updated weights.

| Weight | Initial Value |
|--------|---------------|
| $w^{(1)}_{11}$ | $0.2$ |
| $w^{(1)}_{21}$ | $-0.1$ |
| $w^{(2)}_1$ | $0.5$ |
| $w^{(2)}_2$ | $-0.3$ |
| $b^{(1)}_1$ | $0.1$ |
| $b^{(1)}_2$ | $-0.2$ |
| $b^{(2)}$ | $0.05$ |

Use Sigmoid activation and MSE loss. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Full Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Architecture and Notation (1 mark):**

Let the network have:
- Input: $x$
- Hidden layer activations: $a^{(1)}_1, a^{(1)}_2$
- Output: $\hat{y}$
- Loss: $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$

**Step 2 — Forward Pass (2 marks):**

The hidden pre-activations are:

$$z^{(1)}_j = w^{(1)}_{j1} x + b^{(1)}_j, \quad j = 1, 2$$

The hidden activations:

$$a^{(1)}_j = \sigma(z^{(1)}_j) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z^{(1)}_j}}$$

Output pre-activation and output:

$$z^{(2)} = w^{(2)}_1 a^{(1)}_1 + w^{(2)}_2 a^{(1)}_2 + b^{(2)}$$

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z^{(2)})$$

**Step 3 — Backward Pass — Output Layer (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = \hat{y} - y$$

$$\delta^{(2)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y)\hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(2)}_j} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a^{(1)}_j, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)}$$

**Step 4 — Backward Pass — Hidden Layer (1 mark):**

$$\delta^{(1)}_j = \delta^{(2)} \cdot w^{(2)}_j \cdot a^{(1)}_j(1 - a^{(1)}_j)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(1)}_{j1}} = \delta^{(1)}_j \cdot x, \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(1)}_j} = \delta^{(1)}_j$$

**Step 5 — Weight Update (1 mark):**

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Forward Pass:**

$$z^{(1)}_1 = 0.2(0.5) + 0.1 = 0.20 \Rightarrow a^{(1)}_1 = \sigma(0.20) = 0.5498$$

$$z^{(1)}_2 = -0.1(0.5) + (-0.2) = -0.25 \Rightarrow a^{(1)}_2 = \sigma(-0.25) = 0.4378$$

$$z^{(2)} = 0.5(0.5498) + (-0.3)(0.4378) + 0.05 = 0.2749 - 0.1314 + 0.05 = 0.1935$$

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(0.1935) = 0.5482$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(1.0 - 0.5482)^2 = 0.1020$$

**Backward Pass:**

$$\delta^{(2)} = (0.5482 - 1.0)(0.5482)(1 - 0.5482) = (-0.4518)(0.2477) = -0.1119$$

$$\delta^{(1)}_1 = (-0.1119)(0.5)(0.5498)(1 - 0.5498) = (-0.1119)(0.5)(0.2476) = -0.0139$$

$$\delta^{(1)}_2 = (-0.1119)(-0.3)(0.4378)(1 - 0.4378) = (-0.1119)(-0.3)(0.2461) = 0.0083$$

**Weight Updates:**

$$w^{(2)}_1 \leftarrow 0.5 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.1119) \cdot 0.5498 = 0.5 + 0.00615 = 0.5062$$

$$w^{(2)}_2 \leftarrow -0.3 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.1119) \cdot 0.4378 = -0.3 + 0.00490 = -0.2951$$

$$w^{(1)}_{11} \leftarrow 0.2 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.0139) \cdot 0.5 = 0.2007$$

$$w^{(1)}_{21} \leftarrow -0.1 - 0.1 \cdot 0.0083 \cdot 0.5 = -0.1004$$

$$b^{(2)} \leftarrow 0.05 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.1119) = 0.0612$$

$$b^{(1)}_1 \leftarrow 0.1 - 0.1 \cdot (-0.0139) = 0.1014$$

$$b^{(1)}_2 \leftarrow -0.2 - 0.1 \cdot 0.0083 = -0.2008$$

**(Full 7 marks awarded for correct numerical values within tolerance ±0.001.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the vanishing and exploding gradient problems in detail. Discuss how each can be mitigated with specific architectural or optimization techniques. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Batch Gradient Descent**, **Stochastic Gradient Descent**, **Mini-batch Gradient Descent**, and **Adam Optimizer** in terms of convergence speed, memory requirement, and suitability for large-scale deep learning. Use a comparison table. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline for Question B:**

**Part (a):**
- **Vanishing gradient:** Gradients $\to 0$ in early layers; causes slow training.
  - **Mitigations:** ReLU activation, Batch Normalization, Residual connections (ResNets), LSTM-style gating.
- **Exploding gradient:** Gradients $\to \infty$; causes numerical instability (NaN losses).
  - **Mitigations:** Gradient clipping (threshold $\tau$), weight initialization (Xavier, He), smaller learning rate.

**Part (b):**

| Optimizer | Convergence Speed | Memory | Large-Scale Use |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|------------------|
| Batch GD | Slow, stable | High (full dataset) | Poor |
| SGD | Fast, noisy | Low | Good for simple models |
| Mini-batch GD | Balanced | Medium | Industry standard |
| Adam | Fast, adaptive | Medium | Best for deep learning |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$** in MSE: Causes derivative to be $2(\hat{y} - y)$ instead of $(\hat{y} - y)$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing the chain rule direction:** Always differentiate *backwards* from $\mathcal{L}$ to $w$. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up $\delta$ and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}$:** $\delta$ is the error signal (pre-activation gradient), not the weight gradient. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the sigmoid derivative simplification:** Writing $\sigma'(z)$ is incomplete; you must show $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Not showing intermediate numerical values:** Examiners expect explicit substitution of $\sigma(z)$ values like $0.5498$ in the worked example. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting bias gradients:** Biases also need updating — many students only update weights. (–1 mark)

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Backpropagation = Chain Rule + Reverse-Mode Automatic Differentiation + Gradient Descent.**
- **Forward pass:** Compute activations layer by layer; cache all $z^{(l)}$ and $a^{(l)}$ for reuse.
- **Backward pass:** Compute $\delta^{(L)}$, then propagate via $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \odot f'(z^{(l)})$.
- **Sigmoid derivative shortcut:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — never re-derive from scratch in exams.
- **Weight update:** $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$.
- **Bias update:** $b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \delta^{(l)}$.
- **Learning rate $\eta$ is critical:** Too large → divergence; too small → slow convergence.
- **Vanishing gradient:** Mitigated by ReLU, BatchNorm, ResNets.
- **Exploding gradient:** Mitigated by gradient clipping, careful initialization.
- **Mini-batch + Adam** is the de facto standard for modern deep learning training.
- **Computational efficiency:** Backprop runs in $O(W)$ time — same order as a forward pass. This is what makes deep learning feasible.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Forward store, backward blame, update with care."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regularization Techniques - Overfitting and Underfitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regularization Techniques - Overfitting and Underfitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (CO1 Mapping):**
> *Regularization* refers to a family of mathematical and structural techniques used in machine learning to constrain a model's complexity, thereby improving its **generalization** to unseen data. The two pathological extremes it aims to address are **Overfitting** (high variance) and **Underfitting** (high bias).

### 1.1 The Three Regimes of Model Learning

| Regime | Training Error | Test Error | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underfitting** | High | High | Model is too simple; cannot capture the underlying data pattern |
| **Good Fit** | Low | Low (≈ Training Error) | Model captures the true signal and generalizes well |
| **Overfitting** | Very Low (≈ 0) | Very High | Model memorizes noise and training-specific quirks |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Student Cramming for an Exam

Imagine three students preparing for an exam from a 200-page textbook:

- **Underfitting student**: Reads only the chapter summaries. Has a vague idea but cannot answer detailed questions. → *High training error, high test error.*
- **Well-fit student**: Understands the underlying principles and can solve *new* problems from the same chapter. → *Low training error, low test error.*
- **Overfitting student**: Memorizes the textbook **word-for-word** including all the typos, coffee stains, and page numbers. Crashes on any new question. → *Training error ≈ 0, test error very high.*

Regularization is like asking the cramming student to *"focus on concepts, not memorization"* — it penalizes excessive complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization** is the ability of a trained model to maintain low prediction error on data it has *never seen* during training. KTU examiners expect this term to be used precisely in definitions.

### 1.3 Core Causes (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causes of Overfitting:**
> 1. **Model Complexity** — too many parameters relative to data size.
> 2. **Noisy Training Data** — the model fits random noise as if it were signal.
> 3. **Insufficient Training Data** — fewer samples than parameters.
> 4. **Excessive Training Epochs** — model keeps refining past the optimal point.
>
> **Causes of Underfitting:**
> 1. **Oversimplified Model** — linear model used for non-linear data.
> 2. **Excessive Regularization** — over-aggressive penalties collapse the model.
> 3. **Insufficient Training** — model not given enough iterations to learn.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics You Must Know

- **λ (lambda)**: the **regularization hyperparameter** that controls the strength of the penalty term. Default range: $\lambda \in [10^{-5}, \, 10^{3}]$.
- **Degrees of Freedom (df)**: number of independent parameters effectively used by the model.
- **Effective Complexity**: reduced by regularization even when nominal parameters are fixed.
- **L2 Norm**: $\Vert \theta \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_i^2}$ (Euclidean norm).
- **L1 Norm**: $\Vert \theta \Vert_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \theta_i \vert$ (Manhattan norm).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Foundation of Regularization)

For any supervised regression problem with squared loss, the **expected prediction error** at a point $x$ can be decomposed as:

$$E\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}$$

- **Bias²** — error from erroneous assumptions in the learning algorithm. *High bias = underfitting.*
- **Variance** — error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in the training set. *High variance = overfitting.*
- $\sigma^2_{\text{irreducible}}$ — the noise term that *no* model can remove.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Increasing model complexity *decreases* bias but *increases* variance. Regularization trades a small rise in bias for a large drop in variance, often producing a net reduction in test error.

### 2.2 The Generalized Regularized Cost Function

Every regularization technique modifies the objective (loss) function from $J_0(\theta)$ to $J(\theta)$:

$$J(\theta) = \underbrace{J_0(\theta)}_{\text{Data-fit term}} + \underbrace{\lambda \, \Omega(\theta)}_{\text{Regularization penalty}}$$

where:
- $\theta$ = model parameter vector of dimension $p$.
- $\lambda \geq 0$ = regularization strength.
- $\Omega(\theta)$ = penalty function (L1, L2, Elastic Net, etc.).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Penalty $\Omega(\theta)$ | Modified Objective $J(\theta)$ | Gradient Update Effect | Sparsity? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **No Regularization** | $0$ | $J_0(\theta)$ | Pure data-driven | No |
| **L2 (Ridge)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \theta_i^2$ | $J_0(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{p} \theta_i^2$ | $\theta_i \leftarrow \theta_i(1 - 2\eta\lambda) - \eta \frac{\partial J_0}{\partial \theta_i}$ | No (shrinks smoothly) |
| **L1 (Lasso)** | $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \vert \theta_i \vert$ | $J_0(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{p} \vert \theta_i \vert$ | Soft-thresholding: $\theta_i \leftarrow \text{sign}(\theta_i)\max(\vert \theta_i \vert - \eta\lambda, 0)$ | **Yes** (drives some $\theta_i = 0$) |
| **Elastic Net** | $\alpha \sum \vert \theta_i \vert + (1-\alpha)\sum \theta_i^2$ | Combined L1 + L2 with mixing parameter $\alpha$ | Mixed shrinkage + thresholding | Partial |
| **Dropout (NN)** | Stochastic masking of units | Implicit ensemble penalty | Each iteration trains a sub-network | N/A |
| **Early Stopping** | Constraint on iteration count $T$ | $J_T(\theta) < J_{T-1}$ on val set | Halt when validation loss rises | Approximate |
| **Data Augmentation** | Increases $N_{\text{effective}}$ | Same $J_0$ but smoother | Reduces variance via more samples | No |
| **Batch Norm** | Normalization constraint | Internal covariate shift reduction | Stabilizes gradients | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** All absolute value notations in the KTU exam should be written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$. When typing in plain markdown tables, prefer the LaTeX command `\vert` to avoid breaking table column dividers.

### 2.4 Why L1 Produces Sparsity (Geometric Intuition)

The constraint region imposed by L1 is a **diamond** in parameter space, while the unconstrained loss contours are *ellipses*. The diamond's *corners* lie on the axes — at exactly those points, one or more $\theta_i = 0$. For L2, the constraint is a **circle** that touches the ellipse at a point where all $\theta_i$ are typically non-zero.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis (CT scan classification):** L2 regularization prevents the network from overfitting to hospital-specific scanner artifacts.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Dropout + data augmentation handle the long tail of rare road scenarios.
- **Financial Forecasting:** Lasso (L1) is used to perform **automatic feature selection**, producing interpretable risk models.
- **NLP (Large Language Models):** Weight Decay (L2) and Early Stopping are universal in pre-training pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Bias-Variance Decomposition

We start with the squared error at a fixed test point $x$, where the true label is $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ with $E[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] &= E\left[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] \\
&= E\left[(\varepsilon + (f(x) - \hat{f}(x)))^2\right] \\
&= E[\varepsilon^2] + 2 E[\varepsilon]\, E[f(x) - \hat{f}(x)] + E[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2] \\
&= E[\varepsilon^2] + 0 + E[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2] \\
&= \sigma^2 + E[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2]
\end{aligned}
$$

Next, we add and subtract the expected prediction $E[\hat{f}(x)]$ inside the second term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2] &= E\left[\bigl(f(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)] + E[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\bigr)^2\right] \\
&= (f(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + E[(\hat{f}(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)])^2] \\
&\quad + 2(f(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)])\underbrace{E[E[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)]}_{= \, 0} \\
&= \underbrace{(f(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{E[(\hat{f}(x) - E[\hat{f}(x)])^2]}_{\text{Variance}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining the two derivations yields the **canonical bias-variance-noise decomposition** used in every KTU 2024 exam:

$$E\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This decomposition holds strictly only for squared loss. For 0-1 loss the form is different, but the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the regression/squared-error form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: L2 Regularization Gradient Descent Update Rule

Start with the L2-regularized cost for a linear model with $J_0(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\theta_j^2$$

**Step 1** — Take the partial derivative with respect to $\theta_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} &= \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})\, x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute into the gradient descent update $\theta_j := \theta_j - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_j &:= \theta_j - \eta \left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})\, x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j\right] \\
\theta_j &:= \theta_j \left(1 - \eta\frac{\lambda}{m}\right) - \eta\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})\, x_j^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Decay Interpretation:** The factor $(1 - \eta \lambda / m) < 1$ causes the parameter to *shrink* on every step even when the data-fit gradient is zero. This is why L2 regularization is also called **Weight Decay** in deep learning literature.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: L1 Soft-Thresholding Operator (Sub-Gradient Analysis)

For Lasso, $J(\theta) = J_0(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert \theta_j \vert$. The L1 term is **not differentiable** at $\theta_j = 0$, so we use the sub-gradient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \, \vert \theta_j \vert}{\partial \theta_j} &= \begin{cases} +1, & \theta_j > 0 \\ -1, & \theta_j < 0 \\ \in [-1, +1], & \theta_j = 0 \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the gradient descent update for a single parameter $\theta_j$ where the data-gradient is denoted $g_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_j^{(t+1)} &= \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta(g_j + \lambda \, \text{sign}(\theta_j^{(t)})) \\
&= \underbrace{\bigl(\theta_j^{(t)} - \eta \, g_j\bigr)}_{\text{Unregularized update}} - \eta\lambda \, \text{sign}(\theta_j^{(t)})
\end{aligned}
$$

After convergence, the **soft-thresholding operator** is obtained:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_j^* &= \begin{cases} \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} - \eta\lambda, & \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} > +\eta\lambda \\ 0, & \vert \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} \vert \leq \eta\lambda \\ \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} + \eta\lambda, & \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} < -\eta\lambda \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** If a feature's "natural" weight (from data alone) is small in magnitude (below the threshold $\eta\lambda$), L1 regularization sets its weight **exactly to zero**, achieving built-in feature selection.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| High-dimensional data, need feature selection | **Lasso (L1)** | Produces sparse weights; many $\theta_i = 0$ |
| Multicollinear features, all potentially useful | **Ridge (L2)** | Shrinks correlated coefficients together; retains all |
| Mix of the two above | **Elastic Net** | L1 selects; L2 stabilizes correlated survivors |
| Deep Neural Network | **Dropout + Weight Decay** | Prevents co-adaptation and weight explosion |
| Limited data, large image dataset | **Data Augmentation** | Effectively increases $N$ without new collection |
| Long training, risk of overfitting late | **Early Stopping** | Cheapest regularizer; uses a validation curve |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff — Classic U-Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisX["Model Complexity -->"]
    axisY["Prediction Error"]
    errTotal["Total Error (Bell-shaped)"]
    bias["Bias^2 (Decreasing)"]
    var["Variance (Increasing)"]
    noise["Irreducible Noise sigma^2 (Flat)"]
    sweet["SWEET SPOT - Optimal Complexity"]
    
    axisX --- errTotal
    axisX --- bias
    axisX --- var
    axisX --- noise
    
    bias -.- sweet
    var -.- sweet
    errTotal -.- sweet
    
    style errTotal fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style bias fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style var fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style noise fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style sweet fill:#fef9ef,stroke:#d62828,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.2 Underfitting vs Good-Fit vs Overfitting — Data-Fit Visualization

```mermaid
    m2["Model: y = a + bx + cx^2 - QUADRATIC"]
    m3["Model: y = poly degree 15 - OVERFLEXIBLE"]
    
    r1["Result: UNDERFITTING - High bias, misses curve"]
    r2["Result: GOOD FIT - Captures true trend"]
    r3["Result: OVERFITTING - Passes through every point but zigzags"]

    data1 --> m2 --> r2
    data1 --> m3 --> r3

    style m3 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333

    style r2 fill:#d8f3dc
    style r3 fill:#fff3b0
```


## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently *forget* to include the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the L2 cost function — **always include it for clean derivatives.**
> 2. When asked about Lasso vs Ridge, do **not** say "L1 removes unimportant features" — the precise answer is: *"L1 drives the corresponding coefficient $\theta_i$ to exactly zero, performing implicit feature selection."*
> 3. In the bias-variance derivation, examiners check whether you have **explicitly shown** $E[\varepsilon] = 0$ — a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. Dropout is **not** a penalty term; describe it as a *stochastic structural regularizer* for full marks.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define overfitting and underfitting. How can you visually identify each from a learning curve?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Overfitting (1 mark):** A modeling condition where the model achieves very low error on training data but high error on unseen test data, indicating it has memorized noise and lost generalization ability.
- **Underfitting (1 mark):** A condition where the model is too simplistic to capture the underlying structure of the data, resulting in high error on *both* training and test sets.
- **Visual identification from learning curves (1 mark):** In an overfit model, training error continues to decrease while validation error rises after a certain point (gap widens). In an underfit model, both training and validation errors remain high and converge to similar large values with a small gap.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between L1 and L2 regularization. Which one produces sparse models and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **L1 (Lasso)** adds penalty $\lambda \sum \vert \theta_i \vert$; **L2 (Ridge)** adds $\lambda \sum \theta_i^2$. (1 mark)
- L1 can drive coefficients to **exactly zero**, whereas L2 only shrinks them toward zero without making them zero. (1 mark)
- **L1 produces sparse models (1 mark):** Because the L1 constraint region is a diamond whose corners lie on the coordinate axes. The intersection of the elliptical loss contours with the diamond typically occurs at a corner, where one or more $\theta_i$ are mathematically zero.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and derive the **Bias-Variance decomposition** of expected prediction error for the squared loss function. Clearly identify the irreducible noise term. (7 marks)

**(b)** With suitable equations, explain **L2 (Ridge) regularization**. Derive its gradient descent update rule and interpret the role of the weight-decay factor. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):* Let $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon$ is zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma^2$. The expected squared error at point $x$ is $E[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2]$.

*Step 2 — Expand (1 mark):*

$$E[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2] = E[\varepsilon^2] + E[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2] + 2E[\varepsilon]E[f(x) - \hat{f}(x)]$$

The cross-term vanishes because $E[\varepsilon] = 0$.

*Step 3 — Add and subtract the expected prediction (2 marks):*

Let $\bar{f}(x) = E[\hat{f}(x)]$. Then $f(x) - \hat{f}(x) = (f(x) - \bar{f}(x)) + (\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x))$.

Squaring and taking expectation, the cross-term again vanishes because $E[\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)] = 0$:

$$E[(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^2] = (f(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2 + E[(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2]$$

*Step 4 — Identify the three terms (2 marks):*

- $(f(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2 = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x))$
- $E[(\hat{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x))^2] = \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x))$
- $E[\varepsilon^2] = \sigma^2 = \text{Irreducible Error}$

*Final equation:*

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

The **irreducible noise** $\sigma^2$ is the Bayes error rate — it cannot be reduced by *any* model.

*Interpretation (1 mark embedded in step 4):* Bias decreases and variance increases with model complexity; regularization finds the optimal balance.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Define L2 cost (1 mark):* For parameter vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p$:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\theta_j^2$$

*Step 2 — Take partial derivative (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} &= \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})\, x_j^{(i)} + \frac{\lambda}{m}\theta_j
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Apply gradient descent (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_j &:= \theta_j - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} \\
\theta_j &:= \theta_j\left(1 - \eta\frac{\lambda}{m}\right) - \eta\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})\, x_j^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Weight-Decay Interpretation (2 marks):* The factor $(1 - \eta\lambda/m)$ is strictly less than 1. Therefore, even when the data-fit gradient is zero, every weight shrinks slightly on each step. This continuous shrinkage is the mechanism by which L2 keeps weights small and improves generalization. The decay rate is controlled by the learning rate $\eta$ and the regularization strength $\lambda$.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **L1 (Lasso) regularization** in detail. Derive the soft-thresholding operator and explain how it leads to sparsity. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Dropout** regularization technique for neural networks. Discuss why it acts as an implicit ensemble method. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Define Lasso cost (1 mark):*

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 + \lambda\sum_{j=1}^{p}\vert \theta_j \vert$$

*Step 2 — Sub-gradient of L1 (2 marks):* Since $\vert \theta_j \vert$ is non-differentiable at 0, we define the sub-gradient:

$$
\frac{\partial \vert \theta_j \vert}{\partial \theta_j} = \begin{cases} +1, & \theta_j > 0 \\ -1, & \theta_j < 0 \\ \in [-1, +1], & \theta_j = 0 \end{cases}
$$

*Step 3 — Update rule and soft-thresholding (2 marks):*

$$
\theta_j^{(t+1)} = \theta_j^{(t)} - \eta(g_j + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta_j^{(t)}))
$$

At convergence, the **soft-thresholding operator** is:

$$
\theta_j^* = \begin{cases} \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} - \eta\lambda, & \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} > \eta\lambda \\ 0, & \vert \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} \vert \leq \eta\lambda \\ \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} + \eta\lambda, & \theta_j^{\text{OLS}} < -\eta\lambda \end{cases}
$$

*Step 4 — Why sparsity arises (2 marks):* If the Ordinary Least Squares estimate $\theta_j^{\text{OLS}}$ is small in magnitude (below $\eta\lambda$), Lasso *exactly* sets $\theta_j = 0$. Unlike L2, L1 has no quadratic region near zero — the absolute-value penalty is strong enough to *kill* small weights. This implicit feature selection is mathematically guaranteed by the geometry of the diamond-shaped L1 constraint.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):* Dropout is a stochastic regularization technique where, during each training iteration, every neuron in selected layers is "dropped" (set to 0) with probability $p$ (typically $p = 0.5$ for hidden units). Only the surviving sub-network is updated. At test time, all neurons are kept and weights are scaled by $(1-p)$.

*Step 2 — Working (2 marks):* For a layer with activation vector $a$, we sample a binary mask $r \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1-p)$ and compute $\tilde{a} = a \odot r$. All downstream computations use $\tilde{a}$ instead of $a$. The mask is re-sampled at every forward pass.

*Step 3 — Implicit Ensemble Explanation (3 marks):* Because a different sub-network is sampled at *every* iteration, training a model for $T$ iterations with dropout is statistically equivalent to training $2^H$ thinned networks (where $H$ = number of dropout units) and sharing parameters among them. The final network approximates the **geometric mean** of predictions from all $2^H$ sub-networks — a far richer ensemble than could be trained independently. This averaging effect smooths the decision boundary and dramatically reduces overfitting, as demonstrated in the original Srivastava et al. (2014) paper.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Day**

- ☐ **Overfitting** = low training error, high test error. **Underfitting** = high error on both.
- ☐ The **bias-variance-noise decomposition** for squared loss is the most frequently asked 7-marker: $E[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- ☐ The general regularized objective: $J(\theta) = J_0(\theta) + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$.
- ☐ **L2 (Ridge):** $J = J_0 + \lambda \sum \theta_j^2$ → **shrinks** weights, no sparsity. Gradient update contains factor $(1 - \eta\lambda/m)$ = **weight decay**.
- ☐ **L1 (Lasso):** $J = J_0 + \lambda \sum \vert \theta_j \vert$ → **drives weights to exactly 0**, produces sparse models via **soft-thresholding**.
- ☐ **Elastic Net** = $\alpha \cdot L1 + (1-\alpha) \cdot L2$; used when features are correlated and many.
- ☐ **Dropout** randomly zeros neurons with probability $p$ during training; effectively trains an exponential ensemble of sub-networks.
- ☐ **Early Stopping** halts training when validation loss begins to rise; the cheapest regularizer available.
- ☐ **Data Augmentation** increases effective $N$ via transformations (rotations, flips, noise injection).
- ☐ **λ** = regularization hyperparameter; **higher λ = stronger regularization = more bias, less variance.**
- ☐ Always include the $\frac{1}{2m}$ normalization factor in cost functions to keep derivatives clean.
- ☐ Use **\vert** (not the pipe character `\vert`) when writing absolute value inside LaTeX in markdown tables.
- ☐ Remember to **define all variables** in derivations — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for undefined symbols.
- ☐ Tie every answer back to the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** — it is the unifying concept for this entire module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dropout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dropout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dropout** is a stochastic regularization technique used in deep neural networks where, during each training iteration, individual neurons (along with their incoming and outgoing connections) are "dropped out" — i.e., temporarily removed from the network — with a fixed probability $p$ (called the **dropout rate**). This prevents neurons from co-adapting too much, thereby reducing **overfitting** and improving **generalization**.

In formal mathematical terms (Srivastava et al., 2014 — the canonical Dropout paper), given a hidden layer's activation vector $\mathbf{h}$ during training:

$$\mathbf{h}_{\text{drop}} = \mathbf{r} \odot \mathbf{h}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{r} \in \{0, 1\}^d$ is a **Bernoulli random mask vector** with parameter $p_{\text{keep}} = 1 - p$
- $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**
- $p$ is the **dropout probability** (commonly $p = 0.5$ for hidden units, $p = 0.1$ to $0.2$ for input units)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Critical Phases of Dropout:**
> 1. **Training Phase:** Neurons are randomly dropped with probability $p$
> 2. **Inference Phase:** All neurons are used, but their outputs are scaled by $p_{\text{keep}}$ (or weights are scaled at test time) — this is called **inference scaling**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **cricket team** preparing for a World Cup final. Suppose the team's best batsman (let's call him "Star Player") is always available for every practice match. Over time, the other batsmen become *dependent* on him — they don't practice their own shot selection rigorously because they know Star Player will bail them out.

What if, during practice sessions, the coach **randomly tells Star Player to sit out** for some matches? The remaining players are now *forced* to sharpen their individual skills, build independent strategies, and not blindly rely on any single teammate.

Result: When the actual tournament comes and **all players are available** (the inference/test phase), the team performs *far better* than it would have if only Star Player was dominant.

**Dropout does exactly this for a neural network:**
- During **training**, randomly "sit out" (drop) some neurons so the remaining network cannot over-rely on any specific feature pathway.
- During **testing/inference**, activate the *full network* but compensate for the larger number of active units by scaling outputs (since each neuron was only active $(1-p)$ fraction of the time during training).

---

## 1.3 Why Dropout Works — The Three Core Intuition Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Preventing Co-adaptation of Features:**
> Co-adaptation means neurons become overly dependent on specific other neurons. Dropout breaks these fragile co-adaptations by forcing each neuron to be useful in *many* different sub-networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 2 — Implicit Ensemble Learning:**
> Every training iteration with dropout effectively trains a **different sub-network** (a "thinned" network). With $n$ neurons, there are $2^n$ possible sub-networks. At test time, using the full network approximates averaging predictions from an *exponentially large ensemble* of these sub-networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 3 — Noise Robustness:**
> By injecting structured stochastic noise into activations, dropout acts like a form of **data augmentation in feature space**, making the network robust to small perturbations and improving generalization error bounds.

---

## 1.4 Key Hyperparameters and Standard Metrics

| Hyperparameter | Typical Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Dropout rate ($p$)** | **0.5** (hidden layers) | Probability of dropping a neuron |
| **Input dropout ($p_{\text{in}}$)** | **0.1 to 0.2** | Lower dropout for input features |
| **Output dropout ($p_{\text{out}}$)** | **0.0 to 0.3** | Optional dropout on output layer |
| **Keep probability ($p_{\text{keep}}$)** | $1 - p$ | Probability of retaining a neuron |
| **Mask $\mathbf{r}$** | Bernoulli$(p_{\text{keep}})$ | Binary mask sampled per forward pass |

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Best Practice (KTU-recommended):** A dropout rate of **0.5** on hidden layers and **0.1** on the input layer is a strong default starting point for most feedforward and convolutional architectures (Srivastava et al., 2014).

---

## 1.5 Where Dropout Fits in the Deep Learning Pipeline

Dropout is applied **between layers** during the forward pass. Its position in the network matters:

1. **After activation functions** (most common — e.g., after ReLU): Prevents activation values from over-specialization.
2. **Before activation functions** (less common): Sometimes used for very deep networks.
3. **Inside recurrent cells** (Variational Dropout / Zoneout): Special handling for RNNs.

> [!WARNING]
> Dropout is **NOT** typically applied at the **output layer** for classification tasks, as this would damage the final prediction probabilities. It is exclusively a *hidden-state regularization mechanism*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Dropout Works Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dropout modifies both the **forward pass** and **backward pass** of training. Here is the structured operational logic.

### Step A — Sample the Bernoulli Mask

At the start of every training iteration (every minibatch), for every layer $\ell$ that uses dropout:

$$\mathbf{r}^{(\ell)} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_{\text{keep}})$$

This mask $\mathbf{r}^{(\ell)} \in \{0, 1\}^{n_\ell}$ has the same dimensionality as the layer's activation vector $\mathbf{h}^{(\ell)}$.

### Step B — Apply Mask to Activations (Forward Pass)

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{r}^{(\ell)} \odot \mathbf{h}^{(\ell)}$$

Any neuron whose corresponding mask entry is **0** is effectively silenced — its activation is zeroed out, and it contributes nothing to subsequent layers for this iteration.

### Step C — Inverted Dropout Scaling (Forward Pass)

To maintain the **expected sum of activations** invariant across training and inference:

$$\mathbf{h}_{\text{out}}^{(\ell)} = \frac{1}{p_{\text{keep}}} \tilde{\mathbf{h}}^{(\ell)} = \frac{1}{p_{\text{keep}}} \left( \mathbf{r}^{(\ell)} \odot \mathbf{h}^{(\ell)} \right)$$

This is called **inverted dropout** because scaling is done *during training* (not at test time), making inference computationally cheaper.

### Step D — Backward Pass Modification

During backpropagation, gradients only flow through neurons that were **not dropped** (because dropped neurons contribute zero to the loss). Mathematically:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}^{(\ell)}} = \frac{1}{p_{\text{keep}}} \left( \mathbf{r}^{(\ell)} \odot \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{\text{out}}^{(\ell)}} \right)$$

The mask $\mathbf{r}^{(\ell)}$ is **shared between forward and backward passes** — the same neurons that were dropped during forward propagation must have zero gradient during backward propagation.

### Step E — Inference (Testing) Phase

At inference, **no dropout is applied**. The full network is used:

$$\mathbf{h}_{\text{test}}^{(\ell)} = f\left(\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{h}_{\text{test}}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}\right)$$

Because of the inverted scaling during training, no additional compensation is needed at test time. This is the **engineering elegance** of inverted dropout.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every high-yield formula** you need to solve KTU-level problems on Dropout. Memorize these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mask sampling | $\mathbf{r} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_{\text{keep}})$ | $\mathbf{r} \in \{0,1\}^n$ |
| 2 | Dropout forward | $\tilde{\mathbf{h}} = \mathbf{r} \odot \mathbf{h}$ | Hadamard product |
| 3 | Inverted dropout scaling | $\mathbf{h}_{\text{out}} = \frac{\tilde{\mathbf{h}}}{p_{\text{keep}}}$ | Scale during training |
| 4 | Standard dropout (no inver.) | $\mathbf{h}_{\text{test}} = p_{\text{keep}} \cdot \mathbf{h}_{\text{out}}$ | Scale at test time |
| 5 | Expected activation | $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = p_{\text{keep}} \cdot h_i$ | Per-unit expectation |
| 6 | Variance of activation | $\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}}) h_i^2$ | Bernoulli variance |
| 7 | Dropout probability | $p = 1 - p_{\text{keep}}$ | Standard relation |
| 8 | Number of sub-networks | $2^n$ | For $n$ stochastic neurons |
| 9 | MC-Dropout prediction | $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} f(\mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}, \mathbf{r}_t)$ | Bayesian approx. |
| 10 | Inference-time activation (standard) | $\mathbf{h}_{\text{test}} = p_{\text{keep}} \cdot \mathbf{W}\mathbf{h}$ | No scaling during training |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION NOTE:** When writing $\vert \mathbf{r} \vert$ (number of active units), use $\vert \mathbf{r} \vert$ (single vertical) inside LaTeX math blocks. **Inside markdown tables, do NOT use single vertical pipes** — they break the table syntax. Use $\lvert \mathbf{r} \rvert$ or "cardinality of $\mathbf{r}$" instead.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

| Application Domain | Role of Dropout |
|---|---|
| **Computer Vision (CNNs)** | Reduces overfitting in ResNet, VGG, MobileNet architectures |
| **NLP & Transformers** | Attention dropout + residual dropout in BERT, GPT |
| **Speech Recognition** | Regularizes RNN/LSTM acoustic models |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Prevents policy networks from memorizing trajectories |
| **Bayesian Deep Learning** | MC-Dropout enables approximate Bayesian inference (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) |
| **Medical Imaging** | Critical when training data is scarce (e.g., tumor segmentation) |
| **Generative Models** | Used in GAN discriminators and VAE encoders |

---

## 2.4 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Mechanics

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Sample mask each iteration | Ensures **stochasticity** — every minibatch sees a different sub-network |
| Mask activation (not weights) | Forces neurons to be **independently useful**, not depend on specific others |
| Inverted scaling ($/p_{\text{keep}}$) | Keeps the **expected magnitude** of activations consistent across train/test |
| Mask shared in backprop | Guarantees **gradient flow only through active paths** — clean credit assignment |
| Full network at inference | The full model is a **weighted ensemble average** of all sub-networks |

---

## 2.5 Variants of Dropout (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following variants are **frequently asked in KTU exams**. Understand each one deeply.

| Variant | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Dropout** | Per-neuron Bernoulli mask | FCNN, CNN |
| **Inverted Dropout** | Scale activations by $1/p_{\text{keep}}$ at training | Modern frameworks |
| **Spatial Dropout** | Drops entire feature maps (channels) | CNNs — preserves spatial coherence |
| **DropConnect** | Drops individual **weights** (not activations) | More fine-grained regularization |
| **Variational Dropout** | Same mask across time-steps | RNNs |
| **Zoneout** | Preserves hidden state instead of dropping | RNN hidden states |
| **MC-Dropout** | Keep dropout ON at test time, average over $T$ samples | Bayesian uncertainty estimation |
| **DropBlock** | Drops contiguous regions | CNNs — better than spatial dropout |
| **Gaussian Dropout** | Multiplicative Gaussian noise instead of Bernoulli | Continuous relaxation |
| **Alpha Dropout** | Designed for SELU activations | Self-normalizing networks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Value of Dropped Activation

**Goal:** Show that $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = p_{\text{keep}} \cdot h_i$ for a single neuron activation.

**Given:**
- A pre-dropout activation $h_i$ (treated as a constant during a single forward pass)
- A Bernoulli mask $r_i \in \{0, 1\}$ with $\Pr(r_i = 1) = p_{\text{keep}}$

**Setup:**

$$\tilde{h}_i = r_i \cdot h_i$$

**Derivation:**

Since $h_i$ is constant for this forward pass, expectation is taken over the randomness of $r_i$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = \mathbb{E}[r_i \cdot h_i] = h_i \cdot \mathbb{E}[r_i]$$

By definition of Bernoulli random variable:

$$\mathbb{E}[r_i] = 1 \cdot p_{\text{keep}} + 0 \cdot (1 - p_{\text{keep}}) = p_{\text{keep}}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = h_i \cdot p_{\text{keep}}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = p_{\text{keep}} \cdot h_i}$$

**Interpretation:** Without inverted scaling, the *expected* activation is scaled down by a factor of $p_{\text{keep}}$. This is why we need to either (a) scale up during training (inverted dropout) or (b) scale down at test time (standard dropout).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverted Dropout Compensation

**Goal:** Prove that scaling $\tilde{h}_i$ by $1/p_{\text{keep}}$ during training preserves the expected activation.

**Setup:** Define the inverted-dropout output as:

$$h_i^{\text{inv}} = \frac{1}{p_{\text{keep}}} \cdot \tilde{h}_i = \frac{r_i \cdot h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}}$$

**Derivation:**

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[h_i^{\text{inv}}\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{r_i \cdot h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}}\right] = \frac{h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}} \cdot \mathbb{E}[r_i] = \frac{h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}} \cdot p_{\text{keep}}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}\!\left[h_i^{\text{inv}}\right] = h_i}$$

**Interpretation:** The expected activation under inverted dropout equals the original activation. Therefore, **at test time, no further scaling is needed** — the inference forward pass uses the full network as-is. This is the elegant engineering reason why modern frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch) implement inverted dropout by default.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Variance of Dropped Activation

**Goal:** Compute $\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i]$ to understand the noise injection property.

**Setup:** $\tilde{h}_i = r_i \cdot h_i$, with $h_i$ constant.

**Derivation:**

For any random variable $X = c \cdot Y$ where $c$ is a constant:

$$\text{Var}[cY] = c^2 \cdot \text{Var}[Y]$$

For Bernoulli($p_{\text{keep}}$), the variance is:

$$\text{Var}[r_i] = p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}})$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = h_i^2 \cdot \text{Var}[r_i] = h_i^2 \cdot p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}})$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}}) \cdot h_i^2}$$

**Maximum variance occurs when** $p_{\text{keep}} = 0.5$ (i.e., dropout rate $p = 0.5$), giving:

$$\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i]_{\max} = 0.25 \cdot h_i^2$$

**Interpretation:** Dropout adds the **maximum stochastic noise** at $p = 0.5$. This is one of the empirical justifications for the common choice of dropout rate $p = 0.5$ in hidden layers — it injects the largest training-time regularization signal.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Expected Number of Active Units in a Layer

**Goal:** Derive the expected number of active neurons in a layer of size $n$ with dropout rate $p$.

**Setup:** Let $N_{\text{active}}$ be the number of active neurons in a layer of size $n$.

$$N_{\text{active}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i$$

**Derivation:**

By linearity of expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{active}}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[r_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{\text{keep}} = n \cdot p_{\text{keep}}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{active}}] = n \cdot (1 - p)}$$

**Numerical Example:** A fully-connected layer with $n = 1024$ neurons and dropout rate $p = 0.5$ has, on average, **512 active neurons** per training iteration.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Effective L2 Regularization Interpretation

**Goal:** Show that Dropout is mathematically equivalent to an adaptive L2 regularizer (a deep insight from Srivastava et al., 2014).

**Setup:** Consider a single linear unit $y = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}$ with squared-error loss on a dropout-masked network.

When we drop a subset $S$ of input features, the effective squared norm of weights being regularized becomes:

$$\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2$$

Dropout can be shown to impose an L2 penalty of the form:

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{with} \quad \lambda_{\text{eff}} \propto \frac{p}{2N}$$

where $N$ is the number of training samples and $p$ is the dropout rate.

**Derivation sketch:**

Consider the expected squared output under dropout over a random subset $S$ of active units of size $k = p_{\text{keep}} n$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{S}\!\left[\left(\sum_{i \in S} w_i x_i\right)^2\right] = p_{\text{keep}} \sum_{i} w_i^2 \mathbb{E}[x_i^2] + p_{\text{keep}}^2 \sum_{i \neq j} w_i w_j \mathbb{E}[x_i x_j]$$

Assuming centered inputs ($\mathbb{E}[x_i] = 0$) and unit variance, this reduces to:

$$\mathbb{E}_{S}\!\left[\Vert \mathbf{w}_S^\top \mathbf{x}_S \Vert^2\right] = p_{\text{keep}} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2$$

This shows that dropout **shrinks weight magnitudes** by a factor of $\sqrt{p_{\text{keep}}}$, equivalent to L2 regularization with rate-dependent strength.

**Interpretation:** Dropout is **not identical** to standard L2 regularization — the penalty is applied **adaptively**, scaled by the activation magnitude. Large activations get a stronger penalty, while small activations get a weaker penalty. This is *more flexible* than fixed L2 regularization.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Standard Dropout | Inverted Dropout | DropConnect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scaling location | Test time | Train time | Train time |
| Test-time cost | Lower (scaled weights) | Standard | Standard |
| Framework default | Old TF | **Modern PyTorch/TF** | Custom impl. |
| Granularity | Neuron | Neuron | Weight |
| Co-adaptation reduction | Yes | Yes | Stronger |
| Implementation complexity | Low | **Low** | High |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A hidden layer has 100 neurons with activations $\mathbf{h}$. The dropout rate is $p = 0.4$, so $p_{\text{keep}} = 0.6$. A random mask turns off neurons at indices 5, 17, 23, 41, 67, 89, 92 (i.e., 7 neurons are dropped). Using **inverted dropout**, compute the final activation value at index 10 if the pre-dropout activation was $h_{10} = 2.5$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Mask value at index 10:

$$r_{10} = 1 \quad (\text{since index 10 is not in the dropped set})$$

Step 2 — Dropped activation:

$$\tilde{h}_{10} = r_{10} \cdot h_{10} = 1 \cdot 2.5 = 2.5$$

Step 3 — Inverted dropout scaling:

$$h_{10}^{\text{inv}} = \frac{\tilde{h}_{10}}{p_{\text{keep}}} = \frac{2.5}{0.6} = 4.1\overline{6}$$

Step 4 — Expected value check (over many iterations):

$$\mathbb{E}[h_{10}^{\text{inv}}] = h_{10} = 2.5 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{h_{10}^{\text{inv}} \approx 4.17}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define the Dropout technique in neural networks. Explain why dropout is applied only during training and not during inference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dropout is a **regularization technique** introduced by Srivastava et al. (2014) to reduce overfitting in deep neural networks. During each training iteration, individual neurons are randomly dropped (set to zero) along with their connections with a fixed probability $p$, called the **dropout rate**.

Mathematically, given activations $\mathbf{h}$ of a layer:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}} = \mathbf{r} \odot \mathbf{h}, \quad \mathbf{r} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_{\text{keep}})$$

Dropout is **only applied during training** because:
1. At training, we want to prevent **co-adaptation** and force neurons to learn robust, independent features.
2. At **inference**, we want to use the **full network's representational capacity** to make accurate predictions. Stochasticity at test time would introduce unnecessary noise.
3. With **inverted dropout** (scaling by $1/p_{\text{keep}}$ during training), the test-time forward pass needs no modification — the expected activations match those of the original un-dropped network.

**(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the inference explanation)**

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the difference between Standard Dropout and Inverted Dropout? Which one is preferred in modern deep learning frameworks and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Standard Dropout | Inverted Dropout |
|---|---|---|
| Scaling location | Inference (test) time | Training time |
| Test-time computation | Requires weight scaling | No scaling needed |
| Practical advantage | None | **Faster inference** |
| Framework adoption | Legacy / academic | **PyTorch, TensorFlow (default)** |

**Inverted Dropout** scales activations by $\frac{1}{p_{\text{keep}}}$ **during training**:

$$\mathbf{h}_{\text{out}} = \frac{\mathbf{r} \odot \mathbf{h}}{p_{\text{keep}}}$$

This makes the **expected activation** equal to the original activation: $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{h}_{\text{out}}] = \mathbf{h}$.

It is preferred in modern frameworks because:
1. **No test-time modification** is needed — inference code is simpler and faster.
2. **No risk** of forgetting to apply the scaling at deployment, which would cause silent performance degradation.
3. It makes the **model deployment-ready** without any post-training weight adjustments.

**(Award: 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for inverted dropout formula, 1 mark for justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern, Part B questions on this topic typically carry **14 marks** with internal choice. The structure below mirrors actual KTU question papers.

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Dropout regularization technique in detail. Discuss its role in:
   - (i) Preventing overfitting
   - (ii) Breaking co-adaptation of features
   - (iii) Implicit ensemble learning

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a hidden layer with $n = 200$ neurons and dropout rate $p = 0.5$:
   - (i) Calculate the expected number of active neurons per training iteration.
   - (ii) Compute the variance of a single dropped activation if its pre-dropout value is $h_i = 3.0$.
   - (iii) If you switch to inverted dropout, derive the new expected activation after scaling.

---

### Model Answer for Question B-A

#### Part (a) — Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]

**Dropout Definition and Mechanism:**

Dropout is a **stochastic regularization technique** that improves the generalization of deep neural networks by randomly deactivating neurons during training. For a layer with activations $\mathbf{h} \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{h}} = \mathbf{r} \odot \mathbf{h}, \quad \mathbf{r} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(1 - p)$$

**(i) Preventing Overfitting [2 marks]:**

Deep networks with millions of parameters are highly prone to **overfitting** — memorizing training data while failing to generalize. Dropout combats this by:
- Injecting stochastic noise into activations, which forces the network to learn **redundant, distributed representations**.
- Acting as an **implicit data augmentation** in the feature space — every iteration sees a slightly different network.
- Reducing the **effective capacity** of the network during training (fewer active parameters per iteration), preventing memorization.

**(ii) Breaking Co-adaptation [2 marks]:**

**Co-adaptation** occurs when neurons become overly reliant on the specific activations of other neurons. For example, a detector neuron for "cat ears" might learn to depend on a specific detector for "fur texture." If "fur texture" fails, "cat ears" fails too.

Dropout **breaks this dependency** by randomly removing companion neurons. Each neuron must therefore learn to be useful in **many different contexts** and cannot rely on a fixed set of co-active partners. This produces **robust, decorrelated feature detectors**.

**(iii) Implicit Ensemble Learning [3 marks]:**

Consider a network with $n$ stochastic neurons. Each training iteration samples one of $2^n$ possible **sub-networks** (after Bernoulli masking). The full network at test time, with all neurons active, effectively **averages** the predictions of these exponentially many sub-networks.

This is mathematically analogous to **model ensembling** — combining many weak learners to form a strong learner. The key insight (from Srivastava et al., 2014) is that:

- **Ensemble variance** $\downarrow$ as number of models $\uparrow$
- **Ensemble bias** stays approximately the same as individual model bias
- Therefore, **overall generalization error** $\downarrow$

Dropout achieves this ensemble effect at **negligible computational overhead** compared to explicitly training $2^n$ separate networks.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Number of neurons: $n = 200$
- Dropout rate: $p = 0.5$, hence $p_{\text{keep}} = 1 - p = 0.5$
- Pre-dropout activation: $h_i = 3.0$

**(i) Expected Number of Active Neurons [2 marks]:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.4:

$$\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{active}}] = n \cdot p_{\text{keep}} = 200 \cdot 0.5$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{active}}] = 100 \text{ neurons}}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, **100 out of 200 neurons** are active per training iteration.

---

**(ii) Variance of a Single Dropped Activation [3 marks]:**

Using the variance formula from Section 3.3:

$$\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}}) \cdot h_i^2$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = 0.5 \cdot (1 - 0.5) \cdot (3.0)^2 = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9 = 2.25$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = 2.25}$$

**Interpretation:** The dropped activation has a variance of 2.25 around its expected value of $p_{\text{keep}} \cdot h_i = 1.5$. This stochastic noise is what forces the network to learn robust features.

---

**(iii) Inverted Dropout Expected Activation [2 marks]:**

Under inverted dropout, the scaled activation is:

$$h_i^{\text{inv}} = \frac{r_i \cdot h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}}$$

Taking expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[h_i^{\text{inv}}] = \frac{h_i \cdot \mathbb{E}[r_i]}{p_{\text{keep}}} = \frac{h_i \cdot p_{\text{keep}}}{p_{\text{keep}}} = h_i$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[h_i^{\text{inv}}] = h_i = 3.0}$$

**Interpretation:** Inverted dropout preserves the expected activation exactly, which is why no scaling is needed at inference time. This is the key engineering advantage over standard dropout.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]**
   - (i) Derive the expected value and variance of a dropped activation $\tilde{h}_i = r_i \cdot h_i$, where $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_{\text{keep}})$.
   - (ii) Show mathematically why inverted dropout preserves the expected activation at test time without requiring scaling.

**(b) [7 Marks]**
   - (i) Explain the concept of **MC-Dropout** and how it enables approximate Bayesian inference in deep networks.
   - (ii) Compare and contrast Dropout with **DropConnect** and **Spatial Dropout** with suitable use-case diagrams in tabular form.

---

### Model Answer for Question B-B

#### Part (a) — Derivations [7 Marks]

**(i) Expected Value and Variance [4 marks]:**

**Setup:** A single activation $h_i$ is treated as a constant during a forward pass. The mask $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_{\text{keep}})$ has the following moments:

$$\mathbb{E}[r_i] = p_{\text{keep}}$$

$$\mathbb{E}[r_i^2] = p_{\text{keep}} \quad (\text{since } r_i^2 = r_i \text{ for binary } r_i)$$

$$\text{Var}[r_i] = \mathbb{E}[r_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[r_i])^2 = p_{\text{keep}} - p_{\text{keep}}^2 = p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}})$$

**Expected value of dropped activation:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = \mathbb{E}[r_i h_i] = h_i \mathbb{E}[r_i] = p_{\text{keep}} h_i$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[\tilde{h}_i] = p_{\text{keep}} h_i}$$

**Variance of dropped activation:**

$$\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = \text{Var}[r_i h_i] = h_i^2 \text{Var}[r_i] = h_i^2 p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}})$$

$$\boxed{\text{Var}[\tilde{h}_i] = h_i^2 p_{\text{keep}}(1 - p_{\text{keep}})}$$

---

**(ii) Inverted Dropout Justification [3 marks]:**

Under inverted dropout, the scaled activation is:

$$h_i^{\text{inv}} = \frac{r_i h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}}$$

Computing the expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}[h_i^{\text{inv}}] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{r_i h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}}\right] = \frac{h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}} \mathbb{E}[r_i] = \frac{h_i}{p_{\text{keep}}} \cdot p_{\text{keep}}$$

$$\mathbb{E}[h_i^{\text{inv}}] = h_i$$

This shows that the **expected value of the inverted-dropped activation equals the original activation**. Therefore, at inference time, using the full network (no dropout) gives predictions consistent with the expected behavior during training — **no test-time scaling is needed**.

In contrast, with standard dropout (no inverted scaling), the test-time forward pass must multiply all weights by $p_{\text{keep}}$ to compensate, otherwise activations would be inflated by a factor of $1/p_{\text{keep}}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Conceptual Explanations [7 Marks]

**(i) MC-Dropout and Bayesian Approximation [4 marks]:**

**MC-Dropout** (Monte Carlo Dropout), introduced by Gal & Ghahramani (2016), is a technique that **keeps dropout enabled at inference time** and runs multiple stochastic forward passes to estimate predictive uncertainty.

The MC-Dropout prediction for an input $\mathbf{x}$ after $T$ stochastic forward passes:

$$\hat{y} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} f(\mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}, \mathbf{r}_t)$$

where $\mathbf{r}_t$ is the dropout mask sampled at pass $t$.

**Predictive variance (uncertainty estimate):**

$$\text{Var}[\hat{y}] \approx \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left(f(\mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}, \mathbf{r}_t) - \hat{y}\right)^2$$

**Why it approximates Bayesian inference:**

Gal & Ghahramani showed that a neural network with dropout applied before every weight layer is mathematically equivalent to a **variational approximation** to a Gaussian Process. The dropout mask $\mathbf{r}$ plays the role of a variational sampling parameter. Therefore:

- **MC-Dropout mean** $\hat{y} \approx$ Bayesian predictive mean
- **MC-Dropout variance** $\approx$ Epistemic uncertainty estimate

This enables deep networks to express **"I don't know"** predictions, critical for safety-critical applications like medical diagnosis and autonomous driving.

---

**(ii) Comparison: Dropout vs DropConnect vs Spatial Dropout [3 marks]:**

| Aspect | Standard Dropout | DropConnect | Spatial Dropout |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What is dropped** | Neuron activations | Individual weights | Entire feature maps (channels) |
| **Mask granularity** | Per neuron | Per weight | Per channel |
| **Probability formulation** | $r_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_{\text{keep}})$ | $M_{ij} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_{\text{keep}})$ | Per-channel mask |
| **Computational cost** | Low | Higher | Low |
| **Best suited for** | FCNN, MLPs | Large dense layers | CNNs |
