- This is the **central equation** of the LSTM.
- The Hadamard (element-wise) product $\odot$ allows each memory component to be independently regulated.
- Notice the **additive structure**: $C_{t-1}$ is added to (not multiplied by) the new candidate. This additive pathway is precisely what mitigates vanishing gradients.
- Gradient of $C_t$ w.r.t. $C_{t-1}$ is diagonal $\text{diag}(f_t)$, so if $f_t$ is near 1, the gradient passes almost unchanged.

### Step 4: Output Gate ($o_t$) and Hidden State ($h_t$) — "What to Expose"

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

- The output gate filters the squashed cell state to produce the hidden state exposed externally.
- $\tanh(C_t)$ ensures $h_t$ remains bounded in $(-1, 1)$.
- $h_t$ is what is passed to the next time step **and** to upper layers of a deep network.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| Equation | Operation | Key Insight for Exam |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Forget gate | Output in $(0,1)$ |
| $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Input gate | Decides what to write |
| $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | Candidate memory | Output in $(-1,1)$ |
| $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | **Core memory update** | Additive → gradient preservation |
| $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Output gate | Decides what to expose |
| $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Hidden state output | Bounded working memory |
| $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(f_t)$ | Gradient flow | If $f_t \to 1$, no vanishing |
| # Parameters per cell | $4H(H+d) + 4H$ | Four weight matrices, four biases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Board Exams:** A common examiner trick is to ask "How many parameters does a single LSTM cell have?" — the answer is $\mathbf{4H(H+d) + 4H}$ where $H$ is the hidden size and $d$ is the input size. The factor of 4 corresponds to the four learned transformations (forget, input, candidate, output).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

LSTMs underpin numerous production-grade systems:

- **Natural Language Processing:** Machine translation (Google Neural Machine Translation, 2016), text generation, named-entity recognition.
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Energy load prediction, financial market modeling, weather forecasting, predictive maintenance in Industry 4.0.
- **Speech & Audio:** Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems, music composition (e.g., Google's Magenta project).
- **Healthcare:** ICU mortality prediction using Electronic Health Records (MIMIC-III dataset).
- **Video & Image Sequences:** Activity recognition, video captioning, gesture recognition.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Network intrusion detection, fraud detection in sequential transaction logs.

The architectural advantage of **selective memory retention** is what allows LSTMs to be deployed in safety-critical domains where preserving long-range context is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Additive Cell State Update Solves Vanishing Gradients

We will rigorously derive the gradient flow through the cell state over $T$ time steps. This derivation is a **high-frequency KTU exam question**.

**Setup:** Consider the loss $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t$ computed at each time step. We want $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial C_k}$ for any past cell state $C_k$.

### Step 1: Direct Partial Derivative at the Next Time Step

At time $t = k+1$, the cell state update is:

$$
C_{k+1} = f_{k+1} \odot C_k + i_{k+1} \odot \tilde{C}_{k+1}
$$

Differentiating with respect to $C_k$ element-wise (using the Hadamard product rule):

$$
\frac{\partial C_{k+1}}{\partial C_k} = \text{diag}(f_{k+1}) + \frac{\partial (f_{k+1} \odot C_k)}{\partial C_k} + \frac{\partial (i_{k+1} \odot \tilde{C}_{k+1})}{\partial C_k}
$$

The first term dominates and the last two terms are typically small in magnitude. So we approximate:

$$
\frac{\partial C_{k+1}}{\partial C_k} \approx \text{diag}(f_{k+1})
$$

### Step 2: Chain Rule Over $T - k$ Time Steps

The total gradient flow from $C_T$ back to $C_k$ is the product of diagonal matrices:

$$
\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial C_k} = \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} \approx \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \text{diag}(f_t)
$$

### Step 3: Element-wise Form

For any scalar component indexed by $j$:

$$
\left[ \frac{\partial C_T}{\partial C_k} \right]_{j,j} \approx \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} f_t^{(j)}
$$

**Interpretation:** If every forget gate component $f_t^{(j)}$ is close to 1 (e.g., $0.95$), then the gradient is $0.95^{T-k}$. For $T - k = 100$ time steps:

$$
0.95^{100} \approx 0.0059
$$

Compare this to a vanilla RNN where the equivalent factor is $\prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \tanh'(W_{hh} h_{t-1}) \cdot W_{hh}$, which in practice gives values like $0.5^{100} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{-31}$ — effectively zero (vanishing). The LSTM preserves a **1,000× larger** gradient magnitude here.

### Step 4: The Bias Initialization Trick

To enforce $f_t \approx 1$ by default, the bias $b_f$ is initialized to **$1$** (or higher). The sigmoid then evaluates to:

$$
\sigma(1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1}} \approx 0.731
$$

This means even without training, the default behavior is to "remember." During training, the network learns to selectively suppress $f_t$ for genuinely irrelevant information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the approximation is justified:** The exact derivative includes cross-terms from $\frac{\partial f_{t+1}}{\partial C_t}$ and $\frac{\partial i_{t+1}}{\partial C_t}$. In practice, these terms are of order $\mathcal{O}(10^{-3})$ compared to $\text{diag}(f_{t+1})$ and can be neglected for pedagogical clarity. This is the **standard textbook argument** (Goodfellow et al., *Deep Learning*, MIT Press, 2016, §10.10).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Forward Pass with Numerical Worked Example

Let us walk through a **concrete numerical example** with $H = 2$ and $d = 1$ to consolidate the equations. Suppose at time $t$:

- $x_t = [0.5]$
- $h_{t-1} = [0.1, \ -0.2]^T$
- $C_{t-1} = [0.4, \ 0.6]^T$
- $b_f = [1, 1]^T$, $b_i = [0, 0]^T$, $b_C = [0, 0]^T$, $b_o = [0, 0]^T$

(Weights are chosen simple for illustration; in practice these are learned.)

### Step 1: Forget Gate

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$

With $W_f = I$ (identity for simplicity) on the hidden part and zero on the input part:

$$
W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] = [0.1, \ -0.2]^T
$$

$$
f_t = \sigma([0.1 + 1, \ -0.2 + 1]^T) = \sigma([1.1, \ 0.8]^T) = [0.750, \ 0.690]^T
$$

**Logic:** Sigmoid squashes 1.1 to 0.750 and 0.8 to 0.690; values are close to 1, indicating "remember most of the past."

### Step 2: Input Gate and Candidate

$$
i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) = \sigma([0.1, \ -0.2]^T) = [0.525, \ 0.450]^T
$$

$$
\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C) = \tanh([0.1, \ -0.2]^T) = [0.0997, \ -0.1974]^T
$$

### Step 3: Cell State Update

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

Computing element-wise:

$$
C_t^{(1)} = 0.750 \times 0.4 + 0.525 \times 0.0997 = 0.300 + 0.0523 = 0.3523
$$

$$
C_t^{(2)} = 0.690 \times 0.6 + 0.450 \times (-0.1974) = 0.414 - 0.0888 = 0.3252
$$

Thus:

$$
C_t = [0.3523, \ 0.3252]^T
$$

**Logic:** The first component decreased slightly (0.4 → 0.352) because both the forget gate and input gate moderated the new value. The second component decreased more (0.6 → 0.325) due to the negative candidate and partial forgetting.

### Step 4: Output Gate and Hidden State

$$
o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o) = [0.525, \ 0.450]^T
$$

$$
h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) = [0.525, \ 0.450]^T \odot \tanh([0.3523, \ 0.3252]^T)
$$

$$
\tanh(0.3523) \approx 0.3386, \quad \tanh(0.3252) \approx 0.3140
$$

$$
h_t = [0.525 \times 0.3386, \ 0.450 \times 0.3140]^T = [0.1778, \ 0.1413]^T
$$

This $h_t$ is the output exposed to the next time step and to upper network layers.

## 3.3 Forward Pass Equations — Final Canonical Form

For quick reference, the **complete LSTM forward pass** at time $t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_f) \\
i_t &= \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_i) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_C) \\
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
o_t &= \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_o) \\
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $[h_{t-1}; x_t]$ denotes the **column-wise concatenation** of vectors $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ and $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ into a single vector of dimension $H + d$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The LSTM Cell Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **internal data flow** of a single LSTM cell, including all four learnable transformations and the regulated memory highway.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["INPUT Xt"] --> concat1["CONCATENATE Ht-1 AND Xt"]
    B["PREVIOUS HIDDEN Ht-1"] --> concat1
    concat1 --> Wf["WEIGHT MATRIX Wf"]
    concat1 --> Wi["WEIGHT MATRIX Wi"]
    concat1 --> WC["WEIGHT MATRIX WC"]
    concat1 --> Wo["WEIGHT MATRIX Wo"]
    Wf --> sigF["SIGMOID ACTIVATION"]
    Wi --> sigI["SIGMOID ACTIVATION"]
    WC --> tanhC["TANH ACTIVATION"]
    Wo --> sigO["SIGMOID ACTIVATION"]
    sigF --> multF["HADAMARD PRODUCT WITH Ct-1"]
    sigI --> multI["HADAMARD PRODUCT WITH CANDIDATE"]
    tanhC --> multI
    D["PREVIOUS CELL STATE Ct-1"] --> multF
    multF --> sumNode["ELEMENT-WISE ADDITION"]
    multI --> sumNode
    sumNode --> newC["NEW CELL STATE Ct"]
    newC --> tanhOut["TANH OF Ct"]
    sigO --> multO["HADAMARD PRODUCT WITH TANH Ct"]
    tanhOut --> multO
    multO --> newH["NEW HIDDEN STATE Ht"]
    newC --> outA["EXPOSED TO NEXT TIMESTEP"]
    newH --> outB["EXPOSED TO UPPER LAYER"]
    newH --> nextStep["FEED Ht AS Ht-1 TO NEXT TIMESTEP"]
    newC --> nextCell["FEED Ct AS Ct-1 TO NEXT TIMESTEP"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Unrolled Through Time)

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **temporal unrolling** of an LSTM network over $T = 3$ time steps, depicting how $h_t$ and $C_t$ propagate horizontally. This is the canonical figure used in KTU lecture slides and standard deep learning textbooks.

```mermaid
graph LR
    X1["INPUT X1"] --> cellA["LSTM CELL T=1"]
    X2["INPUT X2"] --> cellB["LSTM CELL T=2"]
    X3["INPUT X3"] --> cellC["LSTM CELL T=3"]
    cellA -->|"h1, C1"| cellB
    cellB -->|"h2, C2"| cellC
    cellA --> Y1["OUTPUT Y1"]
    cellB --> Y2["OUTPUT Y2"]
    cellC --> Y3["OUTPUT Y3"]
    cellA -.->|"FORGET GATE f1"| cellA
    cellA -.->|"INPUT GATE i1"| cellA
    cellA -.->|"OUTPUT GATE o1"| cellA
    cellB -.->|"FORGET GATE f2"| cellB
    cellB -.->|"INPUT GATE i2"| cellB
    cellB -.->|"OUTPUT GATE o2"| cellB
    cellC -.->|"FORGET GATE f3"| cellC
    cellC -.->|"INPUT GATE i3"| cellC
    cellC -.->|"OUTPUT GATE o3"| cellC
```

## 4.3 Gated Memory Highway — Conceptual Block Diagram

The following flowchart emphasizes the **additive memory pathway** that distinguishes LSTMs from vanilla RNNs. Each cell can be seen as a *regulated conveyor belt* where the forget gate acts as a *flow control valve* and the input gate as a *loading mechanism*.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PrevCell["Ct-1 LONG TERM MEMORY"] --> FT["FORGET GATE FT = SIGMOID LAYER"]
    FT -->|"ELEMENT WISE MULTIPLY"| Survived["RETAINED MEMORY COMPONENTS"]
    InpGate["INPUT GATE IT = SIGMOID LAYER"] --> Load["SCALING FACTOR"]
    Candidate["CANDIDATE CT-TILDE = TANH LAYER"] --> NewInfo["NEW INFORMATION VECTOR"]
    Load -->|"ELEMENT WISE MULTIPLY"| NewInfo
    Survived --> SumNode["ELEMENT WISE ADDITION"]
    NewInfo --> SumNode
    SumNode --> NewCell["CT NEW LONG TERM MEMORY"]
    NewCell --> OutTanh["TANH COMPRESSION"]
    OutGate["OUTPUT GATE OT = SIGMOID LAYER"] --> Filter["OUTPUT FILTER"]
    OutTanh --> Filter
    Filter --> NewHidden["HT WORKING MEMORY EXPOSED"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Memorization Tip:** When drawing the LSTM cell in an exam, always:
> 1. Draw the **horizontal $C_t$ line on top** (the memory highway).
> 2. Draw the **three gates as vertical sigmoids** (forget on the left, input in the middle-left, output in the middle-right).
> 3. Use **$\times$ symbols** for Hadamard products and **$+$ symbols** for additions.
> 4. Label the **candidate $\tilde{C}_t$** as a tanh box adjacent to the input gate.
> 5. Indicate the **direction of gradient flow** with dashed arrows during backpropagation discussions.
>
> Examiners award **partial marks (typically 2 of 7)** for a correctly drawn and labeled diagram even if equations contain minor errors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define LSTM and State Its Key Advantage Over Vanilla RNN. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network** is a type of recurrent neural network designed to capture long-term dependencies in sequential data. Unlike a vanilla RNN, it introduces a **memory cell** with a **gated mechanism** (forget, input, and output gates) that selectively regulates information flow.

**Key Advantage:** It mitigates the **vanishing gradient problem** in Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) by enforcing an **additive update** of the cell state $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, which preserves gradient magnitude across long time horizons.

*(Allocate ~1 mark for definition, ~1 mark for gating mechanism mention, ~1 mark for vanishing gradient solution.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Purpose of the Forget Gate in an LSTM Cell? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **forget gate** $f_t$ in an LSTM cell decides **which information from the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ should be discarded** at the current time step. It is computed as:

$$
f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)
$$

The output $f_t$ is a vector with values in $(0, 1)$ applied element-wise to $C_{t-1}$. Values close to 0 indicate "forget this component," and values close to 1 indicate "retain this component." The bias $b_f$ is typically initialized to 1 to encourage the default behavior of remembering.

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks for equation, ~1 mark for explanation, ~0.5 mark for bias initialization.)*

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Detailed Analysis of LSTM Architecture **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Modified)]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the architecture of a single LSTM cell, clearly labeling the three gates, the cell state, and the hidden state. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward pass equations of an LSTM cell and explain, with a clear mathematical argument, why LSTMs alleviate the vanishing gradient problem during Backpropagation Through Time. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Diagram (3 Marks):**
Draw a rectangular cell with:
- Three vertical sigmoid blocks labeled **Forget Gate ($f_t$)**, **Input Gate ($i_t$)**, **Output Gate ($o_t$)**.
- One tanh block labeled **Candidate Cell State ($\tilde{C}_t$)**.
- A horizontal line across the top representing the **cell state $C_t$**.
- An output line on the right representing the **hidden state $h_t$**.
- Input arrows on the left showing $x_t$ and $h_{t-1}$ entering through concatenation.

**Role of Each Component (4 Marks):**

| Component | Role |
|:---|:---|
| **Forget Gate ($f_t$)** | Decides which past information in $C_{t-1}$ to discard. Outputs values in $(0, 1)$ as a soft mask. |
| **Input Gate ($i_t$)** | Decides which new candidate values $\tilde{C}_t$ are worth storing. |
| **Candidate Cell State ($\tilde{C}_t$)** | Creates new candidate values in $(-1, 1)$ that could be added to the memory. |
| **Cell State ($C_t$)** | The long-term memory highway; updated additively as $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$. |
| **Output Gate ($o_t$)** | Filters the squashed cell state to produce the working memory $h_t$ exposed externally. |
| **Hidden State ($h_t$)** | The short-term output; passed to the next time step and to higher network layers. |

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Forward Pass Derivation (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_f) \\
i_t &= \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_i) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_C) \\
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
o_t &= \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_o) \\
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Vanishing Gradient Argument (4 Marks):**

The gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to a past cell state $C_k$ flows backward through $T - k$ multiplicative factors:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial C_k} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial C_T} \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}
$$

Since $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, the dominant term of $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}$ is $\text{diag}(f_t)$, so:

$$
\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial C_k} \approx \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} \text{diag}(f_t)
$$

Element-wise:

$$
\left[\frac{\partial C_T}{\partial C_k}\right]_{j,j} \approx \prod_{t=k+1}^{T} f_t^{(j)}
$$

If $f_t^{(j)} \approx 1$ (encouraged by the bias initialization $b_f = 1$), the product remains near 1 even for large $T - k$, preventing the exponential decay that plagues vanilla RNNs. In contrast, vanilla RNNs have $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(\tanh'(W_{hh} h_{t-1})) W_{hh}$, which leads to products of values strictly less than 1 in magnitude, hence vanishing gradients.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Comparative and Computational Analysis **[14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Modified)]**

**(a)** Compare LSTM with vanilla RNN and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Tabulate differences in architecture, parameters, and use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an LSTM cell with hidden size $H = 64$ and input size $d = 32$, compute the total number of trainable parameters. Also, explain how the forget gate bias is initialized and justify why. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Memory Mechanism** | Single hidden state $h_t$ | Cell state $C_t$ + hidden state $h_t$ | Single hidden state $h_t$ (no separate cell state) |
| **Gates** | None | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (reset, update) |
| **Number of Parameters** | $H(H+d) + H$ | $4H(H+d) + 4H$ | $3H(H+d) + 3H$ |
| **Vanishing Gradient Solution** | No native solution | Yes (additive cell state update) | Yes (gated linear units) |
| **Long-Range Dependencies** | Poor | Excellent | Very Good |
| **Computational Cost** | Lowest | Highest (4× vanilla RNN) | Moderate (3× vanilla RNN) |
| **Use Case Preference** | Short sequences, low-cost | Long sequences, complex tasks | Mid-length sequences, faster training |

**Key Differentiators (2 Marks):**
- LSTM has a **separate output gate**, while GRU **merges the cell state and hidden state** into a single vector.
- LSTM's **forget + input gate pair** is conceptually equivalent to GRU's **update gate**, but the parameterizations differ.
- LSTMs are preferred when **fine-grained memory control** is needed; GRUs are preferred when **training speed and parameter efficiency** matter more.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Parameter Computation (4 Marks):**

Each of the four learnable transformations in an LSTM has:
- A weight matrix of shape $H \times (H + d)$
- A bias vector of shape $H \times 1$

With $H = 64$ and $d = 32$:

- Weights per transformation: $64 \times (64 + 32) = 64 \times 96 = 6144$
- Biases per transformation: $64$
- Per transformation total: $6144 + 64 = 6208$
- Four transformations (forget, input, candidate, output):

$$
\text{Total parameters} = 4 \times 6208 = \mathbf{24{,}832}
$$

**Forget Gate Bias Initialization (3 Marks):**

The forget gate bias $b_f$ is initialized to **$1$** (or a small positive value like $1$ or $2$) rather than the default zero initialization. The justification is:

- Sigmoid(0) = 0.5, which means at the start of training, the network would **forget half of every past memory component by default**.
- Sigmoid(1) ≈ 0.731, which means the network **retains about 73% of every past component by default**, providing a stable starting point.
- The network can then *learn* to decrease $b_f$ for genuinely irrelevant information, rather than starting from an aggressive forgetting stance and struggling to recover useful long-term memories.

This bias initialization trick, proposed by **Gers et al. (2000)**, was a critical practical innovation that made deep LSTM training feasible.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LSTM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the output gate with the input gate** — students frequently swap their equations. Memorize: *Input* controls **writing** to memory, *Output* controls **reading** from memory, *Forget* controls **erasing** from memory.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the tanh on the cell state** when computing $h_t$. The correct form is $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$, **NOT** $h_t = o_t \odot C_t$. Examiners specifically check for this.
>
> 3. **Miscounting parameters** — remember that each gate has a **bias vector** in addition to its weight matrix. The factor of 4 applies to both weights and biases, not just weights.
>
> 4. **Stating the vanishing gradient "solution" without the additive update argument** — the examiner wants to see the explicit gradient product $\prod \text{diag}(f_t)$ and the contrast with vanilla RNN's $\prod \tanh'(W_{hh} h_{t-1}) W_{hh}$. A vague statement like "LSTMs solve vanishing gradients" loses 2 of 4 marks.
>
> 5. **Writing the candidate equation as $\tilde{C}_t = \sigma(\cdot)$ instead of $\tanh(\cdot)$** — the candidate uses tanh (range $-1$ to $1$), not sigmoid. This is a frequently-tested distinction.
>
> 6. **Drawing the cell state line on the bottom** instead of the top of the diagram. The standard convention (and the one examiners expect) places the cell state on top to emphasize the "memory highway" metaphor.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LSTM stands for Long Short-Term Memory** and was introduced to solve the vanishing/exploding gradient problem in vanilla RNNs.
- **Three gates** operate at every time step: **Forget** ($f_t$), **Input** ($i_t$), **Output** ($o_t$) — all are sigmoid layers, all conditioned on $[h_{t-1}; x_t]$.
- The **candidate cell state** $\tilde{C}_t$ uses a **tanh activation** (range $-1$ to $1$), not sigmoid.
- The **cell state update** $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is the heart of the LSTM. The **additive structure** is what preserves gradient flow.
- The **hidden state** $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ is the working memory exposed externally; the cell state $C_t$ is the long-term memory passed internally.
- The **forget gate bias** is initialized to **$1$** to encourage remembering by default.
- The total number of parameters per LSTM cell is $4H(H+d) + 4H$ — a 14-mark exam favorite.
- The **GRU is a simplified alternative** with 2 gates (reset, update) and 3 learnable transformations, but lacks a separate cell state and output gate.
- LSTMs are best suited for **long sequential data** with complex temporal patterns: NLP, time-series forecasting, speech recognition, video analysis.
- **Gradient flow** through $C_t$ is $\prod \text{diag}(f_t)$, which stays bounded away from 0 when $f_t \approx 1$, unlike vanilla RNNs where gradients vanish exponentially.
- A common KTU board trick: ask for the **number of parameters, the role of the forget gate, or the vanishing gradient argument** — be prepared with crisp 3-mark answers for each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs)** are a streamlined variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) gating architectures, formally introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**. GRUs are engineered to mitigate the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** that plagues standard RNNs during back-propagation through time (BPTT). They achieve this by introducing two dedicated gating mechanisms — the **Update Gate** and the **Reset Gate** — which dynamically regulate the flow of information across temporal sequences.

A GRU cell, at any time step $t$, takes two inputs:
1. The previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$
2. The current input vector $x_t$

And produces one output — the current hidden state $h_t$ — which is passed forward to the next time step.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Editor-in-Chief

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a journalist (the network) writing a long news article (a long sequence). Every day, new facts arrive. An **Editor-in-Chief** sits beside the journalist and decides:
> - **"How much of yesterday's draft should I keep?"** → This is the **Update Gate**.
> - **"Should I throw away old context because today's news completely changes the story?"** → This is the **Reset Gate**.

Just as a skilled editor decides what to remember, what to forget, and what to add, the GRU's two gates mathematically control memory retention and contextual reset within the recurrent network.

## 1.3 Why GRUs? — The Motivation

Standard RNNs compute the hidden state using:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} \cdot h_{t-1} + W_{xh} \cdot x_t + b)$$

The repeated multiplication of $W_{hh}$ during BPTT causes gradients to either shrink to **0** (vanish) or blow up to **infinity** (explode). GRUs solve this by using **gated additive memory updates**, allowing gradients to flow through the network unimpeded over long sequences.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** GRUs are often compared with **LSTMs**. GRUs have **2 gates** while LSTMs have **3 gates** (input, forget, output). GRUs merge the **cell state** and **hidden state** into a single vector, making them computationally lighter and faster to train.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Gates of a GRU

### 2.1.1 The Update Gate ($z_t$)

The Update Gate determines **how much of the past information (from previous time steps) needs to be passed along to the future**. It is analogous to the output of the forget and input gates of an LSTM combined.

> [!NOTE]
> A value of $z_t \to 1$ means "copy the previous hidden state entirely" (long-term memory).
> A value of $z_t \to 0$ means "completely replace the hidden state with the new candidate" (short-term memory).

### 2.1.2 The Reset Gate ($r_t$)

The Reset Gate is used to decide **how much of the past information to forget**. It is applied directly to the previous hidden state before computing the new candidate activation, allowing the model to selectively discard irrelevant historical context.

> [!NOTE]
> A value of $r_t \to 0$ means "forget the previous hidden state" — useful when starting a new semantic segment in a sequence.
> A value of $r_t \to 1$ means "keep the previous hidden state fully".

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The forward pass of a GRU cell at time step $t$ executes the following logical steps:

1. **Concatenation:** Concatenate the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and current input $x_t$ into a single vector $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$.
2. **Gate Computation:** Pass this vector through two separate linear layers, followed by the sigmoid ($\sigma$) activation, to produce $z_t$ and $r_t$.
3. **Candidate State:** Compute the candidate hidden state $\tilde{h}_t$ using $\tanh$ activation. The reset gate $r_t$ element-wise multiplies with $h_{t-1}$ to control how much past context is used.
4. **Linear Interpolation (Final Output):** The final hidden state $h_t$ is a convex combination (weighted average) of the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ and the candidate hidden state $\tilde{h}_t$, where the weights are controlled by $z_t$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Range / Bound | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Controls retention of past memory |
| Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Controls forgetting of past context |
| Candidate State | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Proposed new memory content |
| Hidden Output | $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Final output passed forward |
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | Gate activation function |
| Hyperbolic Tangent | $\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | Candidate state activation |
| Element-wise Product | $\odot$ | — | Hadamard product operator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** The operator $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**, NOT matrix multiplication. This is a frequent point of confusion in KTU examinations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

GRUs are extensively deployed in:
- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Machine translation, text summarization, speech recognition.
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock price prediction, weather modeling, IoT sensor analytics.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure prediction, gene sequence modeling.
- **Audio Processing:** Music generation, voice activity detection.

The reduced parameter count of GRUs (compared to LSTMs) makes them ideal for **edge devices** and **real-time systems** where memory and latency are constrained.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of GRU Forward Pass

Let the hidden state dimension be $n$ and input dimension be $d$. The concatenated input vector at time $t$ has dimension $(n + d)$.

### Step 1: Construct the Concatenated Vector

The previous hidden state $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and current input $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ are concatenated:

$$c_t = [h_{t-1}; x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{n+d}$$

The semicolon $;$ denotes vertical concatenation (stacking of vectors).

### Step 2: Compute the Update Gate

The Update Gate $z_t$ is computed by applying a linear transformation followed by the sigmoid function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t &= \sigma(W_z \cdot c_t + b_z) \\
&= \sigma\Big( W_z \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} + b_z \Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $W_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+d)}$ is the trainable weight matrix for the update gate
- $b_z \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the bias vector
- $\sigma$ is the sigmoid activation squashing values to $(0, 1)$

### Step 3: Compute the Reset Gate

The Reset Gate $r_t$ follows an analogous structure with its own dedicated weight matrix $W_r$ and bias $b_r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_t &= \sigma(W_r \cdot c_t + b_r) \\
&= \sigma\Big( W_r \begin{bmatrix} h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} + b_r \Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why two separate weight matrices?** The Update and Reset gates serve fundamentally different purposes — the network must learn distinct transformations for "what to remember" versus "what to forget". Sharing weights would constrain the model's representational capacity.

### Step 4: Compute the Candidate Hidden State

First, the reset gate is applied element-wise to the previous hidden state to compute the "reset" hidden state:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_t \odot h_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This reset-modulated vector is then concatenated with $x_t$ and passed through a $\tanh$ activation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{h}_t &= \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}; x_t] + b) \\
&= \tanh\Big( W \begin{bmatrix} r_t \odot h_{t-1} \\ x_t \end{bmatrix} + b \Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The $\tanh$ squashes the output to $[-1, 1]$, representing the "new information content" that the candidate proposes to add to the hidden state.

### Step 5: Compute the Final Hidden State via Linear Interpolation

The final hidden state is a **convex combination** of $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$, weighted by $z_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t \\
&= z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t + (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation is a weighted average. If $z_t = 1$, then $h_t = \tilde{h}_t$ (complete replacement). If $z_t = 0$, then $h_t = h_{t-1}$ (complete retention). Intermediate values produce a smooth interpolation, which is the key mechanism enabling **gradient flow** through time.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: GRU vs LSTM vs Vanilla RNN

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM | GRU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of Gates | 0 | 3 (Input, Forget, Output) | 2 (Update, Reset) |
| Cell State $C_t$ | Absent | Present (separate) | Merged with $h_t$ |
| Hidden State | $h_t$ | $h_t$ | $h_t$ |
| Total Parameters (per cell) | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Training Speed | Fastest | Slowest | Fast |
| Long-Term Dependency Capture | Poor | Excellent | Very Good |
| Memory Footprint | Smallest | Largest | Moderate |
| Vanishing Gradient Resistance | None | Strong | Strong |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Interpolation Identity

The interpolation $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ can be rearranged to highlight the **residual connection**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= h_{t-1} - z_t \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t \\
h_t - h_{t-1} &= z_t \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** The change in hidden state is a **gated residual update**. This residual structure is mathematically similar to ResNet skip connections, allowing gradients to bypass the $\tanh$ non-linearity during backpropagation. The gradient $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ contains a direct path of magnitude $(1 - z_t)$, which prevents vanishing when $(1 - z_t)$ is near 1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GRU Cell Internal Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal functional flow of a single GRU cell at time step $t$, mapping all mathematical operations and data pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[/h_t_minus_1 - Previous Hidden State/]
    inputB[/x_t - Current Input Vector/]
    concatA[Concatenation Layer]
    linZ[Linear Layer W_z + b_z]
    linR[Linear Layer W_r + b_r]
    sigZ[Sigmoid Activation sigma]
    sigR[Sigmoid Activation sigma]
    gateZ[/z_t - Update Gate/]
    gateR[/r_t - Reset Gate/]
    hadamardA[Hadamard Product r_t O h_t_minus_1]
    concatB[Concatenation Layer]
    linH[Linear Layer W + b]
    tanhH[Hyperbolic Tangent Activation]
    candH[/h_tilde_t - Candidate Hidden State/]
    mulA[Multiply z_t O h_tilde_t]
    oneminusz[Compute 1 - z_t]
    mulB[Multiply 1_minus_z_t O h_t_minus_1]
    addA[Element-wise Addition]
    outputA[/h_t - Current Hidden State/]

    inputA --> concatA
    inputB --> concatA
    concatA --> linZ
    concatA --> linR
    linZ --> sigZ
    linR --> sigR
    sigZ --> gateZ
    sigR --> gateR

    gateR --> hadamardA
    inputA --> hadamardA
    hadamardA --> concatB
    inputB --> concatB
    concatB --> linH
    linH --> tanhH
    tanhH --> candH

    gateZ --> mulA
    candH --> mulA
    gateZ --> oneminusz
    oneminusz --> mulB
    inputA --> mulB
    mulA --> addA
    mulB --> addA
    addA --> outputA

    outputA -.->|Next Time Step| inputA
```

## 4.2 Unrolled GRU Across Multiple Time Steps

The following diagram shows how a GRU network is unrolled across a sequence of $T$ time steps, demonstrating the recurrent nature of information propagation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1[/x_1/] --> cellA[GRU Cell t=1]
    x2[/x_2/] --> cellB[GRU Cell t=2]
    x3[/x_3/] --> cellC[GRU Cell t=3]
    x4[/x_4/] --> cellD[GRU Cell t=T]
    h0[/h_0 - Initial Hidden State/] --> cellA
    cellA --> h1[/h_1/]
    h1 --> cellB
    cellB --> h2[/h_2/]
    h2 --> cellC
    cellC --> h3[/h_3/]
    h3 --> cellD
    cellD --> hT[/h_T - Final Output/]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: RNN vs LSTM vs GRU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rnnBlock[RNN Cell - No Gates - Tanh Activation Only]
    lstmBlock[LSTM Cell - 3 Gates - Input, Forget, Output - Separate Cell State]
    gruBlock[GRU Cell - 2 Gates - Update, Reset - Merged Hidden State]

    rnnBlock -.->|Short sequences, low complexity| useCaseA[Simple Time Series]
    lstmBlock -.->|Long sequences, complex patterns| useCaseB[Machine Translation, Speech]
    gruBlock -.->|Moderate sequences, efficiency-critical| useCaseC[IoT, Edge Devices, Chatbots]

    style gruBlock fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style lstmBlock fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:1px
    style rnnBlock fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The green-highlighted GRU block in the comparison diagram represents the optimal trade-off between representational power and computational efficiency — a frequent KTU exam discussion point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between the Update Gate and Reset Gate in a GRU. State the activation functions used for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Gate | Function | Activation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Update Gate ($z_t$)** | Decides how much of the past hidden state should be retained and forwarded to the next time step. Controls the interpolation between $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$. | **Sigmoid ($\sigma$)** — output range $[0, 1]$ |
| **Reset Gate ($r_t$)** | Decides how much of the past hidden state should be discarded before computing the new candidate state. It modulates $h_{t-1}$ element-wise via Hadamard product. | **Sigmoid ($\sigma$)** — output range $[0, 1]$ |

**Awarding marks:** (1 mark) for the Update Gate function, (1 mark) for the Reset Gate function, (1 mark) for the correct activation function and its range.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: List any three advantages of GRUs over standard LSTMs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Reduced Parameter Count:** GRUs have only 2 gates (Update and Reset) compared to LSTM's 3 gates (Input, Forget, Output), resulting in fewer trainable parameters. **(1 mark)**

2. **Faster Training and Inference:** Due to simpler architecture, GRUs converge faster on small to medium datasets and are computationally cheaper. **(1 mark)**

3. **Merged Cell and Hidden State:** GRUs combine the cell state and hidden state into a single vector, simplifying the information flow and reducing memory footprint. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, students must answer **one full question** (either A or B) from the module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: With a neat labeled diagram, explain the architecture of a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Derive all the governing equations for the forward pass and explain how GRUs overcome the vanishing gradient problem.**

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Forward Pass Equations (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**

A GRU cell consists of the following components:
- **Two Gating Units:** Update Gate ($z_t$) and Reset Gate ($r_t$)
- **One Candidate Hidden State Generator:** $\tilde{h}_t$
- **One Linear Interpolation Unit:** Combines $h_{t-1}$ and $\tilde{h}_t$ to produce $h_t$

The cell takes two inputs — $h_{t-1}$ (previous hidden state) and $x_t$ (current input) — and produces one output $h_t$.

**Forward Pass Equations (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Update Gate:**

$$
z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)
$$

**Step 2 — Reset Gate:**

$$
r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)
$$

**Step 3 — Candidate Hidden State:**

$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b)
$$

**Step 4 — Final Hidden State:**

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

#### Part (b) — Vanishing Gradient Mitigation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

In a standard RNN, during back-propagation through time, the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $h_{t-k}$ involves repeated multiplication by the Jacobian $W_{hh}^T$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_{t-k}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_t} \prod_{i=1}^{k} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}
$$

If the largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ is less than 1, the product shrinks exponentially, causing the **vanishing gradient problem**.

**How GRUs Solve It (7 marks breakdown):**

1. **Additive Update Structure (2 marks):** The final hidden state equation $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ is an **additive** combination, not multiplicative. Differentiating with respect to $h_{t-1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} = (1 - z_t) + \text{additional terms}
$$

The term $(1 - z_t)$ provides a **direct linear path** for the gradient, bypassing the non-linear transformations.

2. **Gated Gradient Control (2 marks):** When the network learns $z_t \approx 0$ for unimportant transitions, the gradient path through $(1 - z_t) \approx 1$ remains strong, allowing long-range dependencies to be learned.

3. **Sigmoid Bounded Gradients (2 marks):** Since $\sigma'(x) \leq 0.25$, the gradient does not explode. The reset gate also bounds how much past information influences the candidate, preventing chaotic gradient behavior.

4. **Residual Connection Analogy (1 mark):** The structure is analogous to ResNet skip connections — gradients can flow unimpeded across many time steps without attenuation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Compare the architectures of LSTM and GRU with suitable diagrams. Derive the equations for the candidate hidden state and final hidden state of a GRU. Discuss any two real-world applications where GRUs outperform LSTMs.**

#### Part (a) — LSTM vs GRU Comparison and GRU Derivations (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | LSTM | GRU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gates | 3 (Input, Forget, Output) | 2 (Update, Reset) |
| Cell State | Separate $C_t$ | Merged with $h_t$ |
| Hidden State | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | $h_t = (1-z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ |
| Memory Mechanism | Additive (forget + input) | Single interpolation |
| Parameters | $4 \times (n \times (n+d) + n)$ | $3 \times (n \times (n+d) + n)$ |

**Derivation of Candidate Hidden State (2 marks):**

The candidate hidden state $\tilde{h}_t$ represents new information proposed for inclusion in the hidden state. The reset gate $r_t$ first modulates the previous hidden state:

$$
r_t \odot h_{t-1}
$$

This is then concatenated with $x_t$ and passed through a linear layer and $\tanh$ activation:

$$
\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}; x_t] + b)
$$

**Derivation of Final Hidden State (2 marks):**

The final hidden state combines past and new information:

$$
h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t
$$

This can be rearranged as $h_t = h_{t-1} + z_t \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})$, showing a residual update.

#### Part (b) — Real-World Applications Where GRUs Outperform LSTMs (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Application 1: Speech Recognition on Edge Devices (3.5 marks):**

GRUs are preferred in on-device speech recognition (e.g., virtual assistants on smartphones, smart speakers) because:
- **Lower memory footprint:** ~33% fewer parameters than LSTM
- **Lower latency:** Faster inference suitable for real-time processing
- **Lower power consumption:** Critical for battery-operated devices

**Application 2: Short-to-Medium Length Text Classification (3.5 marks):**

For tasks like sentiment analysis of tweets, news article classification, or chatbot intent recognition (sequence lengths 20-200 tokens):
- **Faster convergence:** GRUs reach comparable accuracy with fewer training epochs
- **Reduced overfitting risk:** Fewer parameters mean less tendency to overfit on smaller datasets
- **Competitive accuracy:** Empirical studies (Chung et al., 2014) show GRUs match or exceed LSTM performance on several benchmarks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Confusing Hadamard product with matrix multiplication:** Students often write $r_t \cdot h_{t-1}$ instead of $r_t \odot h_{t-1}$. This costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Omitting bias terms:** Equations like $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t])$ without $b_z$ are incomplete. Deduct **0.5 marks** per missing bias.
> 3. **Wrong activation for candidate state:** Using sigmoid instead of $\tanh$ for $\tilde{h}_t$ — deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Saying GRU has 3 gates:** This is a critical conceptual error — GRUs have exactly 2 gates. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not explaining vanishing gradient mitigation explicitly:** Simply stating "GRUs solve vanishing gradients" without deriving the additive update property loses **2-3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GRU Full Form:** Gated Recurrent Unit — introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**.
- **Number of Gates:** **2** (Update Gate and Reset Gate) — this is the key distinguishing feature.
- **Activation Functions:** Sigmoid ($\sigma$) for gates; Tanh for candidate state.
- **Core Innovation:** Additive interpolation of hidden states prevents multiplicative gradient decay.
- **Final Hidden State Formula:** $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ — memorize this verbatim.
- **Operator Distinction:** $\odot$ = Hadamard (element-wise) product; never matrix multiplication.
- **GRU vs LSTM:** GRUs have fewer parameters, faster training, and merged cell/hidden state.
- **Vanishing Gradient Solution:** The $(1 - z_t)$ term in the interpolation provides a direct gradient highway.
- **Initial Hidden State:** $h_0$ is typically initialized to a zero vector $\mathbf{0}$ of appropriate dimension.
- **Dimensions:** For hidden size $n$ and input size $d$, weight matrices $W_z, W_r, W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (n+d)}$.
- **Common Use Cases:** Speech recognition, text generation, time-series forecasting, anomaly detection.
- **KTP Quick Recall:** **U**pdate = **W**hat to keep; **R**eset = **W**hat to forget.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** is the gradient-based learning algorithm used to train **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)**. It works by *unrolling* the recurrent network across all time steps of an input sequence, treating it as a deep feedforward network with shared parameters across layers (where each "layer" is a time step). Standard backpropagation is then applied across this unrolled computational graph to compute gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter at every time step.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, BPTT is positioned as the canonical extension of the classical backpropagation algorithm (covered in Module 1 / earlier deep learning modules) to **temporally extended architectures**. The principle is mathematically identical: chain rule + reverse-mode automatic differentiation — but the *domain* of differentiation is the time dimension, not just the layer dimension.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Aligned)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Unrolling** | Duplicating the RNN cell for each time step to form a static directed acyclic graph (DAG). |
| **Hidden State** | The internal memory vector $\mathbf{h}_t$ that summarizes past information at time $t$. |
| **Parameter Sharing** | The same weight matrices ($\mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$) are reused at every time step. |
| **Truncated BPTT** | A practical variant that limits gradient computation to a fixed window $\tau$ of past time steps. |
| **Teacher Forcing** | Using ground-truth previous outputs (instead of model predictions) as inputs during training. |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Echo Game"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a student (the RNN) listening to a 10-word sentence.** After hearing the whole sentence, the teacher asks: *"What did the 5th word mean?"* To answer, the student has to mentally **rewind** through words 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 (most recent influence) and then words 4, 3, 2, 1 (older influence), assigning *blame or credit* to each word for the final understanding.

**BPTT is precisely this rewinding process:**

- The *forward pass* = listening to the sentence and building an internal understanding (hidden states).
- The *backward pass* = going back in time, propagating the error signal through each word (time step), and updating the student's listening skill (weights).

**Critical insight:** In a long sentence, the further back you go, the more *faint* (vanishing) or *overwhelming* (exploding) the error signal becomes — this is the **Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Problem**, the central motivation for advanced variants like **LSTM** and **GRU**.

---

## 1.3 Why BPTT Matters in Engineering

BPTT is the workhorse algorithm behind:
- **Speech Recognition Systems** (e.g., Google Assistant, Siri)
- **Machine Translation** (sequence-to-sequence models)
- **Time-Series Forecasting** (stock prices, weather, sensor data)
- **Music Generation** and natural language generation
- **Bioinformatics** (protein sequence modeling)

The algorithm is mathematically equivalent to backpropagation in a feedforward network, but its challenges (long-range dependencies, gradient stability) make it a non-trivial research and engineering topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RNN Forward Pass — Foundational Equations

For a standard (Elman) RNN processing an input sequence $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_T$ with corresponding targets $\mathbf{y}_1, \mathbf{y}_2, \dots, \mathbf{y}_T$, the forward dynamics are:

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\,\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\,\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h\right)
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t = \text{softmax}\!\left(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\,\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y\right)
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{h}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ is the hidden state vector at time $t$
- $\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ is the input vector at time $t$
- $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$ is the predicted output (e.g., class probabilities)
- $\mathbf{W}_{xh} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times D}$ is the input-to-hidden weight matrix
- $\mathbf{W}_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times H}$ is the hidden-to-hidden (recurrent) weight matrix
- $\mathbf{W}_{hy} \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H}$ is the hidden-to-output weight matrix
- $\mathbf{b}_h, \mathbf{b}_y$ are bias vectors
- $\tanh(\cdot)$ is the hyperbolic tangent activation (saturates between $-1$ and $+1$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter sharing** is critical: $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is the *same* matrix at every time step. This is what makes the network "recurrent" rather than simply "deep" along the time axis.

---

## 2.2 Loss Function Over a Sequence

The total loss is the sum (or average) of per-time-step losses:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t\!\left(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t,\, \mathbf{y}_t\right)
$$

For classification tasks, $\mathcal{L}_t$ is typically the **cross-entropy loss**:

$$
\mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} \mathbf{y}_{t,c}\,\log\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t,c}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The loss depends on the *entire history* of hidden states $\{\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2, \dots, \mathbf{h}_T\}$ because each $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t$ depends on $\mathbf{h}_t$, which depends on $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$, and so on back to $\mathbf{h}_0$. This is the source of the "through time" dependency.

---

## 2.3 The Backward Pass — Gradient Computation

The objective of BPTT is to compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{xh}}$, $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$, and $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}}$.

### Step A — Output Layer Gradient

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}}
$$

### Step B — Hidden State Gradient (The Heart of BPTT)

Define $\boldsymbol{\delta}_t = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}$. This is the **error signal** at the hidden state at time $t$.

The recurrence for $\boldsymbol{\delta}_t$ flows **backwards in time**:

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}_t = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \cdot \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)\right) \cdot \boldsymbol{\delta}_{t+1}
$$

Expanded in summation form (the canonical BPTT form):

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}_t = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \;+\; \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \left[\prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \cdot \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_j)\right)\right] \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The product $\prod_{j=t+1}^{k}$ is the source of vanishing/exploding gradients.** If the largest singular value of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is $>1$, the product **explodes** exponentially; if $<1$, it **vanishes** exponentially.

### Step C — Parameter Gradients

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \boldsymbol{\delta}_t^{\top} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \cdot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{xh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \cdot \mathbf{x}_t^{\top}
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Where Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$ | Hidden state update | Forward pass |
| 2 | $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$ | Output prediction | Forward pass |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t, \mathbf{y}_t)$ | Total sequence loss | Objective |
| 4 | $\boldsymbol{\delta}_t = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}$ | Hidden error signal | Backward pass |
| 5 | $\boldsymbol{\delta}_t = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}(1-\tanh^2)\,\boldsymbol{\delta}_{t+1}$ | BPTT recurrence | Backward pass |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\top}$ | Recurrent weight gradient | Update step |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{xh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \mathbf{x}_t^{\top}$ | Input weight gradient | Update step |
| 8 | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t} \dfrac{\partial \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hy}}$ | Output weight gradient | Update step |
| 9 | $\gamma = \sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) \cdot \max \, \vert 1 - \tanh^2(\cdot) \vert$ | Spectral radius condition for stability | Gradient analysis |
| 10 | Truncated BPTT window: $\tau$ | Only backpropagate $\tau$ steps back | Practical training |

---

## 2.5 The Vanishing & Exploding Gradient Problem

Let $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_H$ be the eigenvalues (or singular values) of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. Then:

$$
\left\Vert \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_j} \right\Vert \;\leq\; \left(\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})\right)^{k-j} \cdot \prod_{i=j+1}^{k} \gamma_i
$$

where $\gamma_i = \vert 1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_i) \vert \in [0, 1]$.

> [!WARNING]
> If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1/\gamma_{\max}$, gradients **vanish** exponentially as $k - j$ grows. The model becomes unable to learn dependencies longer than ~10 steps. This is the central motivation for **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** networks introduced in the next module.

---

## 2.6 Truncated BPTT (TBPTT) — Practical Variant

For sequences of length $T = 1000$ or more, full BPTT is computationally infeasible. **Truncated BPTT** limits the backward pass to a window of $\tau$ time steps:

$$
\boldsymbol{\delta}_t^{\text{trunc}} \approx \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top}\,\text{diag}(1-\tanh^2)\cdot\boldsymbol{\delta}_{t+1}^{\text{trunc}}, \quad \text{for } t > t_{\text{current}} - \tau
$$

- **Forward pass**: still unroll the full sequence to maintain state continuity.
- **Backward pass**: only compute gradients for the most recent $\tau$ steps.

This is a **biased estimator** of the true gradient but is computationally tractable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Full BPTT Gradient Derivation for $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$

### Setup

We consider a sequence of length $T=3$ for concreteness. The network produces hidden states $\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2, \mathbf{h}_3$ and outputs $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_1, \hat{\mathbf{y}}_2, \hat{\mathbf{y}}_3$.

Loss at each time step: $\mathcal{L}_t = \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t, \mathbf{y}_t)$.

Total loss:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_1 + \mathcal{L}_2 + \mathcal{L}_3
$$

We want to find $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$.

### Step 1 — Note that $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ affects the loss at *every* time step

$\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ appears in the recurrence at $t=1, 2, 3$. By the **chain rule for shared parameters**:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply chain rule to expand $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}$

Each $\mathcal{L}_t$ depends on $\mathbf{h}_t$, which depends on $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$, etc. So:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}
$$

### Step 3 — Derive the recurrent Jacobian $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{t-1}}$

From $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t)$ where $\mathbf{a}_t = \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{t-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_t)\right) \cdot \mathbf{W}_{hh} \equiv \mathbf{D}_t \, \mathbf{W}_{hh}
$$

### Step 4 — Compose Jacobians across time

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{D}_j \, \mathbf{W}_{hh}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine everything

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{D}_j \, \mathbf{W}_{hh} \right) \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}}
$$

And since $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \mathbf{D}_k \, \mathbf{h}_{k-1}^{\top}$ (treating $\mathbf{h}_{k-1}$ as a column vector):

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{D}_j \, \mathbf{W}_{hh} \right) \mathbf{D}_k \, \mathbf{h}_{k-1}^{\top}}
$$

### Step 6 — Sum over all time steps

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{D}_j \, \mathbf{W}_{hh} \right) \mathbf{D}_k \, \mathbf{h}_{k-1}^{\top}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (T=2 Simplified)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the type of small numerical example KTU examiners often set to test understanding of the chain rule through time.

**Setup:** $H = 1$ (single hidden unit), $D = 1$ (single input), activation = $\tanh$.

Given:
- $\mathbf{x}_1 = 0.5,\ \mathbf{x}_2 = 0.8$
- $\mathbf{h}_0 = 0.0$
- $\mathbf{W}_{xh} = 0.4,\ \mathbf{W}_{hh} = 0.6,\ b_h = 0$
- Targets: $y_1 = 1,\ y_2 = 0$

### Forward Pass

**Time $t=1$:**

$$
a_1 = (0.4)(0.5) + (0.6)(0.0) = 0.20
$$

$$
h_1 = \tanh(0.20) = 0.1974
$$

**Time $t=2$:**

$$
a_2 = (0.4)(0.8) + (0.6)(0.1974) = 0.32 + 0.1184 = 0.4384
$$

$$
h_2 = \tanh(0.4384) = 0.4115
$$

### Derivative Terms

The derivative of $\tanh$ is $1 - \tanh^2(x)$:

$$
D_1 = 1 - (0.1974)^2 = 0.9610
$$

$$
D_2 = 1 - (0.4115)^2 = 0.8307
$$

### BPTT Backward — Compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}$

Assume MSE loss: $\mathcal{L}_t = (y_t - h_t)^2$ (simplified — treating $h_t$ as the prediction).

Then $\partial \mathcal{L}_t / \partial h_t = 2(h_t - y_t)$.

**At $t=2$:** $\partial \mathcal{L}_2/\partial h_2 = 2(0.4115 - 0) = 0.8230$

**At $t=1$:** $\partial \mathcal{L}_1/\partial h_1 = 2(0.1974 - 1) = -1.6052$

### Apply the formula

For $T=2$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{2} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial h_t} \left( \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} D_j \, W_{hh} \right) D_k \, h_{k-1}
$$

**Compute each term:**

| $(t, k)$ | $\partial \mathcal{L}_t/\partial h_t$ | Product $\prod D_j W_{hh}$ | $D_k h_{k-1}$ | Contribution |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(1,1)$ | $-1.6052$ | $1$ (empty product) | $0.9610 \times 0.0 = 0$ | $0$ |
| $(2,1)$ | $0.8230$ | $D_2 \cdot W_{hh} = 0.8307 \times 0.6 = 0.4984$ | $0.9610 \times 0.0 = 0$ | $0$ |
| $(2,2)$ | $0.8230$ | $1$ (empty product) | $0.8307 \times 0.1974 = 0.1640$ | $\mathbf{0.1350}$ |

**Total gradient:** $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} \approx 0.1350$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that for $k=1$, the term is zero because $h_0 = 0$ — a pedagogical reminder that **initial hidden state matters** for the magnitude of early gradients.

### Update Rule (Gradient Descent)

$$
\mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\text{new}} = \mathbf{W}_{hh} - \eta \cdot 0.1350 = 0.6 - (0.01)(0.1350) = 0.59865
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary of the Chain-Through-Time

The essence of BPTT can be written in one unified expression:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \underbrace{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}}_{\text{future loss}} \cdot \underbrace{\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{D}_j \mathbf{W}_{hh}}_{\text{path through time}} \cdot \underbrace{\mathbf{D}_k \mathbf{h}_{k-1}^{\top}}_{\text{local gradient at } k}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Three structural components:**
> 1. **Local gradient** at the affected time step $k$.
> 2. **Path-dependent multiplier** — the chain of Jacobians from $k$ to $t$.
> 3. **Outer sum** aggregating contributions across all (target, source) time pairs.
>
> The "path-dependent multiplier" is **why** BPTT struggles with long sequences — long paths mean more multiplications, which compounds the vanishing/exploding effect.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Compact (Recurrent) RNN Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["Input xt"] --> nodeCell["RNN Cell"]
    nodeHPrev["Hidden ht-1"] --> nodeCell
    nodeCell --> nodeHNew["Hidden ht"]
    nodeCell --> nodeY["Output yt"]
    nodeHNew -.->|"loop in time"| nodeHPrev
```

**Interpretation:** The single "RNN Cell" is the same block reused at every time step. The dashed loop-back arrow represents the recurrent dependency in compact form.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Unrolled RNN Through Time (T=4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1["x1"] --> cell1["Cell t=1"]
    h0["h0"] --> cell1
    cell1 --> h1["h1"]
    cell1 --> y1["y1"]

    x2["x2"] --> cell2["Cell t=2"]
    h1 --> cell2
    cell2 --> h2["h2"]
    cell2 --> y2["y2"]

    x3["x3"] --> cell3["Cell t=3"]
    h2 --> cell3
    cell3 --> h3["h3"]
    cell3 --> y3["y3"]

    x4["x4"] --> cell4["Cell t=4"]
    h3 --> cell4
    cell4 --> h4["h4"]
    cell4 --> y4["y4"]

    y1 --> loss["Loss L"]
    y2 --> loss
    y3 --> loss
    y4 --> loss
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **computational graph** on which BPTT operates. Each "Cell" is a copy of the same RNN with shared weights. Gradients flow in the **reverse direction** (right to left) during the backward pass.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — BPTT Backward Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lossnode["Total Loss L"] --> bpttCore["BPTT Engine"]
    h4["h4"] --> bpttCore
    h3["h3"] --> bpttCore
    h2["h2"] --> bpttCore
    h1["h1"] --> bpttCore
    bpttCore --> gradWhh["dL/dWhh"]
    bpttCore --> gradWxh["dL/dWxh"]
    bpttCore --> gradWhy["dL/dWhy"]
    gradWhh --> sgd["SGD Update"]
    gradWxh --> sgd
    gradWhy --> sgd
```

**Interpretation:** The BPTT engine collects hidden states, loss signal, and parameters, then emits gradients which are passed to an optimizer (SGD/Adam/RMSProp) for parameter updates.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Vanishing vs Exploding Gradient Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VChain["Vanishing: sigma less than 1"]
        direction LR
        v1["0.8"] --> v2["0.64"] --> v3["0.512"] --> v4["0.41"] --> v5["0.33"]
    end

    subgraph EChain["Exploding: sigma greater than 1"]
        direction LR
        e1["1.2"] --> e2["1.44"] --> e3["1.728"] --> e4["2.07"] --> e5["2.49"]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** As the chain of Jacobians lengthens (more time steps), the product of values either shrinks toward zero (vanishing) or grows toward infinity (exploding). The shrinkage/growth rate is governed by $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh})$.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Truncated BPTT Window (Practical Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t=0"] --> t1["t=1"] --> t2["t=2"] --> t3["t=3"] --> t4["t=4"] --> t5["t=5"] --> t6["t=6"]

    t3 -.->|"backprop window tau=3"| t4
    t4 -.-> t5
    t5 -.-> t6

    style t0 stroke-dasharray: 4 4
    style t1 stroke-dasharray: 4 4
    style t2 stroke-dasharray: 4 4
```

**Interpretation:** Dashed boxes = gradients *not* computed (beyond the truncation window $\tau = 3$). The forward pass still processes the full sequence, but the backward pass is clipped.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). Mention its computational complexity. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BPTT** is the training algorithm for **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)**. It works by *unrolling* the RNN across all $T$ time steps of an input sequence, transforming the cyclic graph into a static directed acyclic graph (DAG). Standard backpropagation is then applied on this unrolled graph, using the chain rule to propagate gradients backward through time.
>
> **Computational Complexity:**
> - **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(T)$ per weight matrix update (one pass forward, one pass backward through the unrolled graph).
> - **Space complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(T \cdot H)$ to store all hidden states needed for the backward pass.
> - **Per-step gradient cost:** $\mathcal{O}(H^2)$ due to the $\mathbf{W}_{hh} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times H}$ multiplication.
>
> For long sequences ($T \gg 100$), full BPTT becomes intractable, motivating **Truncated BPTT** which limits backpropagation to a window of $\tau$ steps.

---

### Q2. What is the vanishing gradient problem in BPTT? How does it affect learning long-term dependencies? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> During BPTT, the gradient of the loss with respect to a parameter at time step $k$ involves the product of Jacobians $\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{D}_j \mathbf{W}_{hh}$ across all intermediate time steps. This product has $t - k$ terms.
>
> - If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$, the product **decays exponentially** with $t - k$, causing the gradient to vanish for long-range dependencies.
> - The hidden unit activation derivative $\gamma_j = 1 - \tanh^2(\cdot) \le 1$ further compounds this decay.
>
> **Effect on learning:**
> - The model can learn short-term dependencies (within ~5–10 time steps) but **cannot** learn long-term patterns.
> - For example, in language modeling, the network may fail to connect a pronoun at position 50 with its antecedent at position 5.
>
> **Solutions:** Use **LSTM/GRU** (gated architectures), use **ReLU** activation, careful weight initialization, or **gradient clipping** for the exploding counterpart.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive the BPTT equations for the hidden state error signal $\delta_t$ in a vanilla RNN with $\tanh$ activation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simple RNN has weights $W_{xh} = 0.3$, $W_{hh} = 0.5$, and bias $b_h = 0$. Given $h_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 0.4$, $x_2 = 0.6$, compute $h_1$ and $h_2$ using the forward pass. If the target at $t=2$ is $y_2 = 0.5$ and the loss is $\mathcal{L} = (y_2 - h_2)^2$, compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The hidden state is:

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{a}_t), \quad \text{where } \mathbf{a}_t = \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h
$$

The loss is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t, \mathbf{y}_t)
$$

We define $\delta_t = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}$.

**Assumption 1:** The loss at time $t$ depends on $\mathbf{h}_t$ only (not on earlier hidden states directly).

**Assumption 2:** Activations are $\tanh$, with derivative $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$.

**Derivation:**

Using the chain rule on $\mathcal{L}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \sum_{k>t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}
$$

We need $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}$. Since $\mathbf{h}_k$ depends on $\mathbf{h}_{k-1}$, recursively:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}} = \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \mathbf{D}_j \mathbf{W}_{hh}
$$

where $\mathbf{D}_j = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_j))$.

Substituting back:

$$
\delta_t = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \delta_k \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \mathbf{D}_j \mathbf{W}_{hh}
$$

Equivalently, in **recursive form** (used in implementation):

$$
\boxed{\delta_t = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \mathbf{D}_{t+1} \, \delta_{t+1}}
$$

This is the canonical BPTT equation. It is computed **backwards in time**, starting from $\delta_T$ (which depends only on the local loss at $T$).

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $W_{xh} = 0.3$, $W_{hh} = 0.5$, $b_h = 0$, $h_0 = 0$, $x_1 = 0.4$, $x_2 = 0.6$, $y_2 = 0.5$.

**Forward Pass:**

**Time $t=1$:**

$$
a_1 = W_{xh} \cdot x_1 + W_{hh} \cdot h_0 + b_h = (0.3)(0.4) + (0.5)(0) + 0 = 0.12
$$

$$
h_1 = \tanh(0.12) = 0.1191
$$

**Time $t=2$:**

$$
a_2 = W_{xh} \cdot x_2 + W_{hh} \cdot h_1 + b_h = (0.3)(0.6) + (0.5)(0.1191) + 0
$$

$$
a_2 = 0.18 + 0.0596 = 0.2396
$$

$$
h_2 = \tanh(0.2396) = 0.2355
$$

**Derivative Terms:**

$$
D_1 = 1 - h_1^2 = 1 - (0.1191)^2 = 0.9858
$$

$$
D_2 = 1 - h_2^2 = 1 - (0.2355)^2 = 0.9445
$$

**Loss Gradient w.r.t. $h_2$:**

Since $\mathcal{L} = (y_2 - h_2)^2$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_2} = -2(y_2 - h_2) = -2(0.5 - 0.2355) = -0.5290
$$

**BPTT Computation — Since loss only depends on $h_2$:**

For $t=2$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} \bigg|_{t=2} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_2} \cdot D_2 \cdot h_1 = (-0.5290)(0.9445)(0.1191) = -0.0595
$$

For $t=1$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} \bigg|_{t=1} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_2} \cdot (D_2 W_{hh}) \cdot D_1 \cdot h_0
$$

Since $h_0 = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} \bigg|_{t=1} = 0
$$

**Total:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{hh}} = -0.0595}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **unrolling** an RNN. With a suitable diagram, show how an RNN with $T=3$ time steps is unrolled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **vanishing and exploding gradient problems** in BPTT. Explain how **gradient clipping** and **Truncated BPTT** address these issues. Mention one architectural solution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Unrolling:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Unrolling** is the process of transforming a recurrent network (which has cyclic connections in its computational graph) into an equivalent **feedforward** network by duplicating the RNN cell once for each time step and connecting the copies in a chain.

For $T=3$, the unrolled graph has cells at $t=1, 2, 3$. Each cell uses the **same** weight matrices $\mathbf{W}_{xh}, \mathbf{W}_{hh}, \mathbf{W}_{hy}$ (parameter sharing). The hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ from one cell feeds into the next.

**Key benefits of unrolling:**
1. Converts the cyclic graph to a DAG — backpropagation is well-defined on DAGs.
2. Makes explicit the depth along the time dimension.
3. Allows reuse of standard backpropagation code.

**Equivalent Diagram:**

```
x1 → [Cell 1] → h1 → [Cell 2] → h2 → [Cell 3] → h3
        ↓                ↓                ↓
       y1               y2               y3
        ↘                ↘                ↘
                              L
```

The output of the previous cell's hidden state is the input to the next cell. All cells share the same weights.

**Part (b) — Vanishing / Exploding Gradients:**

During BPTT, the gradient involves a product of $t - k$ Jacobians:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \mathbf{D}_j \mathbf{W}_{hh}
$$

- If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) > 1$: product **explodes** exponentially → numerical overflow, oscillating loss.
- If $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$: product **vanishes** exponentially → zero gradients, no learning of long-term dependencies.

**Solution 1 — Gradient Clipping:**

Rescale the gradient vector when its norm exceeds a threshold $\theta$:

$$
\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \begin{cases} \mathbf{g} & \text{if } \Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert \le \theta \\ \dfrac{\theta}{\Vert \mathbf{g} \Vert} \mathbf{g} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This prevents the exploding case but does not fix vanishing.

**Solution 2 — Truncated BPTT:**

Limit the backward pass to a window of $\tau$ steps. The forward pass still sees the full sequence, but the backward gradient is **clipped** at depth $\tau$. This:
- Bounds computation and memory.
- Introduces a bias (gradients beyond $\tau$ are ignored).
- Does not solve vanishing — only caps the depth of the chain.

**Solution 3 (Architectural) — LSTM:**

Long Short-Term Memory cells replace the simple $\tanh$ update with a **gated mechanism** using input, forget, and output gates. The forget gate can learn to preserve gradients over long time spans (effectively setting the relevant eigenvalues of the recurrent Jacobian to ~1), directly addressing the vanishing problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student errors in KTU exams:**
> 1. Confusing **unrolling** (time-domain expansion) with **layer stacking** (depth).
> 2. Forgetting that parameter sharing means the gradient is a **sum** over time steps, not a separate parameter per step.
> 3. Stating that gradient clipping *prevents* vanishing — it only prevents *exploding*.
> 4. Not specifying that the forward pass in TBPTT is unrolled fully; only the backward pass is truncated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — BPTT**

- [x] **BPTT** = Backpropagation applied to an RNN unrolled through time.
- [x] The forward pass is defined by $\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{b}_h)$.
- [x] The output is $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_t = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{W}_{hy}\mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y)$.
- [x] **Parameter sharing** is the defining property: $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$ is the same matrix at every time step.
- [x] The hidden error signal satisfies the recursion: $\delta_t = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \mathbf{W}_{hh}^{\top} \mathbf{D}_{t+1} \delta_{t+1}$.
- [x] Weight gradient for $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$: $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}_{hh}} = \sum_t \delta_t \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\top}$.
- [x] **Vanishing gradient** arises when $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) < 1$ — product of Jacobians decays exponentially.
- [x] **Exploding gradient** arises when $\sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{W}_{hh}) > 1$ — product grows exponentially.
- [x] **Gradient clipping** addresses *exploding* gradients; **Truncated BPTT** bounds computational cost.
- [x] **LSTM/GRU** are the architectural solutions to vanishing gradients.
- [x] **Time complexity** of BPTT is $\mathcal{O}(T \cdot H^2)$; **space complexity** is $\mathcal{O}(T \cdot H)$.
- [x] In exam numericals: always compute forward pass first, store all $h_t$, then backpropagate in reverse.
- [x] A common error: forgetting the $h_0 = \mathbf{0}$ assumption (or specifying it explicitly).
- [x] BPTT unrolls the graph into a DAG — backprop on DAGs is well-defined; the cyclic form is not.
- [x] **Teacher Forcing** speeds up training by feeding ground-truth $y_{t-1}$ instead of $\hat{y}_{t-1}$.
- [x] The KTU examiner expects you to **state the assumptions** (e.g., $\tanh$ activation, MSE/cross-entropy loss) before writing equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Clipping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gradient Clipping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Gradient Clipping — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
