# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

### Q1. Define Attention Mechanism. List any two types of attention.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **Attention Mechanism** is a neural network component that dynamically assigns importance weights to different parts of the input sequence, allowing the model to focus on the most relevant information when producing an output. It is formally defined as a mapping from a Query ($Q$) and a set of Key-Value ($K, V$) pairs to a weighted output.
>
> **Two types of attention** (1 mark each):
> 1. **Self-Attention**: $Q$, $K$, $V$ are derived from the **same** sequence; used to capture intra-sequence dependencies (e.g., BERT).
> 2. **Cross-Attention**: $Q$ comes from the **decoder** while $K$ and $V$ come from the **encoder**; used in sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation.

---

### Q2. Why is the dot product scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$ in Scaled Dot-Product Attention?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> When $Q$ and $K$ have components with zero mean and unit variance, the dot product $Q \cdot K$ has variance equal to $d_k$. For large $d_k$ (e.g., 64 or 512), this makes the dot-product scores very large in magnitude, which pushes the softmax function into regions of **very small gradients** (saturation), causing slow or stalled training.
>
> Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ **restores unit variance**, keeping the softmax inputs in a numerically stable range (roughly $-3$ to $3$) and ensuring effective gradient flow during backpropagation. (1 mark for variance derivation, 2 marks for explaining gradient saturation and resolution.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the Scaled Dot-Product Attention mechanism in detail. Derive the mathematical formulation and explain each step of the computation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of **Multi-Head Attention**. How does it differ from a single attention head, and what are its advantages? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Scaled Dot-Product Attention is the standard attention function used in Transformer architectures, defined as:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

**Step-by-step explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Input projection**: Three matrices $Q$ (queries), $K$ (keys), and $V$ (values) are obtained by linearly projecting the input embeddings. Each has shape $n \times d_k$ (for $Q, K$) or $n \times d_v$ (for $V$).

2. **Score computation**: The raw compatibility scores are computed as $S = Q K^{\top}$, producing an $n \times n$ matrix where entry $S_{ij}$ indicates how much query $i$ attends to key $j$.

3. **Scaling**: The scores are divided by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to normalize the variance. As $d_k$ grows, the variance of $Q \cdot K$ grows linearly, and without scaling the softmax would saturate.

4. **Optional masking**: For causal (autoregressive) attention, a mask is applied to prevent attending to future positions. The mask sets those entries to $-\infty$ before softmax.

5. **Softmax normalization**: Applied row-wise, converting scores into a probability distribution $A$ where each row sums to 1. $A_{ij}$ represents the attention weight of position $i$ on position $j$.

6. **Weighted aggregation**: The final output is the matrix product $A V$, which is a context-aware representation where each position's output is a weighted sum of all value vectors, with weights given by the attention distribution.

**Why this works (1 mark):** The mechanism allows every position to directly access every other position in $O(1)$ path length, eliminating the sequential bottleneck of RNNs and enabling full parallelization.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Diagram (2 marks):** See Section 4.2 above for the Multi-Head Attention architecture.

**Explanation (5 marks):**

**Single-Head Attention** computes one set of attention scores. It captures only **one type of relationship** at a time (e.g., syntactic or semantic, but not both).

**Multi-Head Attention** runs $h$ independent attention heads in parallel, each operating on a different learned linear projection of $Q$, $K$, $V$:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$$

The outputs of all heads are concatenated and projected:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h) W^O$$

**Key differences and advantages (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Head | Multi-Head |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| Subspaces | 1 | $h$ parallel |
| Per-head dim | $d_{model}$ | $d_{model}/h$ |
| Total parameters | Same | Same (split across heads) |
| Relationship types captured | One | Multiple, jointly |

**Advantages of Multi-Head Attention:**
1. **Multiple representation subspaces**: Each head can learn to attend to different features (e.g., one head may focus on subject-verb agreement, another on coreference).
2. **Joint attention**: Different heads can focus on different positions simultaneously.
3. **Parameter efficiency**: Total parameter count remains the same as single-head, but representational capacity is increased.
4. **Stability**: Averaging across heads reduces the variance of attention outputs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Differentiate between **Self-Attention** and **Cross-Attention** with suitable diagrams. Give one real-world example where each is used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following input sequence of 3 tokens represented as:

$$
Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Given $d_k = 2$, compute the Scaled Dot-Product Attention output step by step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Self-Attention**: All three of $Q$, $K$, $V$ are derived from the **same** input sequence. It allows each position in the sequence to look at all other positions in the same sequence to build a contextualized representation.
- **Cross-Attention**: $Q$ comes from one source (typically the decoder), while $K$ and $V$ come from a **different** source (typically the encoder). It allows one sequence to attend to another.

**Diagrams (2 marks):** See Section 4.3 above for the comparative dataflow.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Source of $Q$ | Same sequence | Different sequence (e.g., decoder) |
| Source of $K, V$ | Same sequence | Different sequence (e.g., encoder) |
| Purpose | Build contextual understanding within a sequence | Align two different sequences |
| Used in | BERT, GPT, ViT (encoder) | Encoder-decoder Transformers, DETR |

**Real-world examples (1 mark):**
- **Self-Attention**: BERT for masked language modeling, where the model predicts a masked word by attending to all surrounding words in the same sentence.
- **Cross-Attention**: Machine translation (English → Malayalam), where the decoder attends to the encoded English source to generate the Malayalam target word.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Given data (1 mark):**
- $n = 2$ tokens, $d_k = 2$, $d_v = 2$.
- $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $V = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$

**Step 1: Compute $S = Q K^{\top}$ (1 mark)**

$$Q K^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Apply scaling (1 mark)**

$$S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{1.414} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 1.414 \\ 0 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Apply softmax row-wise (2 marks)**

**Row 1**: $\text{softmax}([0.707, 1.414])$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{0.707} &\approx 2.028 \\
e^{1.414} &\approx 4.113 \\
\text{Sum} &= 6.141 \\
A_1 &= [0.330, 0.670]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row 2**: $\text{softmax}([0, 0.707])$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{0} &= 1.000 \\
e^{0.707} &\approx 2.028 \\
\text{Sum} &= 3.028 \\
A_2 &= [0.330, 0.670]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.330 & 0.670 \\ 0.330 & 0.670 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute output $A V$ (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.330 & 0.670 \\ 0.330 & 0.670 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (0.330)(2) + (0.670)(0) & (0.330)(0) + (0.670)(2) \\ (0.330)(2) + (0.670)(0) & (0.330)(0) + (0.670)(2) \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.660 & 1.340 \\ 0.660 & 1.340 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):**

$$\text{Output} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.660 & 1.340 \\ 0.660 & 1.340 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor** — A very common error. Always show the scaling step explicitly, even if the question only asks for "dot-product attention." Examiners award marks for showing $\sqrt{d_k}$ in the denominator.
> 2. **Confusing self-attention with cross-attention** — In self-attention, all three matrices $Q$, $K$, $V$ come from the **same** source. In cross-attention, $Q$ comes from one source and $K, V$ from another. Do not mix this up.
> 3. **Skipping the softmax step** — Students often compute $QK^{\top}$ and jump directly to multiplication with $V$, forgetting the row-wise softmax normalization. This is a critical step that converts raw scores into a probability distribution.
> 4. **Mis-stating multi-head dimensions** — Each head operates on dimension $d_{model}/h$, **not** $d_{model}$. Writing $d_k = d_{model}$ for a multi-head setup loses a mark.
> 5. **Forgetting masking in causal/decoder attention** — When asked about decoder attention, the masking step must be explicitly mentioned and explained; otherwise, full marks will not be awarded.
> 6. **Dimension mismatches in matrix multiplication** — Always verify shapes: $Q K^{\top}$ must yield an $n \times n$ matrix. If dimensions do not match, the computation is wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Attention Mechanism**

- **Core formula**: $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$
- **Three matrices**: Query (what we want), Key (what we contain), Value (what we deliver).
- **Scalling factor**: $\sqrt{d_k}$ — always present; restores unit variance.
- **Softmax** is applied **row-wise** on the $n \times n$ score matrix.
- **Self-Attention** = $Q, K, V$ from same source. **Cross-Attention** = $Q$ from decoder, $K, V$ from encoder.
- **Multi-Head** = $h$ parallel heads, each on a $d_{model}/h$ dimensional subspace, outputs concatenated and projected.
- **Per-head dim**: $d_k = d_v = d_{model}/h$ (typical: $d_{model} = 512$, $h = 8$, so $d_k = 64$).
- **Complexity**: $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ for standard attention — quadratic in sequence length.
- **Masking**: Decoder uses **causal (look-ahead) masking** to prevent attending to future tokens.
- **Add & Norm**: Every attention sub-layer in a Transformer is followed by a **residual connection** and **layer normalization**.
- **Positional encoding**: Must be added to input embeddings **before** attention, since attention is permutation-equivariant.
- **Variants to know**: Bahdanau (additive), Luong (multiplicative), Scaled Dot-Product, Multi-Head, Sparse, Linear.
- **Applications**: NLP (BERT, GPT), CV (ViT, DETR), Speech (Whisper), Biology (AlphaFold 2), Multimodal (CLIP).
- **Hyperparameters (Transformer base)**: $d_{model} = 512$, $h = 8$, $d_k = d_v = 64$, $d_{ff} = 2048$, $N = 6$ layers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) Control** is a foundational, industry-standard **closed-loop feedback control mechanism** that continuously computes a corrective control action by aggregating three independent error-based terms. The controller minimizes the instantaneous error $e(t)$, defined as the difference between a desired reference setpoint $r(t)$ and the measured process variable $y(t)$.

Mathematically, the continuous-time PID control law in its standard parallel (non-interacting) form is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ is the controller output (actuating signal)
- $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are the **tunable gain coefficients**
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ is the **tracking error**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PID control sits at the intersection of classical control theory and modern Artificial Intelligence. It serves as a baseline benchmark for evaluating advanced AI-based controllers such as Neural Network Controllers, Fuzzy Logic Controllers, and Reinforcement Learning Agents.

## 1.2 The Three Control Actions — Conceptual Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Analogy — The "Driving a Car" Metaphor**

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a highway with gentle slopes.

| Term | Real-world Driving Analogy | Mathematical Behavior |
|------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| **P (Proportional)** | The harder you press the accelerator the more you are *behind* the target speed. | Reacts to **present** error magnitude. |
| **I (Integral)** | You remember that earlier a wind gust slowed you, so you press the accelerator a bit extra to compensate. | Reacts to **accumulated past** errors. |
| **D (Derivative)** | Anticipating an upcoming hill, you ease off the accelerator *before* the speed actually drops. | Reacts to the **future trend** of error. |

The brilliance of PID lies in blending these three human-like reasoning capabilities — **present observation, past memory, and future prediction** — into one elegant, mathematically tractable equation.

> [!TIP]
> In modern AI/ML, this same three-way decomposition inspires **Actor-Critic** architectures where the Critic (I-term memory) and the policy gradient (D-term anticipation) complement a base reactive policy (P-term).

## 1.3 Standard Error Definition and Control Objective

The control objective is to drive $e(t) \rightarrow 0$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$, ensuring the plant output $y(t)$ asymptotically tracks the setpoint $r(t)$. The **stability**, **accuracy**, and **speed** of this tracking depend entirely on the choice of $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$.

A more robust variant replaces the error on the derivative term with the derivative of the **process variable** (to avoid "derivative kick" on setpoint changes):

$$u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau - K_{d}\,\frac{dy(t)}{dt}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-by-Component Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Action
- **Operational Logic:** The control output is directly proportional to the instantaneous tracking error.
- **Mathematical Form:** $P_{out}(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$
- **Effect on the System:**
  * Increases the system's **loop gain**, which in turn reduces the **rise time** $t_r$.
  * Decreases (but cannot fully eliminate) the **steady-state error** $e_{ss}$.
  * Too large a $K_p$ leads to **oscillations** and potentially **instability**.
- **Limitation:** A pure P-controller always leaves a residual steady-state error (called *offset*) for a step input in plants without an integrator.

### 2.1.2 Integral (I) Action
- **Operational Logic:** The control output is proportional to the **time-integral (accumulated history)** of the error.
- **Mathematical Form:** $I_{out}(t) = K_i \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau$
- **Effect on the System:**
  * Drives the **steady-state error to zero** for step inputs — this is the integral action's signature property.
  * Introduces a **pole at the origin** in the open-loop transfer function, elevating the system type by 1.
  * Increases **overshoot** and prolongs **settling time** $t_s$ if tuned aggressively.
- **Limitation:** Integral windup — a phenomenon where the integrator accumulates large errors during actuator saturation, causing severe overshoot when saturation ends.

### 2.1.3 Derivative (D) Action
- **Operational Logic:** The control output is proportional to the **rate of change (future trend)** of the error.
- **Mathematical Form:** $D_{out}(t) = K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$
- **Effect on the System:**
  * Adds **phase lead** to the system, increasing **phase margin** and improving **transient stability**.
  * **Damps oscillations** and reduces **overshoot**.
  * Sensitive to **measurement noise** since differentiation amplifies high-frequency components.
- **Limitation:** The pure derivative term is **non-causal** in continuous ideal form and is often implemented as a filtered derivative in practice.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Continuous-time PID control law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Generates actuator command |
| 2 | Tracking error definition | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Foundation of all feedback terms |
| 3 | Laplace-domain PID controller | $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Root-locus and Bode analysis |
| 4 | Time-domain integral windup condition | $\int e\,dt > \int e_{max}\,dt$ | Anti-windup threshold detection |
| 5 | Ziegler-Nichols ultimate gain | $K_u = \dfrac{1}{G(j\omega_u)\vert}$ | Closed-loop tuning benchmark |
| 6 | Z-N ultimate period | $T_u = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_u}$ | Period of sustained oscillation |
| 7 | Z-N tuned P-only gain | $K_p = 0.5\,K_u$ | Conservative proportional setting |
| 8 | Z-N tuned PID gains (1st method) | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ | Quarter-decay ratio response |
| 9 | Derivative filter (practical D) | $D_{filt}(s) = \dfrac{K_d s}{\tau_f s + 1}$ | Noise rejection in real D action |
| 10 | Discrete-time PID (positional) | $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p(e[k]-e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k] + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s}(e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2])$ | Digital implementation |
| 11 | Steady-state error of P-only | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{A}{1+K_p K_{dc}}$ | Quantifies offset |
| 12 | Integrator zero in transfer form | $C(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | Reveals closed-loop pole structure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on LaTeX rendering:** The absolute value notation $\vert G(j\omega_u) \vert$ uses `\vert` (not the pipe character) to preserve the markdown table's structural integrity.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

PID controllers are the **silent workhorses** behind:
- **Robotics:** Joint position and velocity control in robotic arms.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Steering, throttle, and brake control loops.
- **Process Industries:** Temperature, pressure, flow, and level control in chemical plants.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surfaces, altitude hold, and autopilot systems.
- **AI Integration:** PID serves as the **classical baseline** against which modern Deep Reinforcement Learning and Model Predictive Control (MPC) algorithms are benchmarked.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Time-Domain to Laplace-Domain PID Transfer Function

We begin with the standard parallel-form continuous-time PID equation:

$$u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace Transform to the Proportional Term.**
The Laplace transform of a constant times a function is the constant times the transform:

$$\mathcal{L}\{K_p\,e(t)\} = K_p\,E(s)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Laplace Transform to the Integral Term.**
Using the standard result $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau)d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau\right\} = \frac{K_i}{s}\,E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Laplace Transform to the Derivative Term.**
Using $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = sE(s) - e(0^-)$. Assuming zero initial conditions $e(0^-) = 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{K_d\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = K_d\,s\,E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Three Transformed Terms.**

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s}E(s) + K_d s E(s)$$

**Step 5 — Factor Out $E(s)$ to Form the Controller Transfer Function.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\;}$$

**Step 6 — Place Under a Common Denominator to Reveal Pole-Zero Structure.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C(s) &= \frac{K_p s + K_i + K_d s^2}{s} \\
     &= \frac{K_d\left(s^2 + \frac{K_p}{K_d}s + \frac{K_i}{K_d}\right)}{s}
\end{aligned}$$

This derivation is critical for KTU board examinations because it exposes the fact that a PID controller adds **one pole at the origin** and **two zeros** in the left half-plane, fundamentally altering the closed-loop system's stability and transient response characteristics.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Steady-State Error of a Type-1 Plant with PID

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Consider a unity-feedback control system where the plant is $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+5)}$. A PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ is placed in the forward path. Determine the steady-state error for a unit step input $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ when the gains are chosen as $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 5$, $K_d = 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Open-Loop Transfer Function $L(s)$.**

$$L(s) = C(s) \cdot G(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right)\cdot\frac{10}{s(s+5)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Numerical Values.**

$$L(s) = \left(2 + \frac{5}{s} + 0.5 s\right)\cdot\frac{10}{s(s+5)}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the Controller into a Single Fraction.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C(s) &= \frac{0.5 s^2 + 2 s + 5}{s} \\
L(s) &= \frac{(0.5 s^2 + 2 s + 5)}{s} \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+5)} \\
L(s) &= \frac{10(0.5 s^2 + 2 s + 5)}{s^2(s+5)} \\
L(s) &= \frac{5 s^2 + 20 s + 50}{s^2(s+5)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Position Error Constant $K_p^{ess}$.**

The error constant is the limit of $L(s)$ as $s \to 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_p^{ess} &= \lim_{s \to 0} L(s) \\
          &= \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{5 s^2 + 20 s + 50}{s^2(s+5)} \\
          &= \frac{0 + 0 + 50}{0 \cdot 5} \\
          &= \infty
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Final Value Theorem for Steady-State Error.**

The steady-state error for a unit step is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p^{ess}} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Interpretation:** The integral term $\dfrac{K_i}{s}$ elevated the system type from 1 to **Type-2** (two poles at the origin), guaranteeing **zero steady-state error** for step inputs — a hallmark property that makes PID indispensable in precision control.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ziegler-Nichols First Method (Open-Loop Reaction Curve)

This method uses the plant's **open-loop step response** characterized by three parameters: delay time $L$, time constant $T$, and process gain $K$.

**Step 1 — Obtain the S-shaped Reaction Curve.**
Apply a unit step to the open-loop plant and record the output. The S-curve is characterized by the point of inflection.

**Step 2 — Draw the Tangent at the Inflection Point.**
Identify where the tangent line crosses the time axis (giving $L$) and where it reaches the final steady-state value (giving $L + T$).

**Step 3 — Compute the Steady-State Process Gain $K$.**

$$K = \frac{\Delta y_{ss}}{\Delta u_{applied}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Z-N Tuning Table for a Quarter-Decay Ratio Response.**

| Controller Type | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|-----------------|-------|-------|-------|
| P | $\dfrac{T}{K L}$ | — | — |
| PI | $\dfrac{0.9 T}{K L}$ | $3L$ | — |
| PID | $\dfrac{1.2 T}{K L}$ | $2L$ | $0.5 L$ |

**Step 5 — Convert the Time-Based Parameters to Gains.**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}, \qquad K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$$

This derivation enables KTU students to perform complete open-loop tuning in real lab environments, particularly relevant for the AI/ML laboratory modules where classical control serves as a baseline.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Closed-Loop PID Control System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR[Reference Setpoint r of t] --> nodeSum[ Summing Junction ]
    nodeN[Noise Disturbance] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeE[Error Signal e of t]
    nodeE --> nodeP[Proportional Block Kp]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Integral Block Ki over s]
    nodeE --> nodeD[Derivative Block Kd times s]
    nodeP --> nodeAdd[ Summation of PID Terms ]
    nodeI --> nodeAdd
    nodeD --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeU[Controller Output u of t]
    nodeU --> nodePlant[Plant or Process G of s]
    nodePlant --> nodeY[Process Output y of t]
    nodeY --> nodeFb[Feedback Path]
    nodeFb --> nodeSum
    nodeY --> nodeOut[Measured Response]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Note:** The negative sign at the feedback path implicitly appears in the summing junction convention used in KTU textbooks. The noise block represents real-world measurement disturbances that make the D-term's noise sensitivity a critical concern.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Time-Domain Step Response of a PID-Tuned System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart[Step Input Applied] --> nodeRise[Rise Time tr]
    nodeRise --> nodePeak[First Peak]
    nodePeak --> nodeOS[Overshoot Mp percent]
    nodeOS --> nodeDecay[Oscillatory Decay]
    nodeDecay --> nodeSettle[Settling Time ts within 2 percent]
    nodeSettle --> nodeFinal[Steady State Value y final]
    nodeFinal --> nodeTune[Tuning Objective Achieved]
    nodeOS --> nodeErr1[Kp increases Mp and tr]
    nodeDecay --> nodeErr2[Ki eliminates steady state error]
    nodeSettle --> nodeErr3[Kd reduces ts and Mp]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** This diagram maps the three PID terms to the three most important time-domain performance metrics. This mapping is a **favorite KTU 14-mark question** pattern — students are often asked to explain *which gain affects which performance parameter*.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: AI/ML Augmentation of Classical PID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePID[Classical PID Controller] --> nodeBase[Baseline Performance]
    nodeBase --> nodeRL[Reinforcement Learning Agent]
    nodeBase --> nodeFLC[Fuzzy Logic Controller]
    nodeBase --> nodeNN[Neural Network Controller]
    nodeRL --> nodeAdapt[Adaptive Gain Tuning Kp Ki Kd]
    nodeFLC --> nodeAdapt
    nodeNN --> nodeAdapt
    nodeAdapt --> nodePID
    nodePlant[Plant G of s] --> nodeSensor[Sensor Measurement y of t]
    nodeSensor --> nodePID
    nodeSensor --> nodeRL
    nodeSensor --> nodeFLC
    nodeSensor --> nodeNN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Topic Mapping:** This figure illustrates how the PCRAT502 syllabus positions PID within the broader AI/ML landscape — not as a relic, but as the **interpretable, mathematically tractable foundation** upon which intelligent adaptive control is built.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define a PID controller. List the effect of each of the three terms — Proportional, Integral, and Derivative — on the steady-state error of a unity feedback control system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is a feedback control mechanism that computes the control signal $u(t)$ as a weighted sum of the present error, the integral of past errors, and the derivative of the current error. The governing equation is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Effects on Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):**
- **Proportional term ($K_p$):** **Reduces** but does **not eliminate** the steady-state error. The residual offset is $e_{ss} = \dfrac{A}{1+K_p K_{dc}}$.
- **Integral term ($K_i$):** **Eliminates** the steady-state error for step inputs by accumulating past error until it becomes zero.
- **Derivative term ($K_d$):** Has **no direct effect** on steady-state error. It influences only the transient response.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is integral windup? State two methods to prevent it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Integral windup** is a phenomenon in PID controllers where, during actuator saturation, the integrator term continues to accumulate error even though the actuator is already at its physical limit. When the error eventually changes sign, the controller takes time to "unwind" the accumulated integral, causing large overshoot and poor transient response.

**Two Prevention Methods:**
1. **Clamping (Conditional Integration):** Halt the integration process when the actuator output reaches its saturation limits.
2. **Back-calculation:** Compute the difference between the saturated and unsaturated control output and feed it back to the integrator at a rate $1/T_t$ (tracking time constant) to discharge the excess.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Derive the transfer function of a standard parallel-form PID controller. Explain how each gain influences the rise time, settling time, and overshoot of a closed-loop step response. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Starting with the Time-Domain Equation.**

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Laplace Transform Under Zero Initial Conditions.**

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s}E(s) + K_d s E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Form the Transfer Function.**

$$\frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

**Step 4 — Combined Polynomial Form.**

$$C(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Influence on Transient Response:**

| Gain | Rise Time $t_r$ | Settling Time $t_s$ | Overshoot $M_p$ |
|------|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Increase $K_p$ | **Decreases** | Small increase | **Increases** |
| Increase $K_i$ | Decreases | **Increases** | **Increases** |
| Increase $K_d$ | Minor change | **Decreases** | **Decreases** |

**Step 5 — Architectural Insight.**
The PID controller introduces one pole at the origin and two zeros. The two zeros are placed in the left half-plane to provide a phase-lead action that compensates for the plant's phase lag, dramatically improving both stability margins and closed-loop bandwidth.

---

**b) A unity feedback system has a plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$. A PID controller with gains $K_p = 10$, $K_i = 5$, $K_d = 2$ is connected in cascade. Compute the open-loop transfer function and determine the steady-state error for a unit ramp input. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Form the PID Controller Transfer Function.**

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = 10 + \frac{5}{s} + 2s$$

**Step 2 — Combine into a Single Fraction.**

$$C(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 10s + 5}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Open-Loop Transfer Function $L(s) = C(s)G(s)$.**

$$L(s) = \frac{2s^2 + 10s + 5}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{2s^2 + 10s + 5}{s^2(s+1)(s+4)}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the System Type.**
The open-loop transfer function has **two poles at the origin** ($s^2$ in the denominator), making it a **Type-2 system**.

**Step 5 — Compute the Velocity Error Constant $K_v$.**

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s L(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{2s^2 + 10s + 5}{s^2(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{0 + 0 + 5}{0 \cdot 1 \cdot 4} = \infty$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Ramp Steady-State Error Formula.**

For a unit ramp input, the steady-state error is:

$$e_{ss}^{ramp} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Concluding Remark.**
A Type-2 system tracks a **unit ramp** with **zero steady-state error**, demonstrating the powerful steady-state accuracy enhancement that the integral term in a PID controller provides over a PI or P-only controller.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a) Explain the Ziegler-Nichols ultimate cycle (closed-loop) tuning method for a PID controller. The ultimate gain is found to be $K_u = 8$ and the ultimate period is $T_u = 4$ seconds. Compute the recommended PID gains. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Conceptual Overview of the Method.**
The Ziegler-Nichols **ultimate cycle** method uses a **closed-loop experiment** with a pure P-controller. The proportional gain is increased until the system exhibits **sustained, constant-amplitude oscillations**. The gain at this point is the **ultimate gain $K_u$** and the period of oscillation is the **ultimate period $T_u$**.

**Step 2 — Apply the Z-N PID Tuning Formulas.**

$$K_p = 0.6 \, K_u, \qquad T_i = 0.5 \, T_u, \qquad T_d = 0.125 \, T_u$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Given Values.**

$$K_p = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \times 4 = 2 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \times 4 = 0.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Convert the Time Constants to PID Gains.**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{2} = 2.4$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 4.8 \times 0.5 = 2.4$$

**Step 5 — Final Tuned Controller.**

$$\boxed{u(t) = 4.8\,e(t) + 2.4\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + 2.4\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Caveat:** Z-N tuning targets a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio** — it is often **aggressive** and may need subsequent fine-tuning for AI/ML applications where smoother trajectories are preferred.

---

**b) For a first-order plant with time delay $G(s) = \dfrac{K\,e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1}$, where $K = 2$, $T = 3$ s, and $L = 1$ s, use the Ziegler-Nichols **reaction curve (open-loop)** method to determine the PID controller gains. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the Z-N First Method Formulas.**

$$K_p = \frac{1.2\,T}{K\,L}, \qquad T_i = 2L, \qquad T_d = 0.5L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Given Parameter Values.**

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \times 3}{2 \times 1} = \frac{3.6}{2} = 1.8$$

$$T_i = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_d = 0.5 \times 1 = 0.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to $K_i$ and $K_d$.**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{1.8}{2} = 0.9$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 1.8 \times 0.5 = 0.9$$

**Step 4 — Final Tuned Controller Expression.**

$$\boxed{u(t) = 1.8\,e(t) + 0.9\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + 0.9\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}}$$

**Step 5 — Practical Implementation Note.**
The derivative term is normally implemented with a **first-order low-pass filter** $D_{filt}(s) = \dfrac{K_d s}{\tau_f s + 1}$ with $\tau_f \approx 0.1 T_d$ to prevent amplification of high-frequency measurement noise — a critical concern highlighted in the KTU 2024 control systems laboratory manual.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PID Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** in the derivative-on-measurement variant — students often lose **1 to 2 marks** by writing $+K_d\dot{y}(t)$ instead of $-K_d\dot{y}(t)$.
> 2. **Skipping the system-type analysis** before computing the steady-state error — without identifying the system type, the wrong error formula is applied, leading to **full-mark loss** on the 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Not converting** $T_i$ and $T_d$ into $K_i$ and $K_d$ — many students leave the tuning answer in the time-constant form, which the KTU valuation key marks as **incomplete (–1 mark)**.
> 4. **Confusing the two Z-N methods** — the reaction-curve method uses plant step-response parameters $(L, T, K)$, while the ultimate cycle method uses closed-loop $(K_u, T_u)$. Mixing them is a **fatal error**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PID Core Law:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ — commit to memory.
- **Laplace-Domain Transfer Function:** $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$.
- **PID adds one pole at origin and two left-half-plane zeros** — this is the structural reason for its success.
- **P-term** reduces rise time but cannot eliminate $e_{ss}$.
- **I-term** eliminates $e_{ss}$ for step inputs but causes overshoot and integral windup.
- **D-term** adds phase lead, dampens oscillations, and reduces overshoot — but amplifies noise.
- **Z-N Ultimate Cycle:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- **Z-N Reaction Curve:** $K_p = 1.2 T / (KL)$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = 0.5L$.
- **Type-N System Rule:** An $N$-th order pole at the origin guarantees zero $e_{ss}$ for inputs up to order $N-1$.
- **Anti-windup techniques** — clamping, back-calculation, conditional integration.
- **Discrete-Time Implementation:** Use the velocity (incremental) form to avoid setpoint derivative kick.
- **AI/ML Connection:** PID is the **classical benchmark** against which Fuzzy, Neural, RL, and MPC controllers are validated in advanced control research.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuning PID Gains for Optimal Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tuning PID Gains for Optimal Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PID Tuning** is the process of selecting the optimal proportional ($K_p$), integral ($K_i$), and derivative ($K_d$) gain parameters of a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller to achieve a desired closed-loop dynamic response. In the context of **Advanced AI & ML (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCRAT502)**, the term extends beyond classical Ziegler-Nichols heuristics to encompass **data-driven, optimization-based, and learning-based gain synthesis**, where techniques such as gradient descent, evolutionary algorithms, Bayesian optimization, and reinforcement learning are deployed to minimize a user-defined performance index (e.g., Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error, ITAE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** PID tuning under Module 3 bridges *Classical Control Theory* with *Computational Intelligence*. Students must demonstrate competence in both heuristic tuning (Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon) and ML-driven tuning (ANN-based adaptive PID, RL-based tuning, PSO/GA optimization).

### 1.2 The PID Control Law

The continuous-time PID controller output is mathematically expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ is the tracking error between the reference $r(t)$ and the measured plant output $y(t)$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control Meets Driving Instructor" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Learning to Drive a Car with Three Instructors**
> - **Proportional ($K_p$) — The "Reactive" Instructor:** "How far are you from the correct lane position right now?" A reactive instructor pulls harder the further you drift. Too strong ($K_p$ too high) → you overcorrect and oscillate (weaving across lanes).
> - **Integral ($K_i$) — The "Patient" Instructor:** "For how long have you been off-target on average?" This eliminates persistent bias (steady-state error) by remembering past mistakes. Too strong → the car becomes sluggish and overshoots badly.
> - **Derivative ($K_d$) — The "Predictive" Instructor:** "How fast is your error changing?" This anticipates future error and applies damping. Too strong → the system becomes twitchy and amplifies noise.
> - **Optimal Tuning** = finding the right *balance of personalities* (gains) for a *specific* car (plant) and *road conditions* (disturbances). ML-driven tuning automates this balance by learning from telemetry data.

### 1.4 Why Optimality Matters

The term **"Optimal Performance"** refers to minimizing a cost function $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ where $\boldsymbol{\theta} = [K_p, K_i, K_d]^T$. Common KTU-recognized objectives include:

- **ITAE:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} t \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ — penalizes long-duration errors
- **IAE:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ — total absolute error
- **ISE:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^2(t) \, dt$ — penalizes large errors heavily
- **ITSE:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} t \, e^2(t) \, dt$ — penalizes long-duration large errors
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** typically constrained $\le 5\%$ to $10\%$
- **Settling time ($t_s$):** within $2\%$ to $5\%$ tolerance band

> [!NOTE]
> The gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are typically **dimensionless controller parameters** (after internal scaling), or carry units of $[1]$, $[s^{-1}]$, and $[s]$ respectively in a parallel-form PID. The **standard metrics** of optimality are **rise time ($t_r$)**, **overshoot ($M_p$)**, **settling time ($t_s$)**, and **steady-state error ($e_{ss}$)**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Gain Decoupling — Step-by-Step Role Analysis

**Step 1 — Proportional Action (Present Error Correction)**
- Mechanism: $u_p(t) = K_p e(t)$
- Effect: Increases **loop bandwidth**, reduces **rise time** $t_r$, and improves **reaction speed**.
- Trade-off: Excessive $K_p$ → **oscillations, reduced phase margin**, possible instability.
- Mathematical role: Multiplies the error by a constant gain.

**Step 2 — Integral Action (Historical Error Elimination)**
- Mechanism: $u_i(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$
- Effect: Drives **steady-state error** $e_{ss} \to 0$ for step inputs; improves **low-frequency disturbance rejection**.
- Trade-off: Excessive $K_i$ → **integrator windup**, overshoot, slow recovery.
- Mathematical role: Adds a pole at origin ($s=0$) to the open-loop transfer function.

**Step 3 — Derivative Action (Predictive Damping)**
- Mechanism: $u_d(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$
- Effect: Adds **phase lead**, improving **transient response** and **damping ratio** $\zeta$.
- Trade-off: Sensitive to measurement **noise**; never used alone.
- Mathematical role: Adds a zero that anticipates error growth.

### 2.2 The Open-Loop Transfer Function of a Unity-Feedback PID-Controlled Plant

For a plant $G_p(s)$ and PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$, the loop transfer function is:

$$L(s) = C(s) G_p(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) G_p(s)$$

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}$$

Stability is determined by the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** on the characteristic polynomial $1 + C(s) G_p(s) = 0$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Physical Meaning / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Controller output signal |
| Laplace Domain PID | $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Transfer function form |
| Steady-State Error (Step) | $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$ (approx. for unity) | Bias offset to step input |
| Overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage peak overshoot |
| Settling Time (2% band) | $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Time to settle within tolerance |
| Rise Time | $t_r \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | 10%–90% rise metric |
| ZN Ultimate Gain | $K_u$ = gain at sustained oscillation | Critical proportional gain |
| ZN Ultimate Period | $T_u$ = oscillation period at $K_u$ | Period of marginal oscillation |
| ZN-PID Formula (Classic) | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ | Ziegler-Nichols PID rules |
| ITAE Index | $J = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \mid e(t) \mid \, dt$ | Optimization cost function |
| Cohen-Coon (FOPDT) | Uses $K, L, T$ parameters | Open-loop step response tuning |
| Routh Stability | All first column $\gt 0$ | Necessary & sufficient stability |
| Phase Margin | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle L(j\omega_{gc})$ | Robustness indicator |
| Gain Margin | $GM = \frac{1}{\mid L(j\omega_{pc}) \mid}$ | Robustness indicator |
| ANN-PID Update Rule | $\Delta K = -\eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial K}$ | Gradient-based ML tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Quick Reference:** $K_p$ is dimensionless (or matches actuator units), $K_i$ has units of $s^{-1}$ (controller output per error-second), and $K_d$ has units of $s$. Time constants $T_i = K_p/K_i$ and $T_d = K_d/K_p$ are expressed in **seconds**.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** Joint position and torque control in manipulators.
- **Process Control:** Temperature, pressure, flow, and level regulation in chemical plants.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surfaces, autopilot systems, quadcopter attitude control.
- **Automotive:** Cruise control, lane-keeping, anti-lock braking (ABS), EV battery thermal management.
- **ML-Cyber-Physical Systems:** Reinforcement learning agents tuning PID gains in real-time for non-stationary plants.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a First-Order Plus Dead-Time (FOPDT) Plant with PID

**Starting Plant Model (FOPDT):**
A majority of industrial processes can be approximated by the First-Order Plus Dead-Time (FOPDT) model used in Ziegler-Nichols and Cohen-Coon tuning:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K \cdot e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the PID Controller Form**

Using the parallel PID $C(s) = K_p\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ where $K_i = K_p/T_i$ and $K_d = K_p T_d$:

$$C(s) = \frac{K_p(T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1)}{T_i s}$$

**Step 2 — Form the Loop Transfer Function**

$$L(s) = C(s) G_p(s) = \frac{K K_p (T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1) e^{-Ls}}{T_i s (Ts + 1)}$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the Dead-Time via First-Order Pade Approximation**

For analytical tractability in the KTU exam context, replace $e^{-Ls} \approx \frac{1 - Ls/2}{1 + Ls/2}$:

$$L(s) \approx \frac{K K_p (T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1)(1 - Ls/2)}{T_i s (Ts + 1)(1 + Ls/2)}$$

**Step 4 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function**

$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$$

where $D(s) = T_i s (Ts + 1)(1 + Ls/2) + K K_p (T_i T_d s^2 + T_i s + 1)(1 - Ls/2)$.

**Step 5 — Stability via Routh-Hurwitz**

For the simplified third-order characteristic polynomial (after collecting terms and assuming $T_d$ small), the necessary stability conditions yield:

$$K_p \lt \frac{T_i(T + L/2)}{K L (T_i + T_d - L/2)}$$

This inequality defines the **stability envelope** for the proportional gain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Cycle (Closed-Loop) Tuning Rules

**Step 1 — Set the Controller to Proportional-Only Mode**

Disable integral and derivative: $K_i = 0$, $K_d = 0$. Use $C(s) = K_p$.

**Step 2 — Increase $K_p$ Until Sustained Oscillation Occurs**

Gradually increase $K_p$ from a small value. At some critical value $K_p = K_u$, the closed-loop output $y(t)$ exhibits **sustained sinusoidal oscillations** with constant amplitude. The closed-loop poles lie exactly on the imaginary axis $s = \pm j \omega_u$.

**Step 3 — Measure the Ultimate Period $T_u$**

The oscillation period $T_u$ (in seconds) is recorded:

$$T_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ZN Empirical Formulas**

| **Controller Type** | **$K_p$** | **$T_i$** | **$T_d$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| P (Proportional) | $0.5 K_u$ | — | — |
| PI (Proportional-Integral) | $0.45 K_u$ | $T_u/1.2$ | — |
| PID (Classic) | $0.60 K_u$ | $0.5 T_u$ | $0.125 T_u$ |
| PID (Some Overshoot) | $0.33 K_u$ | $0.5 T_u$ | $0.33 T_u$ |
| PID (No Overshoot) | $0.20 K_u$ | $0.5 T_u$ | $0.33 T_u$ |

**Step 5 — Convert Time Constants Back to Gain Form**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}, \quad K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Gradient-Based ML Tuning of PID Gains

In the **AI-driven** paradigm, we treat PID gains as learnable parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} = [K_p, K_i, K_d]^T$ and minimize a cost function $J$ over a trajectory of length $N$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ e(k)^2 + \lambda_u \, \Delta u(k)^2 \right]$$

where $\lambda_u$ penalizes excessive control effort.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ e(k) \frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} + \lambda_u \, \Delta u(k) \frac{\partial \Delta u(k)}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Chain Rule Through the Plant Dynamics**

For a discrete plant $y(k) = f(y(k-1), u(k-1))$, the error gradient back-propagates through the plant dynamics. This is the **Back-Propagation Through Time (BPTT)** approach for PID:

$$\frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = -\frac{\partial y(k)}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = -\frac{\partial f}{\partial u} \cdot \frac{\partial u(k)}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

**Step 3 — Update Rule (Stochastic Gradient Descent)**

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}_{n+1} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_n - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (analogous to a "tuning speed" parameter). Common $\eta$ ranges: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$.

**Step 4 — Convergence Criterion**

Stop when $\vert \Delta \boldsymbol{\theta} \vert \lt \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$) or when $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ plateaus.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Classical vs. ML-Based PID Tuning

| **Aspect** | **Classical (ZN, Cohen-Coon)** | **ML / Optimization-Based** |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge of Plant | Required (FOPDT parameters $K, L, T$) | Learned from I/O data |
| Convergence Speed | Single-step heuristic | Iterative, may take minutes/hours |
| Optimality | Empirical, ~25% overshoot typical | Near-optimal w.r.t. chosen $J$ |
| Robustness | Depends on model accuracy | Better with regularization |
| Computational Cost | Negligible | High (training phase) |
| Real-Time Adaptation | Not possible natively | Possible via online learning |
| Noise Sensitivity | Low | Depends on data quality |
| Exam-Relevance (KTU) | High — must know formulas | High — explain gradient logic |
