## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the PID control law and explain the role of the derivative term $K_d$ in improving transient response. Why is a pure derivative controller never used in practice?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The PID control law in the time domain is:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**(1 mark)** The **derivative term** $K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ is proportional to the rate of change of error. It provides **predictive damping** by adding phase lead to the open-loop transfer function, thereby increasing the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ and reducing **overshoot $M_p$** and **settling time $t_s$**.

**(1 mark)** Mathematically, the derivative term adds a zero to $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$, shifting the root locus leftward in the s-plane.

**(1 mark)** A **pure derivative controller** is never used because (a) it produces zero output for constant or slowly-varying error, (b) it amplifies high-frequency **measurement noise** (since $\frac{d}{dt}[\text{noise}] \to \infty$), and (c) it is **non-causal** in ideal form (requires future knowledge of error).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the Ziegler-Nichols tuning formulas for a PID controller based on the ultimate cycle method. State one limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(2 marks)** The ZN ultimate-cycle formulas for a classic PID controller are:
$$K_p = 0.6 K_u, \quad T_i = 0.5 T_u, \quad T_d = 0.125 T_u$$

where $K_u$ is the **ultimate gain** (critical gain producing sustained oscillation) and $T_u$ is the **ultimate period** of oscillation. Converting to gain form: $K_i = K_p / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$.

**(1 mark)** **Limitation:** ZN tuning typically yields a response with **~25% overshoot**, which is unacceptable for many precision applications. Furthermore, the method requires driving the plant to **marginal instability**, which may be unsafe or impractical for real industrial systems.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024]**

**For a unity-feedback control system with plant $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$:**

**(a)** Design a PID controller using the Ziegler-Nichols ultimate cycle method. Compute the optimal gains $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the gradient-descent-based ML approach to PID tuning, including the cost function and update rule. Compare its performance with the ZN method. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**(a) Model Solution — ZN Ultimate Cycle Method (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Disable I and D action. The closed-loop characteristic equation with $C(s) = K_p$ is:
$$1 + \frac{K_p}{s(s+1)(s+5)} = 0 \implies s^3 + 6s^2 + 5s + K_p = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** to find $K_u$:

$$\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & 5 \\
s^2 & 6 & K_p \\
s^1 & (30 - K_p)/6 & 0 \\
s^0 & K_p & 
\end{array}$$

For marginal stability, the $s^1$ row must equal zero:
$$30 - K_p = 0 \implies K_p = K_u = 30$$

**Step 3:** Find the oscillation frequency $\omega_u$ from the auxiliary equation (row $s^2$):
$$6s^2 + K_p = 0 \implies 6s^2 + 30 = 0 \implies s^2 = -5 \implies \omega_u = \sqrt{5} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ultimate period:
$$T_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 2.81 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5:** Apply ZN formulas:
$$K_p = 0.6 K_u = 0.6 \times 30 = 18$$
$$T_i = 0.5 T_u = 0.5 \times 2.81 = 1.405 \text{ s}$$
$$T_d = 0.125 T_u = 0.125 \times 2.81 = 0.351 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to parallel-form gains:
$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{18}{1.405} \approx 12.81$$
$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 18 \times 0.351 \approx 6.32$$

**Final PID Gains:** $\boxed{K_p = 18, \quad K_i \approx 12.81, \quad K_d \approx 6.32}$

---

**(b) Model Solution — Gradient-Descent ML Tuning (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Cost Function Definition:**

Define the mean-square error objective over $N$ timesteps:
$$J(K_p, K_i, K_d) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ e(k)^2 + \lambda_u \, \Delta u(k)^2 \right]$$

where $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$ and $\lambda_u$ is a control-effort penalty.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Gradient Computation via Back-Propagation Through Plant:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial K_p} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ e(k) \frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial K_p} + \lambda_u \, \Delta u(k) \frac{\partial \Delta u(k)}{\partial K_p} \right]$$

The partial derivatives of $e(k)$ w.r.t. gains are obtained by back-propagating through the plant dynamics:
$$\frac{\partial e(k)}{\partial K_p} = -\frac{\partial y(k)}{\partial K_p} = -\frac{\partial y(k)}{\partial u(k)} \cdot \frac{\partial u(k)}{\partial K_p} = -G_p'(k) \cdot e(k)$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Update Rule:**

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}, \quad \theta = [K_p, K_i, K_d]^T$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (e.g., $\eta = 0.01$). Convergence is checked via $\vert \Delta \theta \vert \lt 10^{-4}$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Comparison with ZN Method:**

| **Criterion** | **ZN Method** | **Gradient-Descent ML** |
|---|---|---|
| Plant Knowledge | Requires analytical model | Only I/O data needed |
| Optimality | Heuristic (~25% overshoot) | Near-optimal w.r.t. $J$ |
| Computation | One-shot (instant) | Iterative (many evaluations) |
| Real-time adaptation | Not native | Possible via online updates |
| Robustness to noise | Moderate | Depends on data SNR |

The ML approach **excels in optimality and adaptability**, while the ZN method **excels in simplicity and speed of deployment**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a unity-feedback system with PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ and plant $G_p(s) = \frac{K}{Ts+1}$. Apply the Routh-Hurwitz criterion to derive the **range of $K_p$ for stability**, with $K_i$ and $K_d$ fixed. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss the use of **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** and **Genetic Algorithms (GA)** for tuning PID gains. State two advantages of metaheuristic tuning over ZN. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**(a) Model Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Loop Transfer Function:**
$$L(s) = C(s) G_p(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) \cdot \frac{K}{Ts+1}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**
$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic Polynomial:**
$$1 + L(s) = 0 \implies s(Ts+1) + K\left(K_p s + K_i + K_d s^2\right) = 0$$

Expanding:
$$Ts^2 + s + K K_d s^2 + K K_p s + K K_i = 0$$

$$(T + K K_d) s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i = 0$$

**Step 4 — Routh-Hurwitz Analysis:**

For a **second-order polynomial** $a s^2 + b s + c = 0$ to have all roots with negative real parts, we require:
$$a \gt 0, \quad b \gt 0, \quad c \gt 0$$

This yields:
- $(T + K K_d) \gt 0$ — always true since $T, K, K_d \gt 0$
- $(1 + K K_p) \gt 0$ — always true
- $K K_i \gt 0$ — requires $K_i \gt 0$ (assuming $K \gt 0$)

**Step 5 — Range of $K_p$ for Stability:**

Since for a **second-order** characteristic polynomial with all-positive coefficients, **any positive $K_p$ is stable**. Therefore:

$$\boxed{0 \lt K_p \lt \infty}$$

**Note (2 marks):** The derivative term $K_d$ increases the effective inertia of the system, raising the order of the characteristic polynomial only when higher-order plant dynamics exist. For a pure first-order plant, the closed-loop system is **always stable for any positive $K_p$ and $K_i$** — the derivative term $K_d$ does not affect the second-order stability bound directly.

---

**(b) Model Solution — PSO and GA for PID Tuning (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (2.5 marks) — PSO-Based PID Tuning:**

In PSO, each candidate solution is a "particle" representing a gain vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}_i = [K_p, K_i, K_d]^T$. Each particle has a position and velocity, updated as:

$$v_i^{t+1} = w v_i^t + c_1 r_1 (p_{best,i} - \theta_i^t) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - \theta_i^t)$$

$$\theta_i^{t+1} = \theta_i^t + v_i^{t+1}$$

where $w$ is the inertia weight, $c_1, c_2$ are cognitive and social coefficients, $r_1, r_2 \in [0,1]$ are random values, $p_{best,i}$ is the personal best position, and $g_{best}$ is the global best.

The fitness function is the ITAE or ISE cost: $J = \int_0^\infty t \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$. Particles converge toward the global optimum in the search space.

**Step 2 (2.5 marks) — GA-Based PID Tuning:**

A Genetic Algorithm uses **chromosome encoding** where each individual is a candidate gain triplet. The algorithm proceeds through:
1. **Initialization:** Generate a random population of $N$ gain triples.
2. **Fitness Evaluation:** Simulate the closed-loop response for each individual and compute ITAE.
3. **Selection:** Retain the fittest individuals (e.g., via tournament selection).
4. **Crossover:** Combine pairs of parent chromosomes to produce offspring.
5. **Mutation:** Randomly perturb genes with low probability $p_m \sim 0.01$ to $0.1$.
6. **Iteration:** Repeat for $G$ generations until convergence.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Advantages of Metaheuristic Tuning over ZN:**

1. **Optimality:** Metaheuristics minimize a user-defined cost function (e.g., ITAE) directly, producing gains that are demonstrably near-optimal — ZN is empirical and typically gives $\sim 25\%$ overshoot.
2. **Constraint Handling:** PSO and GA can directly incorporate constraints on $M_p$, $t_s$, and control effort $u(t)$, yielding **multi-objective Pareto-optimal** solutions. ZN has no such mechanism.
3. **No Plant Model Required:** GA/PSO work directly on simulated or measured I/O data — the system can be treated as a **black box**, whereas ZN requires driving the plant to instability.
4. **Robustness:** Stochastic exploration in PSO/GA avoids local minima that gradient descent may get trapped in for non-convex cost landscapes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the Routh-Hurwitz auxiliary equation** to find $\omega_u$ after computing $K_u$ — this is a 2-mark deduction in Q.A(a).
> 2. **Mixing up $T_i$ and $K_i$** — remember $K_i = K_p / T_i$ (they are not equal!). Many students write $K_i = T_i$ directly, which is incorrect.
> 3. **In gradient-descent questions**, students often forget the **negative sign** in the update rule $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$. The descent direction requires the negative gradient.
> 4. **For PSO/GA questions**, do not describe them as "gradient-based" — they are **gradient-free, population-based metaheuristics**.
> 5. **Steady-state error** analysis is a frequent sub-part — recall that for a **Type-1** system (one integrator in $L(s)$), the steady-state error to a unit step is **zero**, but to a ramp it is $1/K_v$ where $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s L(s)$.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** $K_p$ is dimensionless (or matches actuator units), but $K_i$ has units of $s^{-1}$ and $K_d$ has units of $s$. The examiner often tests this awareness.

---

### 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **PID Control Law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ — three terms: present, past, future errors.
- [x] **Open-Loop Transfer Function:** $L(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) G_p(s)$.
- [x] **Closed-Loop:** $T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)}$.
- [x] **ZN Ultimate Cycle:** Set $K_i = 0, K_d = 0$, find $K_u$ and $T_u$, then apply $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- [x] **Routh-Hurwitz:** All first-column entries of the Routh array must be **positive** for stability.
- [x] **ITAE:** $J = \int_0^\infty t \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ is the **gold standard** KTU-recognized performance index.
- [x] **Overshoot Formula:** $M_p = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Settling Time (2%):** $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$.
- [x] **Gradient Descent Update:** $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n - \eta \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$ — **always with a negative sign**.
- [x] **PSO Update:** Combines inertia ($w v_i$), cognitive ($c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - \theta)$), and social ($c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - \theta)$) components.
- [x] **GA Steps:** Initialization → Fitness → Selection → Crossover → Mutation → Iterate.
- [x] **Integrator Windup:** When $K_i$ is large, the integral term accumulates during actuator saturation, causing large overshoots. Anti-windup schemes (clamping, back-calculation) are essential in practice.
- [x] **Derivative Filter:** In practice, $K_d s$ is replaced by $\frac{K_d s}{\tau_f s + 1}$ to filter measurement noise.
- [x] **ML-based tuning advantage:** Does not require explicit plant model — works on I/O data (black-box approach).
- [x] **Metaheuristic advantage:** Handles non-convex, multi-modal cost landscapes and supports constraint satisfaction on $M_p$ and $t_s$.
- [x] **Exam tip:** Always state the **units** of $K_p, K_i, K_d$ explicitly when first introducing them.
- [x] **Internal choice awareness:** KTU ESE Module 3 typically offers one of {PID tuning, Fuzzy systems, Neural Control} — be ready for any one of these sub-topics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Predictive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Model Predictive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Model Predictive Control (MPC)** is an advanced *optimal control* methodology that uses an explicit dynamic model of the process to predict its future evolution over a finite time horizon. At every sampling instant, an *open-loop optimal control sequence* is computed by minimizing a cost function subject to system constraints; only the **first control move** is applied, and the entire procedure is repeated at the next instant using updated measurements. This philosophy is universally known as the **Receding Horizon Strategy (RHS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** MPC is studied as a model-based reinforcement learning and optimal control paradigm that bridges classical control theory with sequential decision-making under constraints. It is a foundational precursor to modern data-driven control and offline RL planning.

Mathematically, the MPC law is the *first element* of the optimal sequence obtained from a constrained finite-horizon optimal control problem (FHOCP).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Analogy"

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road:

- Your **brain contains a model** of how the steering wheel affects the car's trajectory.
- You **look ahead** along the road for a finite distance (the *prediction horizon*).
- You **plan** a sequence of steering actions that keeps you on the road, respects the speed limit, and avoids the cliff (these are your *constraints*).
- However, you do **not execute the entire plan blindly** — you only apply the **first steering angle** for the next few seconds.
- Once you have moved, you **re-observe** the road, re-predict, and re-plan.

That is exactly how MPC operates: **predict → optimize → apply first action → repeat**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Prediction Horizon ($N_p$)** | Number of future steps over which outputs are predicted | Determines how far ahead the controller "looks" |
| **Control Horizon ($N_c$)** | Number of future control moves computed | Usually $N_c \leq N_p$ |
| **Sampling Time ($T_s$)** | Time interval between successive optimizations | Hardware-imposed |
| **Cost Function ($J$)** | Scalar measure of tracking error and control effort | The objective to be minimized |
| **Constraints** | Bounds on states ($x$), inputs ($u$), or outputs ($y$) | Hard vs. Soft distinction |
| **Receding Horizon** | Re-solving the optimization at every $k$ | The defining feature of MPC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** When asked to "explain the receding horizon principle," always describe *three* actions performed at each sampling instant: (1) measure/estimate state, (2) solve FHOCP, (3) apply only the first optimal input. Students who forget step (3) lose marks.

### 1.4 Why MPC Matters in Modern Engineering

MPC is the **industry-standard** advanced control technique in:

- **Petrochemical & Refining Industries** — controlling distillation columns, catalytic crackers.
- **Autonomous Vehicles & Robotics** — trajectory planning, obstacle avoidance.
- **Power Electronics & Smart Grids** — inverter control, battery management.
- **Process Industries** — temperature, pressure, and flow regulation.
- **Aerospace** — attitude control of spacecraft, quadrotor trajectory tracking.

It is the only advanced control technique that **handles multi-variable constrained systems** in a principled, optimal manner.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of MPC

Every MPC formulation rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **Internal Dynamic Model** — A mathematical predictor (state-space, transfer function, or learned model).
2. **Rolling Optimization** — A constrained optimization solved online at each sampling instant.
3. **Receding Horizon Implementation** — Only the first move is implemented; the horizon shifts forward.

> [!TIP]
> If a KTU question asks "list the essential elements of MPC," always enumerate these three pillars verbatim. They are high-frequency scoring points.

### 2.2 Linear Discrete-Time State-Space Prediction Model

The workhorse model used in industrial MPC is the **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** discrete state-space system:

$$x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C\,x(k)$$

where:
- $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — state vector at instant $k$
- $u(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ — manipulated input vector
- $y(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ — measured output vector
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — system (state) matrix
- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ — input matrix
- $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ — output matrix

### 2.3 Prediction Equations Over Horizon $N_p$

Starting from the current state $x(k)$, the future states are recursively predicted:

$$x(k+1 \vert k) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k)$$

$$x(k+2 \vert k) = A^{2}x(k) + AB\,u(k) + B\,u(k+1)$$

In general, for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N_p$:

$$x(k+i \vert k) = A^{i}x(k) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} A^{i-1-j}\,B\,u(k+j)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ | Discrete state-space model | $A, B$ constant matrices |
| 2 | $x(k+i \vert k) = A^{i}x(k) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} A^{i-1-j}B\,u(k+j)$ | $i$-step ahead state prediction | Conditional on state at $k$ |
| 3 | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \left[ \Vert y(k+i) - r(k+i) \Vert_{Q}^{2} \right] + \sum_{i=0}^{N_c-1} \left[ \Vert \Delta u(k+i) \Vert_{R}^{2} \right]$ | Standard MPC cost function | $Q \succeq 0$, $R \succ 0$ |
| 4 | $J = X^{T} \mathcal{Q}\,X + U^{T} \mathcal{R}\,U$ | Compact (QP) cost | $\mathcal{Q}, \mathcal{R}$ block-diagonal |
| 5 | $u_{\min} \leq u(k+i) \leq u_{\max}$ | Input magnitude constraints | Box constraints |
| 6 | $\Delta u_{\min} \leq \Delta u(k+i) \leq \Delta u_{\max}$ | Input rate (slew) constraints | Move-size constraints |
| 7 | $y_{\min} \leq y(k+i) \leq y_{\max}$ | Output constraints | Operational bounds |
| 8 | $N_p, N_c$ | Prediction and control horizons | $N_c \leq N_p$ |
| 9 | $T_s$ | Sampling period | Hardware constraint |
| 10 | $\lambda$ | Move-suppression weight (tuning) | Scalar in unconstrained case |

### 2.5 The Constrained Finite-Horizon Optimal Control Problem (FHOCP)

At each sampling instant $k$, MPC solves:

$$\min_{U} \quad J(x(k), U) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \Vert y(k+i \vert k) - r(k+i) \Vert_{Q}^{2} + \sum_{i=0}^{N_c-1} \Vert \Delta u(k+i) \Vert_{R}^{2}$$

subject to:
- $x(k+i+1 \vert k) = A\,x(k+i \vert k) + B\,u(k+i)$  for $i = 0, \dots, N_p-1$
- $u_{\min} \leq u(k+i) \leq u_{\max}$  for $i = 0, \dots, N_c-1$
- $\Delta u_{\min} \leq \Delta u(k+i) \leq \Delta u_{\max}$  for $i = 0, \dots, N_c-1$
- $x_{\min} \leq x(k+i \vert k) \leq x_{\max}$  for $i = 1, \dots, N_p$
- $x(k \vert k) = x(k)$  (initial condition from measurement/observer)

The optimal sequence is $U^{\star} = [u^{\star}(k), u^{\star}(k+1), \dots, u^{\star}(k+N_c-1)]$, and the **MPC law** is:

$$u_{\text{MPC}}(k) = u^{\star}(k \vert k)$$

That is, **only the first element** is applied.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility and Interpretation of Tuning Weights

- **$Q$ (output weighting matrix):** Penalizes tracking error of $y$ relative to reference $r$. Larger $Q \Rightarrow$ faster tracking, more aggressive control.
- **$R$ (move weighting matrix):** Penalizes magnitude of $\Delta u$. Larger $R \Rightarrow$ smoother, slower control action, less actuator wear.
- **$Q$ large vs. $R$ large:** The trade-off between *aggressiveness* and *smoothness* — a classic exam topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Question:** "How do you tune an MPC controller?" — Answer with a structured table relating $N_p$, $N_c$, $Q$, and $R$ to closed-loop behavior (stability, speed, constraint handling, computational cost).

### 2.7 Existence and Feasibility of MPC

Three classes of feasibility must be understood:

| Feasibility Type | Meaning | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Feasibility** | The QP has at least one feasible point | Required at every $k$ |
| **Recursive Feasibility** | If feasible at $k$, feasible at $k+1$ | Guaranteed by terminal constraint $x(k+N_p)=0$ |
| **Stability** | Closed-loop state $\to 0$ | Achieved by $N_p \to \infty$ or terminal cost/constraint |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Unconstrained Linear MPC Law (LQR-MPC Equivalence)

We derive, line by line, the explicit MPC law for a **regulation** problem (target $r=0$) with no constraints. The aim is to show that the unconstrained MPC collapses to the classical **Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)** with horizon $N_p$.

#### Step 1 — Set Up the State Prediction

Define the stacked vectors:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x(k+1 \vert k) \\ x(k+2 \vert k) \\ \vdots \\ x(k+N_p \vert k) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n N_p}, \qquad U = \begin{bmatrix} u(k) \\ u(k+1) \\ \vdots \\ u(k+N_c-1) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{m N_c}$$

Assume $N_c = N_p = N$ for simplicity (standard in unconstrained derivation).

#### Step 2 — Express $X$ as a Function of $x(k)$ and $U$

By direct substitution into $x(k+i) = A^{i}x(k) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} A^{i-1-j}Bu(k+j)$:

$$X = \mathcal{F}\,x(k) + \mathcal{G}\,U$$

where the **prediction matrices** are:

$$\mathcal{F} = \begin{bmatrix} A \\ A^{2} \\ \vdots \\ A^{N} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathcal{G} = \begin{bmatrix} B & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ AB & B & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ A^{N-1}B & A^{N-2}B & \cdots & B \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Step 3 — Define the Block-Diagonal Weighting Matrices

$$\mathcal{Q} = \mathrm{blkdiag}(\underbrace{Q, Q, \dots, Q}_{N\ \text{times}}), \qquad \mathcal{R} = \mathrm{blkdiag}(\underbrace{R, R, \dots, R}_{N\ \text{times}})$$

These live in $\mathbb{R}^{nN \times nN}$ and $\mathbb{R}^{mN \times mN}$ respectively.

#### Step 4 — Write the Cost Function in Compact Form

For output $y = Cx$, define $\tilde{\mathcal{C}} = I_N \otimes C$ (Kronecker product). Then $Y = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\,X$, and:

$$J = (Y - R_{ref})^{T}\mathcal{Q}(Y - R_{ref}) + U^{T}\mathcal{R}\,U$$

Substituting $X = \mathcal{F}x + \mathcal{G}U$ and $Y = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}X$:

$$J = \left(\tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F}x + \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{G}U - R_{ref}\right)^{T}\mathcal{Q}\left(\tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F}x + \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{G}U - R_{ref}\right) + U^{T}\mathcal{R}\,U$$

#### Step 5 — Group the Terms in $U$

Let $\Phi = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{G}$ and $\Psi = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F}x - R_{ref}$. Then:

$$J = (\Psi + \Phi U)^{T}\mathcal{Q}(\Psi + \Phi U) + U^{T}\mathcal{R}\,U$$

Expanding the square:

$$J = \Psi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi + 2 U^{T}\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi + U^{T}\left(\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R}\right)U$$

#### Step 6 — Recognize the Standard Quadratic Program (QP) Structure

$$J(U) = \frac{1}{2} U^{T}\,H\,U + U^{T}\,g + \text{constant}$$

where, identifying with the standard QP form:

$$H = 2\left(\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R}\right)$$

$$g = 2\,\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\,\Psi$$

The constant $\Psi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi$ does not depend on $U$ and is dropped for minimization.

#### Step 7 — Take the Gradient and Set It to Zero

Unconstrained optimum satisfies $\nabla_{U} J = 0$:

$$H\,U^{\star} + g = 0$$

$$\Longrightarrow U^{\star} = -H^{-1}g = -\left(\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R}\right)^{-1}\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi$$

#### Step 8 — Extract the MPC Law (First Move Only)

The optimal sequence is $U^{\star} = [u^{\star}(k), u^{\star}(k+1), \dots, u^{\star}(k+N-1)]^{T}$. Define the **selection matrix** $K_0 = [I_m,\ 0,\ \dots,\ 0] \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times mN}$. The applied control is:

$$u_{\text{MPC}}(k) = K_0\,U^{\star} = -K_0\left(\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R}\right)^{-1}\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi$$

#### Step 9 — Closed-Loop Expression

Substituting $\Psi = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F}x(k) - R_{ref}$:

$$u_{\text{MPC}}(k) = -K_{\text{MPC}}\,x(k) + K_{r}\,R_{ref}$$

where:

$$K_{\text{MPC}} = K_0\left(\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R}\right)^{-1}\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F}$$

$$K_{r} = K_0\left(\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R}\right)^{-1}\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}$$

This is the celebrated result: **unconstrained MPC with horizon $N$ is a static state-feedback law**, equivalent to a finite-horizon LQR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** When there are *no constraints* and *no horizon truncation effects*, the MPC controller is mathematically identical to the classical LQR (infinite horizon) provided $N_p \to \infty$ and $P_\infty$ is the solution to the Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE).

### 3.2 Recursive Feasibility via Terminal Constraint — Detailed Argument

**Claim:** If we add the terminal constraint $x(k+N_p \vert k) = 0$, then feasibility at time $k$ implies feasibility at time $k+1$.

**Reasoning:**

1. At time $k$, suppose optimal sequence $U^{\star}_k = [u^{\star}(k),\dots,u^{\star}(k+N_c-1)]$ drives $x(k+N_p \vert k) = 0$.
2. At time $k+1$, the state $x(k+1 \vert k+1) = x(k+1 \vert k)$ is known (re-measured).
3. The previously optimal *shifted* sequence $\tilde{U} = [u^{\star}(k+1),\dots,u^{\star}(k+N_c-1), 0]$ is feasible at time $k+1$.
4. Therefore, the feasible set at $k+1$ is non-empty, ensuring the QP has a solution.
5. The optimizer may then find a *better* sequence, but feasibility is guaranteed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "How is stability of MPC ensured?", your answer should include the **terminal cost + terminal constraint** (mode 1), or the **terminal equality constraint** (mode 2), or the **dual-mode** prediction (mode 3). These are the three standard stability proofs.

### 3.3 Tuning Guidance — Closed-Loop Behaviour vs. Parameters

The following relationships must be memorized for KTU viva and written exams:

$$\boxed{N_p \uparrow \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Stability \& robustness} \uparrow,\quad \text{Computation} \uparrow \uparrow}$$

$$\boxed{N_c \uparrow \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Performance} \uparrow,\quad \text{Computation} \uparrow}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{ii} \uparrow \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Faster tracking of } y_i}$$

$$\boxed{R_{jj} \uparrow \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Slower, smoother control of } u_j}$$

$$\boxed{T_s \downarrow \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Better tracking},\ \text{Higher CPU load}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MPC Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the closed-loop MPC system showing the interaction between the **plant**, the **model-based optimizer**, and the **receding-horizon executor**. Node identifiers are alphanumeric and labels contain only raw uppercase text to comply with Mermaid parsing rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[REFERENCE SIGNAL r(k)] --> B1[COST FUNCTION J SUMMATION]
    A2[MEASUREMENT y(k)] --> B2[STATE ESTIMATOR OBSERVER]
    B2 --> B3[CURRENT STATE x(k) BAR]
    B3 --> B4[MODEL PREDICTOR FUTURE TRAJECTORY]
    A1 --> B4
    B1 --> B5[CONSTRAINED QP SOLVER]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6[OPTIMAL CONTROL SEQUENCE U STAR]
    B6 --> B7[FIRST MOVE EXTRACTOR U OF K]
    B7 --> C1[PLANT PROCESS]
    C1 --> C2[OUTPUT y(k+1)]
    C2 --> B2
    C2 --> A2
    B6 -.RECEDING HORIZON.-> B8[NEXT INSTANT k EQUALS k PLUS 1 SHIFT]
    B8 --> A2
    style A1 fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#333
    style A2 fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#333
    style B5 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Receding Horizon Strategy — Temporal Sequence

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates how the prediction horizon slides forward in time. At each instant $k$, a *new* optimization is solved and only the *first* sample of the resulting sequence is applied.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K0["INSTANT k EQUALS ZERO
H OPTIMIZE OVER
U0 U1 U2 U3
APPLY U0 ONLY"] --> K1["INSTANT k EQUALS ONE
H OPTIMIZE OVER
U1 U2 U3 U4
APPLY U1 ONLY"]
    K1 --> K2["INSTANT k EQUALS TWO
H OPTIMIZE OVER
U2 U3 U4 U5
APPLY U2 ONLY"]
    K2 --> K3["INSTANT k EQUALS THREE
H OPTIMIZE OVER
U3 U4 U5 U6
APPLY U3 ONLY"]
    style K0 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style K1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style K2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
    style K3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Functional Component Architecture

This block-level flow shows the modular decomposition of an MPC software stack. Each block represents a computational stage performed at every sampling instant $T_s$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[SENSOR DATA ACQUISITION] --> S2[STATE ESTIMATION KALMAN FILTER]
    S2 --> S3[REFERENCE INTERPOLATION TRAJECTORY]
    S3 --> S4[CONSTRAINED OPTIMIZATION QP SOLVER]
    S2 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[FIRST CONTROL MOVE EXTRACTION]
    S5 --> S6[ACTUATOR COMMAND APPLICATION]
    S6 --> S7[PLANT DYNAMICS]
    S7 --> S1
    S4 -.LOG.-> S8[PERFORMANCE METRICS LOGGING]
    S8 -.FEEDBACK.-> S3
    style S4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
    style S6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style S7 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** When asked to draw the MPC block diagram in an exam, *always* include the feedback loop from the plant output back to the model predictor and the explicit "first-move extractor" before the actuator. These two details distinguish a *correct* MPC diagram from a generic optimal-control diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Exam 2024]**

**Define Model Predictive Control (MPC) and explain the concept of the receding horizon strategy with a suitable block diagram description.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Marking Scheme):**

*Model Predictive Control (MPC)* is an advanced model-based control strategy that solves a constrained finite-horizon optimal control problem at every sampling instant using an explicit dynamic model of the process.

*Receding Horizon Strategy (RHS):*

1. **At every sampling instant $k$:** Measure (or estimate) the current state $x(k)$.
2. **Solve the FHOCP:** Compute the optimal control sequence $U^{\star}(k) = [u^{\star}(k),\dots,u^{\star}(k+N_c-1)]$ over the prediction horizon $N_p$ that minimizes the cost $J$ while satisfying constraints.
3. **Apply only the first move:** Implement $u(k) = u^{\star}(k \vert k)$ on the plant.
4. **Shift the horizon:** Increment $k \to k+1$ and repeat the entire procedure with the new measurement.

The name "receding" arises because the optimization window of length $N_p$ is slid (recedes) forward in time by one sampling instant at every iteration. This closed-loop feedback mechanism compensates for model mismatch and external disturbances in a principled way.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, three-step RHS explanation 2 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**Differentiate between Model Predictive Control (MPC) and the classical Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR). List any three distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | MPC | LQR |
|---|---|---|
| Horizon | Finite (and receding) | Infinite |
| Constraints | Explicitly handled | Cannot handle |
| Computation | Online optimization at each $k$ | Off-line (Riccati) |
| Model | Any (linear/nonlinear) | Linear time-invariant |
| Reference tracking | Built-in | Requires augmentation |
| Tuning knobs | $N_p, N_c, Q, R$ jointly | $Q, R$ only |

**Three distinguishing points (any three):**

1. MPC uses a *finite receding* horizon, while LQR uses an *infinite* horizon (steady-state Riccati).
2. MPC explicitly incorporates **input, state, and output constraints**; LQR cannot.
3. MPC solves an *online optimization* (QP) at every instant; LQR is computed *offline* via the DARE.
4. MPC can use **nonlinear or data-driven models**; classical LQR is restricted to LTI systems.
5. MPC outputs the *first move* of an open-loop optimal sequence (receding principle); LQR outputs a *fixed* state-feedback law $u = -Kx$.

*(3 marks — table 1 mark, three distinguishing features 2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A:**
> - Writing "MPC is better than LQR" without justification = **0 marks** for the comparison.
> - Forgetting to mention *finite vs infinite horizon* loses the key differentiator.
> - In the receding-horizon answer, omitting "**only the first input is applied**" costs at least 1 mark.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Formulate the constrained Finite-Horizon Optimal Control Problem (FHOCP) solved at each sampling instant of a Linear MPC. Clearly state the cost function, the dynamic model, the constraints, and the receding horizon implementation steps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the second-order discrete-time system with sampling period $T_s = 0.5$ s:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad Q = I_2, \qquad R = 1$$

Compute the prediction matrices $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ for $N_p = 2$, $N_c = 2$. Hence write the compact QP form $J = \frac{1}{2} U^{T} H U + U^{T} g$ in terms of $x(k)$.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Constrained FHOCP Formulation

**Cost Function:**

At every sampling instant $k$, MPC solves:

$$J(x(k), U) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \Vert y(k+i \vert k) - r(k+i) \Vert_{Q}^{2} + \sum_{i=0}^{N_c-1} \Vert \Delta u(k+i) \Vert_{R}^{2}$$

where:
- $\Vert z \Vert_{Q}^{2} = z^{T}Q z$ is the quadratic weighted norm
- $y(k+i \vert k)$ is the predicted output at $k+i$ given information at $k$
- $r(k+i)$ is the future reference trajectory
- $\Delta u(k+i) = u(k+i) - u(k+i-1)$ is the input *move* (incremental control)

**Dynamic Model (Prediction Equations):**

$$x(k+i+1 \vert k) = A\,x(k+i \vert k) + B\,u(k+i)$$

$$y(k+i \vert k) = C\,x(k+i \vert k), \qquad i = 0, 1, \dots, N_p-1$$

with initial condition $x(k \vert k) = x(k)$.

**Constraints (Hard bounds):**

- Input magnitude: $u_{\min} \leq u(k+i) \leq u_{\max}$
- Input rate: $\Delta u_{\min} \leq \Delta u(k+i) \leq \Delta u_{\max}$
- State: $x_{\min} \leq x(k+i \vert k) \leq x_{\max}$
- Output: $y_{\min} \leq y(k+i \vert k) \leq y_{\max}$

**Receding Horizon Implementation (Three Steps):**

1. **Measure / Estimate:** At time $k$, obtain $x(k)$ (sensor or observer).
2. **Solve FHOCP:** Compute $U^{\star}(k) = \arg\min_{U} J(x(k), U)$ subject to the above.
3. **Apply and Shift:** Set $u(k) = u^{\star}(k \vert k)$; let $k \to k+1$; repeat.

*(7 marks — cost 2, model 2, constraints 2, implementation 1)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation of $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$, $H$, $g$

**Step 1 — Compute $A^2$:**

$$A^{2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $AB$:**

$$AB = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 + 0.25 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.375 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Construct $\mathcal{F}$ (size $4 \times 2$):**

$$\mathcal{F} = \begin{bmatrix} A \\ A^{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Construct $\mathcal{G}$ (size $4 \times 2$):**

$$\mathcal{G} = \begin{bmatrix} B & 0 \\ AB & B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0 \\ 0.375 & 0.125 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Construct $\tilde{\mathcal{C}}$:**

$$\tilde{\mathcal{C}} = I_2 \otimes C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ \cdots & \cdots \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}_{\text{first two cols kept}}$$

Concretely, $\tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F}$ retains only the rows corresponding to the $C$-projection, which for $C = [1\ 0]$ keeps only the first row of each block:

$$\tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Construct $\Phi = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{G}$:**

$$\Phi = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{G} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 & 0 \\ 0.375 & 0.125 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Block-diagonal weights:**

$$\mathcal{Q} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_2, \qquad \mathcal{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_2$$

**Step 8 — Compute $H = 2(\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R})$:**

First $\Phi^{T}\Phi$:

$$\Phi^{T}\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 & 0.375 \\ 0 & 0.125 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 & 0 \\ 0.375 & 0.125 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.015625 + 0.140625 & 0 + 0.046875 \\ 0 + 0.046875 & 0 + 0.015625 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Phi^{T}\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.15625 & 0.046875 \\ 0.046875 & 0.015625 \end{bmatrix}$$

Adding $\mathcal{R}$:

$$\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.15625 & 0.046875 \\ 0.046875 & 1.015625 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$H = 2 \times \begin{bmatrix} 1.15625 & 0.046875 \\ 0.046875 & 1.015625 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.3125 & 0.09375 \\ 0.09375 & 2.03125 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 9 — Compute $g$ (assuming $R_{ref} = 0$):**

$$\Psi = \tilde{\mathcal{C}}\mathcal{F}\,x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) + 0.5 x_2(k) \\ x_1(k) + x_2(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi = \Phi^{T}\Psi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 & 0.375 \\ 0 & 0.125 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 + 0.5 x_2 \\ x_1 + x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.125(x_1 + 0.5 x_2) + 0.375(x_1 + x_2) \\ 0.125(x_1 + x_2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 x_1 + 0.5 x_2 \\ 0.125 x_1 + 0.125 x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$g = 2\,\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) + x_2(k) \\ 0.25 x_1(k) + 0.25 x_2(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final QP Form:**

$$\boxed{J = \frac{1}{2} U^{T} \begin{bmatrix} 2.3125 & 0.09375 \\ 0.09375 & 2.03125 \end{bmatrix} U + \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) + x_2(k) \\ 0.25 x_1(k) + 0.25 x_2(k) \end{bmatrix}^{T} U}$$

The unconstrained optimum is $U^{\star} = -H^{-1} g$ and the MPC law is $u(k) = K_0 U^{\star} = U^{\star}[1:1]$ (the first scalar component).

*(7 marks — F 1, G 1.5, Phi 1, H 2, g 1, final boxed form 0.5)*

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three core elements of MPC: prediction model, rolling optimization, and receding horizon. Discuss how the choice of $N_p$ and $N_c$ affects closed-loop performance, computational load, and stability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A scalar discrete system has $A = 1.2$, $B = 1$, $Q = 1$, $R = 0.1$. For $N_p = N_c = 2$, compute the optimal unconstrained MPC gain sequence, and show that the resulting closed-loop pole lies inside the unit disk (i.e., the system is stable).

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Three Core Elements of MPC

**1. Prediction Model:**

The prediction model is an internal mathematical representation of the plant used by the controller to forecast future behavior. Common choices include:
- Linear time-invariant (LTI) state-space: $x(k+1) = Ax(k) + Bu(k)$
- Transfer function models (Step/NMPC responses)
- Nonlinear models (for nonlinear MPC)
- Data-driven models (neural networks, Gaussian processes)

The model must be **discretized** at the controller sampling period $T_s$.

**2. Rolling Optimization:**

At every sampling instant, the controller solves:

$$\min_{U(k)} J(x(k), U(k))$$

subject to dynamics and constraints. This is typically a **quadratic program (QP)** for linear MPC, solvable in real time using active-set or interior-point methods.

**3. Receding Horizon:**

Only the first element $u^{\star}(k \vert k)$ is applied. The horizon is then shifted forward and re-optimized with fresh measurements.

**Effect of $N_p$ and $N_c$:**

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Performance | Increase Effect on Computation | Increase Effect on Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_p$ | Better tracking & robustness | Quadratic increase in QP size | Strongly stabilizes (recursive feasibility) |
| $N_c$ | Smoother control effort | Linear increase in QP variables | Indirect; usually $N_c \ll N_p$ is sufficient |

**Rule of Thumb:** Choose $N_p$ as the **rise time / settling time** of the open-loop step response divided by $T_s$. Choose $N_c$ typically 10–20% of $N_p$ (or even 1 for slow processes).

*(7 marks — three elements 3, Np Nc table 2, rule of thumb 2)*

---

#### Part (b) — Scalar Numerical MPC

Given: $A = 1.2$, $B = 1$, $Q = 1$, $R = 0.1$, $N_p = N_c = 2$.

**Step 1 — Construct $\mathcal{F}$ (column vector of size 2):**

$$\mathcal{F} = \begin{bmatrix} A \\ A^{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 \\ 1.44 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Construct $\mathcal{G}$ ($2 \times 2$ lower-triangular Toeplitz):**

$$\mathcal{G} = \begin{bmatrix} B & 0 \\ AB & B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1.2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C$ (scalar = 1 since the system is scalar):**

$C = 1$, so $\tilde{\mathcal{C}} = I_2$.

$$\Phi = \mathcal{G} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1.2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $H$:**

$$\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi = \mathcal{G}^{T}\mathcal{G} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1.2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1.2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + 1.44 & 1.2 \\ 1.2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.44 & 1.2 \\ 1.2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Phi + \mathcal{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.54 & 1.2 \\ 1.2 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$H = 2 \begin{bmatrix} 2.54 & 1.2 \\ 1.2 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.08 & 2.4 \\ 2.4 & 2.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the inverse $H^{-1}$:**

$$\det(H) = 5.08 \times 2.2 - 2.4 \times 2.4 = 11.176 - 5.76 = 5.416$$

$$H^{-1} = \frac{1}{5.416} \begin{bmatrix} 2.2 & -2.4 \\ -2.4 & 5.08 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4062 & -0.4431 \\ -0.4431 & 0.9380 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $g$ (regulation case, $R_{ref} = 0$):**

$$\Psi = \mathcal{F}\,x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 \\ 1.44 \end{bmatrix} x(k)$$

$$\Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi = \mathcal{G}^{T}\mathcal{F}\,x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1.2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 \\ 1.44 \end{bmatrix} x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 + 1.728 \\ 1.44 \end{bmatrix} x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 2.928 \\ 1.44 \end{bmatrix} x(k)$$

$$g = 2 \Phi^{T}\mathcal{Q}\Psi = \begin{bmatrix} 5.856 \\ 2.88 \end{bmatrix} x(k)$$

**Step 7 — Optimal sequence:**

$$U^{\star} = -H^{-1} g = -\begin{bmatrix} 0.4062 & -0.4431 \\ -0.4431 & 0.9380 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5.856 \\ 2.88 \end{bmatrix} x(k)$$

First component:
$$u^{\star}(k) = -(0.4062 \times 5.856 + (-0.4431) \times 2.88)\, x(k)$$
$$u^{\star}(k) = -(2.379 - 1.276)\, x(k) = -1.103\, x(k)$$

Second component (not applied):
$$u^{\star}(k+1) = -(-0.4431 \times 5.856 + 0.9380 \times 2.88)\, x(k) = -(-2.595 + 2.701)\, x(k) = -0.106\, x(k)$$

**Step 8 — MPC Law:**

$$\boxed{u_{\text{MPC}}(k) = -1.103\, x(k)}$$

**Step 9 — Closed-loop pole:**

The closed-loop dynamics is $x(k+1) = (A - B K_{\text{MPC}})\, x(k)$:

$$A - B K_{\text{MPC}} = 1.2 - (1)(-1.103) = 1.2 + 1.103 = 2.303$$

**Pole location:** $z = 2.303$, with $\vert z \vert = 2.303 > 1$ ⇒ **outside unit disk ⇒ UNSTABLE!**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The unconstrained MPC with a *finite horizon* ($N_p = 2$) is **not guaranteed stable** for open-loop unstable plants. The student might assume that $Q, R > 0$ implies stability — this is FALSE. Stability requires either (a) $N_p \to \infty$, (b) terminal cost/constraint, or (c) sufficiently large $N_p$ such that the truncated Riccati solution approaches the steady-state one.

This numerical counter-example is *exactly* the kind of question KTU examiners love: it tests whether the student *truly understands* the difference between LQR ($N=\infty$, stable) and finite-horizon MPC.

*(7 marks — F, G, H, g, U-star, closed-loop pole, stability analysis with warning 7)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (14-Mark):**
> - **Omitting the receding horizon in part (a):** You must list *three* sub-steps: measure, optimize, apply first. Missing the "apply only first" costs 1–2 marks.
> - **Numerical mistakes in matrix multiplication:** KTU examiners strictly check matrix dimensions. Writing $\Phi^{T}\Phi$ without verifying $2\times 2$ shape can lose 1 mark even if final numbers are right.
> - **Not stating units / dimensions:** Whenever you write $T_s$, mention "seconds." Whenever you write $Q$, mention "output weighting matrix, $\succeq 0$."
> - **Stability claim without verification:** The numerical example in Q-B(b) shows *instability*; if a student writes "therefore the system is stable" without computing $\vert z \vert$, full marks are forfeited.
> - **Citing wrong horizon equivalence:** Do **not** claim "finite MPC = infinite LQR." State the precise condition under which equivalence holds.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MPC** = model + optimization + receding horizon, solved at every $T_s$.
- **Prediction Horizon $N_p$** = length of look-ahead; **Control Horizon $N_c$** = length of planned input moves.
- **Receding Horizon:** Measure → Optimize → Apply **first move only** → Shift → Repeat.
- **Cost function** has two parts: tracking error ($\Vert y - r \Vert_{Q}^{2}$) and control effort ($\Vert \Delta u \Vert_{R}^{2}$).
- **Tuning rule of thumb:** Increase $Q$ → faster tracking; increase $R$ → smoother control.
- **Unconstrained MPC** $\to$ **equivalent to finite-horizon LQR** (Linear Quadratic Regulator); explicit formula $u = -K_{\text{MPC}} x$ via Hessian inverse.
- **Constrained MPC** $\to$ **Quadratic Program (QP)** solved online; handles hard limits on $u, \Delta u, x, y$.
- **Stability of MPC** is **not automatic**; needs terminal cost, terminal constraint, or sufficiently large $N_p$.
- **Feasibility ≠ Stability:** a controller can be feasible but destabilizing (as shown in Q-B(b)).
- **Block diagram essentials:** model predictor, optimizer, first-move extractor, and feedback from plant output.
- **Memorize the prediction matrix formula:** $\mathcal{F}$ stacks $[A; A^2; \dots; A^{N_p}]$ and $\mathcal{G}$ is a lower-triangular Toeplitz matrix of $A^{i-1-j}B$ entries.
- **MPC is industry-standard** for constrained multi-variable systems where PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller) is inadequate.
- **Difference from PID:** MPC uses an explicit model and anticipates future behavior; PID reacts to current error only.
- **Linear vs Nonlinear MPC:** Linear MPC (LMPC) is a QP; Nonlinear MPC (NMPC) is a non-convex NLP (Nonlinear Program), much more expensive.
- **Implementation requires:** a fast QP solver (active set, interior point), a state estimator (Kalman filter), and a discretized model.
- **Mentioning $\succeq 0$ and $\succ 0$ for $Q$ and $R$** in formulas shows examiner-aware maturity — always do this in KTU scripts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Linearization Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback Linearization Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Feedback Linearization** is an exact nonlinear control design methodology that algebraically transforms a nonlinear multivariable system into an equivalent linear one through a precise change of coordinates (diffeomorphism) and a nonlinear state-feedback control law, **without resorting to linearization via Taylor-series approximation**. The technique is *exact* and *global* in nature (within a region of validity), preserving system nonlinearities in the control law rather than discarding them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> Feedback linearization is a design technique that uses **state feedback** and **coordinate transformation** to convert a nonlinear system into an equivalent linear system in the new coordinates. The resulting linear system is then controlled using well-established linear control techniques (e.g., pole placement, LQR).

Consider a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) nonlinear affine system of the form:

$$\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\,u$$

$$y = h(x)$$

where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector, $u \in \mathbb{R}$ is the control input, $y \in \mathbb{R}$ is the output, $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ are smooth vector fields, and $h(x)$ is a smooth scalar field. The key step is to find a feedback law of the form:

$$u = \alpha(x) + \beta(x)\,v$$

where $v$ is the new (auxiliary) control input, $\alpha(x)$ cancels nonlinearities, and $\beta(x)$ normalizes the input gain. This yields a **linear input–output (or input–state) relationship** between $v$ and the output $y$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Curved Road Mapper"**
> Imagine driving a car on a curved mountain road. From a global (top-down) view, the road is highly nonlinear — sharp bends, varying slopes. However, if you had a **GPS-guided steering system** that continuously transforms the road geometry into a local *straight-line* reference frame at every instant, the steering task would suddenly feel linear to you as the driver.
> - The **coordinate transformation** $z = T(x)$ is analogous to constantly reorienting your local map.
> - The **feedback control law** $u = \alpha(x) + \beta(x) v$ is analogous to the steering assist that applies the correct wheel angle so the *transformed* trajectory obeys a simple linear "go straight" rule.

In essence, feedback linearization does **not** straighten the curved road — it builds a **nonlinear lens** through which the system *appears* linear to the downstream controller.

## 1.3 Why Feedback Linearization? (Engineering Motivation)

| Motivation | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Exactness** | No approximation error, unlike Jacobian linearization |
| **Global Validity** | Works over large operating regions (when conditions hold) |
| **Leverage of Linear Theory** | After transformation, design $v$ using pole placement, LQR, or $H_\infty$ |
| **Decoupling** | Naturally decouples MIMO systems into independent SISO loops |
| **Transparent Design** | Performance and stability specifications are intuitive in linear form |

> [!TIP]
> **Contrast with Jacobian Linearization:** Taylor-series linearization around an equilibrium gives a *local* linear approximation valid only in a small neighborhood. Feedback linearization gives an *exact* linear relationship valid wherever the transformation is well-defined.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Prerequisites

### 2.1.1 Lie Derivative (Scalar Function along a Vector Field)

For a scalar function $\lambda(x) : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ and a vector field $f(x) : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$, the **Lie derivative** of $\lambda$ along $f$ is defined as:

$$L_f \lambda(x) = \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial x}\, f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial x_i}\, f_i(x)$$

### 2.1.2 Repeated and Mixed Lie Derivatives

Higher-order Lie derivatives are computed recursively:

$$L_f^2 \lambda(x) = L_f\!\left(L_f \lambda(x)\right) = \frac{\partial (L_f \lambda)}{\partial x}\, f(x)$$

$$L_f^k \lambda(x) = \frac{\partial (L_f^{k-1} \lambda)}{\partial x}\, f(x)$$

A **mixed Lie derivative** involves two different vector fields:

$$L_g L_f \lambda(x) = \frac{\partial (L_f \lambda)}{\partial x}\, g(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Rule of Thumb**
> - Each Lie derivative is essentially a directional derivative along the indicated vector field.
> - $L_f \lambda(x) = 0$ means $\lambda$ is **invariant** along trajectories of $f$.
> - $L_g L_f \lambda(x) \neq 0$ means the input $u$ influences $\dot{\lambda}$ through $f$.

## 2.2 The Concept of Relative Degree

The **relative degree** $r$ of a SISO nonlinear system at a point $x_0$ is the number of times one must differentiate the output $y = h(x)$ with respect to time until the input $u$ explicitly appears with a non-zero coefficient.

Formally, $r$ satisfies:

$$L_g L_f^{i} h(x) = 0, \quad \forall\, x \in U, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, r-2$$

$$L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x_0) \neq 0$$

### 2.2.1 Successive Output Derivatives

$$\dot{y} = L_f h(x) + \underbrace{L_g h(x)}_{=0}\, u = L_f h(x)$$

$$\ddot{y} = L_f^2 h(x) + \underbrace{L_g L_f h(x)}_{=0}\, u = L_f^2 h(x)$$

$$\vdots$$

$$y^{(r-1)} = L_f^{r-1} h(x) + \underbrace{L_g L_f^{r-2} h(x)}_{=0}\, u = L_f^{r-1} h(x)$$

$$y^{(r)} = L_f^r h(x) + L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)\, u$$

### 2.2.2 Decomposition Theorem

The relative degree partitions the system state into two dynamically meaningful groups:
- **External (input-output) dynamics** of order $r$
- **Internal (zero) dynamics** of order $n - r$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation of $r$:** The relative degree represents the **time delay** between applying an input and observing a corresponding change in the output. It is the nonlinear analogue of the *excess of poles over zeros* in a transfer function.

## 2.3 The Feedback Linearization Control Law

Once the relative degree $r$ is identified and $L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x) \neq 0$ in the operating region, the **state-feedback linearizing control law** is:

$$u = \alpha(x) + \beta(x)\, v = \frac{-L_f^r h(x) + v}{L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)}$$

Substituting this into $y^{(r)}$ yields the **linear input-output relationship**:

$$y^{(r)} = v$$

The auxiliary control $v$ is then designed using standard linear techniques (e.g., $v = -k_1 y - k_2 \dot{y} - \dots - k_{r-1} y^{(r-2)} - k_r y^{(r-1)}$).

## 2.4 Input-State Linearization (Full Linearization)

When $r = n$, the relative degree equals the system order. The internal dynamics **vanish** entirely, and feedback linearization achieves a complete linearization between $v$ and all states. The coordinate transformation is:

$$z = T(x) = \begin{bmatrix} h(x) \\ L_f h(x) \\ \vdots \\ L_f^{n-1} h(x) \end{bmatrix}$$

In these coordinates, the closed-loop dynamics become the controllable canonical form:

$$\dot{z} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix} z + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} v$$

## 2.5 Input-Output Linearization with Zero Dynamics ($r < n$)

When $r < n$, define:

$$z_1 = h(x), \quad z_2 = L_f h(x), \quad \dots, \quad z_r = L_f^{r-1} h(x)$$

The remaining $n - r$ coordinates $\eta_1, \dots, \eta_{n-r}$ must be chosen so that $L_g \eta_i(x) = 0$ for all $i$, ensuring they are unaffected by the input directly. The transformed system becomes:

$$\dot{z}_1 = z_2$$

$$\dot{z}_2 = z_3$$

$$\vdots$$

$$\dot{z}_r = L_f^r h(x) + L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)\, u$$

$$\dot{\eta} = q(z, \eta) + p(z, \eta)\, u$$

After applying $u$, the $\eta$ dynamics reduce to **zero dynamics** when $z \equiv 0$:

$$\dot{\eta} = q(0, \eta)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Stability of zero dynamics is CRITICAL.** If the zero dynamics are unstable, the closed-loop system is internally unstable regardless of how well $y$ tracks $y_{ref}$. Such a system is called **non-minimum phase**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Affine Nonlinear System | $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\,u,\;\; y = h(x)$ | Canonical form for FLC |
| 2 | Lie Derivative | $L_f \lambda = \dfrac{\partial \lambda}{\partial x} f(x)$ | Directional derivative along $f$ |
| 3 | Repeated Lie Derivative | $L_f^k \lambda = \dfrac{\partial (L_f^{k-1} \lambda)}{\partial x} f(x)$ | Recursive definition |
| 4 | Mixed Lie Derivative | $L_g L_f \lambda = \dfrac{\partial (L_f \lambda)}{\partial x} g(x)$ | Two vector fields involved |
| 5 | Relative Degree Condition | $L_g L_f^i h = 0,\;\; i = 0, \dots, r-2$ | First $r-1$ derivatives vanish |
| 6 | Relative Degree Condition | $L_g L_f^{r-1} h \neq 0$ | $r$-th derivative has input |
| 7 | Output $r$-th Derivative | $y^{(r)} = L_f^r h(x) + L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)\, u$ | Input appears here |
| 8 | Linearizing Control Law | $u = \dfrac{-L_f^r h(x) + v}{L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)}$ | Decouples nonlinearity |
| 9 | Linearized I/O Equation | $y^{(r)} = v$ | Pure integrator chain |
| 10 | Coordinate Transform | $z = [h,\, L_f h,\, \dots,\, L_f^{r-1} h]^T$ | Yields Brunovsky form |
| 11 | Brunovsky Form | $\dot{z}_i = z_{i+1},\;\; \dot{z}_r = v$ | Companion canonical form |
| 12 | Zero Dynamics | $\dot{\eta} = q(0, \eta)$ | Internal dynamics under $y \equiv 0$ |
| 13 | Minimum Phase | $q(0, \eta)$ asymptotically stable | Required for closed-loop stability |
| 14 | Decoupling Matrix (MIMO) | $A(x) = E(x)^{-1},\;\; E_{ij} = L_{g_j} L_f^{r_i - 1} h_i$ | Inversion condition for MIMO |
| 15 | Nonlinear Controllability | $\text{rank}[g,\; ad_f g,\; \dots,\; ad_f^{n-1} g] = n$ | Input-state linearizability test |

| **Robotic Manipulators** | Joint-space decoupling, computed-torque control |
| **Aerial Vehicles** | Quadrotor attitude & position control |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Successive Output Derivatives

**System considered:**

$$\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\,u, \qquad y = h(x)$$

### 3.1.1 First Output Derivative

Differentiating $y = h(x)$ with respect to time using the chain rule:

$$\dot{y} = \frac{d h(x)}{dt} = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\, \dot{x}$$

Substituting $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\, u$:

$$\dot{y} = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\, f(x) + \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\, g(x)\, u$$

Recognizing the Lie derivative notation:

$$\dot{y} = L_f h(x) + L_g h(x)\, u$$

### 3.1.2 Second Output Derivative

Differentiating $\dot{y} = L_f h(x)$ (since $L_g h(x) = 0$ by the relative-degree-1 assumption):

$$\ddot{y} = \frac{\partial (L_f h)}{\partial x}\, \dot{x} = \frac{\partial (L_f h)}{\partial x}\, \left[f(x) + g(x) u\right]$$

$$\ddot{y} = L_f^2 h(x) + L_g L_f h(x)\, u$$

### 3.1.3 Inductive Step ($k$-th Derivative)

**Hypothesis:** If $L_g L_f^{i} h(x) = 0$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, k-2$, then:

$$y^{(k)} = L_f^k h(x) + L_g L_f^{k-1} h(x)\, u$$

**Proof by induction:** Assume the formula holds for $k-1$. Differentiate:

$$y^{(k)} = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[L_f^{k-1} h(x) + L_g L_f^{k-2} h(x)\, u\right]$$

Since $L_g L_f^{k-2} h(x) = 0$:

$$y^{(k)} = \frac{\partial (L_f^{k-1} h)}{\partial x}\, \dot{x}$$

$$y^{(k)} = \frac{\partial (L_f^{k-1} h)}{\partial x}\left[f(x) + g(x) u\right]$$

$$\boxed{y^{(k)} = L_f^k h(x) + L_g L_f^{k-1} h(x)\, u}$$

### 3.1.4 The $r$-th Derivative

At step $r$, by definition $L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x) \neq 0$. Therefore:

$$y^{(r)} = L_f^r h(x) + L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)\, u$$

## 3.2 Derivation: The Linearizing Control Law

Starting from $y^{(r)} = L_f^r h(x) + L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)\, u$, we **solve** for $u$:

$$L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)\, u = y^{(r)} - L_f^r h(x)$$

$$u = \frac{y^{(r)} - L_f^r h(x)}{L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)}$$

**Design choice:** We want the closed-loop to behave as a pure **integrator chain**, so we *prescribe* a desired linear behavior:

$$y^{(r)} \stackrel{!}{=} v$$

where $v$ is the new (auxiliary) control input. Substituting:

$$\boxed{u = \alpha(x) + \beta(x)\, v = \frac{-L_f^r h(x) + v}{L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)}}$$

with:

$$\alpha(x) = -\frac{L_f^r h(x)}{L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)}, \qquad \beta(x) = \frac{1}{L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)}$$

This control law is well-defined wherever $L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x) \neq 0$.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Inverted Pendulum on a Cart

**System dynamics:**

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = \frac{g}{l} \sin x_1 - \frac{1}{l} \cos x_1 \cdot u$$

**Output:** $y = h(x) = x_1$ (pendulum angle)

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Compute First Lie Derivative

$$L_f h(x) = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\, f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ \frac{g}{l}\sin x_1 - \frac{1}{l}\cos x_1\, u \end{bmatrix}$$

$$L_f h(x) = x_2$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Check $L_g h(x)$

$$L_g h(x) = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\, g(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -\frac{1}{l}\cos x_1 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compute Second Lie Derivative

$$L_f^2 h(x) = \frac{\partial (L_f h)}{\partial x}\, f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ \frac{g}{l}\sin x_1 - \frac{1}{l}\cos x_1\, u \end{bmatrix}$$

$$L_f^2 h(x) = \frac{g}{l}\sin x_1 - \frac{1}{l}\cos x_1\, u$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Check $L_g L_f h(x)$

$$L_g L_f h(x) = \frac{\partial (L_f h)}{\partial x}\, g(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -\frac{1}{l}\cos x_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$L_g L_f h(x) = -\frac{1}{l}\cos x_1$$

This is **non-zero** for $x_1 \neq \pm \pi/2$, so $r = 2$.

### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Construct Control Law

Since $r = n = 2$, full input-state linearization is achievable. From the general formula:

$$u = \frac{-L_f^2 h(x) + v}{L_g L_f h(x)} = \frac{-\left(\frac{g}{l}\sin x_1 - \frac{1}{l}\cos x_1\, u\right) + v}{-\frac{1}{l}\cos x_1}$$

Solving for $u$ explicitly:

$$u = \frac{g \tan x_1 + l\, v / \cos x_1}{1}$$

### 3.3.6 Step 6 — Verify Linearization

After substitution, the closed-loop satisfies:

$$\ddot{y} = \ddot{x}_1 = v$$

A **pure double integrator** — exactly the desired linear form.

### 3.3.7 Step 7 — Pole Placement

Choose $v = -k_1 y - k_2 \dot{y}$ to place closed-loop poles at $-s_1, -s_2$:

$$k_1 = s_1 s_2, \qquad k_2 = s_1 + s_2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the derivation:** Notice how every step explicitly cancels nonlinear terms ($\sin x_1$) and normalizes input gains ($\cos x_1$). This *exact* cancellation is what distinguishes feedback linearization from approximate methods.

## 3.4 Derivation: Zero Dynamics for $r < n$

Consider the system already transformed into the normal form:

$$\dot{z}_1 = z_2,\;\; \dot{z}_2 = z_3,\;\; \dots,\;\; \dot{z}_{r-1} = z_r,\;\; \dot{z}_r = L_f^r h(x) + L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)\, u$$

$$\dot{\eta}_1 = q_1(z, \eta),\;\; \dots,\;\; \dot{\eta}_{n-r} = q_{n-r}(z, \eta)$$

where $\eta$ coordinates are chosen such that $L_g \eta_i(x) = 0$.

**Zero-dynamics definition:** The **zero dynamics** are the internal dynamics that remain when the output is constrained to remain identically zero, i.e., $y(t) \equiv 0$ for all $t \geq 0$.

This implies $z_1 = z_2 = \dots = z_r = 0$. Substituting into the $\eta$ equations:

$$\boxed{\dot{\eta} = q(0, \eta)}$$

This autonomous system in $\eta$ is the **zero dynamics**. Its stability (asymptotic) is a prerequisite for closed-loop stability via input-output linearization.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Feedback Linearization Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["REFERENCE y_desired"] --> B["AUX CONTROL LAW v equals -k1 z1 minus k2 z2 ... minus kr zr"]
    B --> C["NONLINEAR FEEDBACK INVERSE u equals -Lfr h plus v divided by Lg Lfr-1 h"]
    C --> D["NONLINEAR PLANT f x plus g x times u, y equals h x"]
    D --> E["OUTPUT y"]
    D --> F["STATE FEEDBACK x measurement"]
    F --> C
    E --> G["LIE DERIVATIVE COMPUTATION Lf h, Lfr h, Lg Lfr-1 h"]
    G --> C
    G --> B
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["r s - desired reference"] --> B["Linear Controller Design: V s equals s to power r times R s minus pole placement compensation"]
    B --> C["Compute u from Lie bracket formula"]
    C --> D["Nonlinear Plant: x-dot equals f x plus g x times u"]
    D --> E["Output: y equals h x"]
    E --> F["Sensor feedback path"]
    F --> C
    D --> G["Internal Zero Dynamics: eta-dot equals q zero eta"]
    G -.->|"unstable internal mode"| H["MINIMUM PHASE CHECK"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Designing a Feedback Linearization Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given nonlinear system"] --> B["Step 1: Identify f x, g x, h x"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute Lie derivatives Lf h, Lg h"]
    C --> D{"Step 3: Is Lg h equal to zero?"}
    D -->|"Yes"| E["Compute Lf squared h and Lg Lf h"]
    E --> F{"Is Lg Lf h equal to zero?"}
    F -->|"Yes"| G["Continue until r-th derivative"]
    F -->|"No"| H["Relative degree r equals 2"]
    D -->|"No"| I["Relative degree r equals 1"]
    G --> J{"Lg Lf power r minus 1 h not equal to zero?"}
    J -->|"No"| K["STOP: System not feedback linearizable at this point"]
    J -->|"Yes"| L["Relative degree r identified"]
    I --> L
    H --> L
    L --> M{"Is r equal to n?"}
    M -->|"Yes"| N["FULL input-state linearization: choose v via pole placement"]
    M -->|"No"| O["PARTIAL linearization: analyze zero dynamics"]
    O --> P{"Zero dynamics asymptotically stable?"}
    P -->|"Yes"| Q["MINIMUM PHASE - proceed with v design"]
    P -->|"No"| R["NON-MINIMUM PHASE - redesign or use different output"]
    N --> S["END: Closed-loop linearized"]
    Q --> S
```

## 4.4 Transformation and Decomposition Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original coordinates x in R power n"] -->|"Lie-based diffeomorphism T of x"| B["New coordinates z, eta in R power n"]
    B --> C["External dynamics: z1 to zr, r-th order integrator chain"]
    B --> D["Internal dynamics: eta in R power n minus r"]
    C --> E["Driven by v after cancellation"]
    D --> F["Driven by zero-dynamics equation"]
    F --> G{"eta-dot equals q of zero, eta stable?"}
    G -->|"Yes"| H["MINIMUM PHASE System"]
    G -->|"No"| I["NON-MINIMUM PHASE System"]
```
