# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the concept of "relative degree" of a nonlinear system. Why is it important in feedback linearization?**

**Model Answer:**

The **relative degree** $r$ of a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) nonlinear system $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$, $y = h(x)$ at a point $x_0$ is the number of times the output $y$ must be differentiated with respect to time until the input $u$ appears explicitly with a non-zero coefficient.

Formally:

$$L_g L_f^{i} h(x) = 0, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \dots, r-2$$

$$L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x_0) \neq 0$$

**Importance in feedback linearization:**
1. **Determines the structure** of the linearized model (an $r$-th order integrator chain).
2. **Indicates solvability** — if $r$ is well-defined, the linearizing control law exists locally.
3. **Determines the order of internal (zero) dynamics** = $n - r$ that remain after linearization.
4. **Governs stability requirements** — when $r < n$, zero-dynamics stability must be verified.
5. **Quantifies the input–output "delay"** in the nonlinear system (analogue of relative degree in transfer functions).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $r$ with the system order $n$. Remember: $r \leq n$, and $r = n$ is required for **complete** (input–state) linearization.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define zero dynamics. Why is it essential to analyze zero dynamics in feedback linearization design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The **zero dynamics** of a nonlinear system are the internal dynamics that remain when the output is constrained to be identically zero ($y \equiv 0$) and the input is chosen to maintain this constraint.

Mathematically, after the normal-form transformation $(z, \eta)$ where $z = [h, L_f h, \dots, L_f^{r-1} h]^T$ and $\eta$ are complementary coordinates:

$$\dot{\eta} = q(0, \eta)$$

**Why it is essential:**
1. **Stability prerequisite:** If the zero dynamics are unstable, the closed-loop system is **internally unstable** even if the output tracks the reference perfectly.
2. **Minimum-phase classification:** A system whose zero dynamics are asymptotically stable is called **minimum phase**; otherwise, it is **non-minimum phase**.
3. **Design viability:** Only minimum-phase systems can be safely stabilized using input-output linearization alone.
4. **Hidden dynamics:** Zero dynamics are not directly observable in the output but can grow unbounded, causing physical damage (e.g., a manipulator swinging uncontrollably while its end-effector follows a trajectory).

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Zero dynamics are like the *unseen pendulum beneath the cart*. Even if the cart moves perfectly, an unstable hidden pendulum can wreck the system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the conditions for **input-state linearization** of a Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) nonlinear system $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$. State and explain the role of the **Frobenius Theorem** in this context.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider the SISO system with $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\, u, \qquad y = h(x)$$

**Step 2 — Coordinate Transformation:** We seek a diffeomorphism $z = T(x)$ that transforms the system into the **controllable canonical (Brunovsky) form**:

$$\dot{z}_1 = z_2, \;\; \dot{z}_2 = z_3, \;\; \dots, \;\; \dot{z}_{n-1} = z_n, \;\; \dot{z}_n = \alpha(x) + \beta(x)\, u$$

**Step 3 — First Coordinate Condition:** For $z_1 = T_1(x)$:

$$\dot{z}_1 = \frac{\partial T_1}{\partial x}\, \dot{x} = \frac{\partial T_1}{\partial x}\, f(x) + \frac{\partial T_1}{\partial x}\, g(x)\, u$$

For the Brunovsky form to hold, $\dot{z}_1$ must equal $z_2$, **independent of $u$**. Hence:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial T_1}{\partial x}\, g(x) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad L_g T_1(x) = 0}$$

**Step 4 — Successive Coordinates:** By induction:

$$T_2(x) = L_f T_1(x), \;\; T_3(x) = L_f T_2(x) = L_f^2 T_1(x), \;\; \dots, \;\; T_n(x) = L_f^{n-1} T_1(x)$$

with the chain of involutivity conditions:

$$L_g T_i(x) = 0, \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n-1$$

**Step 5 — Frobenius Theorem:** The conditions $L_g T_i(x) = 0$ require that $T_1(x)$ be **constant along the vector field $g(x)$**. The **Frobenius Theorem** guarantees that such a function $T_1(x)$ exists if and only if the distribution spanned by $\{g(x),\, ad_f g(x),\, \dots,\, ad_f^{n-2} g(x)\}$ is **involutive** (closed under Lie bracketing) in a neighborhood of $x_0$, where the **Lie bracket** is:

$$[f, g] = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\, f - \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\, g = ad_f g$$

**Step 6 — Controllability Test:** Combined with the **Lie algebra rank condition**:

$$\text{rank}\!\left[g(x),\; ad_f g(x),\; ad_f^2 g(x),\; \dots,\; ad_f^{n-1} g(x)\right] = n$$

If both conditions hold, the system is **input-state linearizable** at $x_0$, and the linearizing control law is:

$$u = \frac{-L_f^n T_1(x) + v}{L_g L_f^{n-1} T_1(x)}$$

yielding $\dot{z}_n = v$, i.e., pure integrator chain.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the nonlinear system:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_1^3 + \cos x_1 \cdot u$$

$$y = x_1$$

Design a **feedback linearization controller** to make $y$ track a reference signal $y_d(t)$. State the relative degree and the closed-loop dynamics.

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify $f(x)$ and $g(x)$:**

$$f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -x_1^3 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad g(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \cos x_1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad h(x) = x_1$$

**Step 2 — Compute Lie Derivatives:**

$$L_f h(x) = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\, f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -x_1^3 \end{bmatrix} = x_2$$

**Step 3 — Check $L_g h(x)$:**

$$L_g h(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \cos x_1 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compute Second Lie Derivative:**

$$L_f^2 h(x) = \frac{\partial (L_f h)}{\partial x}\, f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ -x_1^3 \end{bmatrix} = -x_1^3$$

**Step 5 — Check $L_g L_f h(x)$:**

$$L_g L_f h(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \cos x_1 \end{bmatrix} = \cos x_1$$

**Step 6 — Determine Relative Degree:**
Since $L_g h = 0$ and $L_g L_f h = \cos x_1 \neq 0$ for $x_1 \neq \pm \pi/2$, the **relative degree $r = 2$**. Also, since $r = n = 2$, **full input-state linearization** is achievable.

**Step 7 — Design Linearizing Control Law:**

$$u = \frac{-L_f^2 h(x) + v}{L_g L_f h(x)} = \frac{-(-x_1^3) + v}{\cos x_1} = \frac{x_1^3 + v}{\cos x_1}$$

**Step 8 — Choose Auxiliary Control $v$ for Tracking:**

To achieve $y(t) \to y_d(t)$ with second-order error dynamics, set:

$$v = \ddot{y}_d - k_1 (y - y_d) - k_2 (\dot{y} - \dot{y}_d)$$

Defining error $e = y - y_d$:

$$\ddot{e} + k_2 \dot{e} + k_1 e = 0$$

**Step 9 — Choose Gains (Pole Placement):** Select $k_1 = \omega_n^2$ and $k_2 = 2\zeta\omega_n$ for desired damping $\zeta$ and natural frequency $\omega_n$.

**Step 10 — Final Closed-Loop Dynamics:**

$$\ddot{y} = \ddot{y}_d - k_1(y - y_d) - k_2(\dot{y} - \dot{y}_d)$$

This is a stable linear tracking system with well-defined transient response.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are **zero dynamics**? Derive the zero dynamics for the following nonlinear system and comment on its stability:

$$\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2^2$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = x_2 + u$$

$$y = x_1$$

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify $f$, $g$, $h$:**

$$f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} -x_1 + x_2^2 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad g(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad h(x) = x_1$$

**Step 2 — Compute $L_f h$ and $L_g h$:**

$$L_f h(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -x_1 + x_2^2 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = -x_1 + x_2^2$$

$$L_g h(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $L_f^2 h$ and $L_g L_f h$:**

$$L_f^2 h(x) = \frac{\partial (L_f h)}{\partial x}\, f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 2x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -x_1 + x_2^2 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$L_f^2 h(x) = -(-x_1 + x_2^2) + 2x_2 \cdot x_2 = x_1 - x_2^2 + 2x_2^2 = x_1 + x_2^2$$

$$L_g L_f h(x) = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 2x_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 2x_2$$

**Step 4 — Relative Degree:** At $x_2 \neq 0$, $r = 2 = n$, so **full linearization** is possible. However, **at $x_2 = 0$**, the relative degree is not defined — singularity.

**Step 5 — Zero Dynamics via Algebraic Approach:** Since $r = n$ here, **zero dynamics are trivial** (the internal dynamics are empty). Hence no stability concern arises from zero dynamics.

**Comment on stability of full system:** Because $r = n$, input-output linearization directly leads to a globally defined linear controller wherever $x_2 \neq 0$. The control law:

$$u = \frac{-L_f^2 h + v}{L_g L_f h} = \frac{-(x_1 + x_2^2) + v}{2 x_2}$$

is well-defined except on the singular manifold $x_2 = 0$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram how **input-output linearization** differs from **input-state linearization**. Discuss the role of **minimum-phase** condition.

#### Model Solution:

**Difference between Input-Output and Input-State Linearization:**

| Aspect | Input-Output Linearization | Input-State Linearization |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Linearize the input–output map only | Linearize the full state dynamics |
| **Relative Degree** | $r \leq n$ | Requires $r = n$ |
| **Transformation** | $\dim(z) = r$, $\dim(\eta) = n - r$ | $\dim(z) = n$, no $\eta$ |
| **Internal Dynamics** | Present (zero dynamics) | Absent |
| **Closed-Loop Form** | $\dot{z}_1 = z_2,\; \dots,\; \dot{z}_r = v$, plus $\eta$-dynamics | Pure $n$-th order Brunovsky form |
| **Stability Concern** | Must check minimum-phase property | Automatic if controller stabilizes $z$ |

**Block Diagram Comparison:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IO["Input-Output Linearization r less than n"]
        A1["Reference yd"] --> B1["Aux Controller v"]
        B1 --> C1["FLC Law u from Lie derivatives"]
        C1 --> D1["Nonlinear Plant"]
        D1 --> E1["Output y"]
        D1 --> F1["ZERO DYNAMICS eta-dot equals q of zero eta"]
        F1 --> G1{"Minimum Phase?"}
    end

    subgraph IS["Input-State Linearization r equals n"]
        A2["Reference yd"] --> B2["Aux Controller v"]
        B2 --> C2["FLC Law u"]
        C2 --> D2["Nonlinear Plant"]
        D2 --> E2["All states tracked"]
        D2 --> F2["No internal dynamics remain"]
    end
```

**Role of the Minimum-Phase Condition:**

A system is **minimum phase** if its zero dynamics $\dot{\eta} = q(0, \eta)$ are **asymptotically stable** at the equilibrium $\eta = 0$.

1. **Internal Stability:** Although the output $y$ may track the reference perfectly, the unobserved states $\eta$ may diverge. Minimum-phase guarantees they remain bounded.

2. **Practical Implementation:** Non-minimum-phase systems (e.g., flexible-link manipulators, certain aircraft) cannot be stabilized by input-output linearization alone. Alternative strategies (output redefinition, sliding mode, backstepping) are required.

3. **Design Verifiability:** A Lyapunov function $V(\eta)$ satisfying $\dot{V}(\eta) = \nabla V \cdot q(0, \eta) < 0$ for $\eta \neq 0$ is the formal test.

4. **Trade-off Insight:** Sometimes designers relax tracking performance to recover stability via **singular perturbations** or **approximate I/O linearization**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Skipping the relative degree check:** Always verify $L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x) \neq 0$ before writing the control law. Marks are lost when this condition is silently assumed.
> 2. **Forgetting zero-dynamics stability:** In partial linearization ($r < n$), students often stop after designing $v$ without checking whether the zero dynamics are stable. The examiner expects explicit verification.
> 3. **Confusing $r$ with $n$:** The relative degree $r$ is *not* the system order. Students who equate them lose marks on synthesis questions.
> 4. **Algebraic errors in Lie derivatives:** A single mis-computed $L_f h$ propagates through the entire solution. KTU evaluators specifically watch for sign errors in $\partial h / \partial x$.
> 5. **Ignoring singularities:** Control laws like $u = \frac{v}{\cos x_1}$ are singular at $x_1 = \pm \pi/2$. State the region of validity explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feedback Linearization Control**

- **Core Idea:** Algebraically (not approximately) convert a nonlinear system into a linear one via coordinate change + nonlinear feedback.
- **Lie Derivative:** $L_f \lambda = \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial x} f(x)$; measures rate of change of $\lambda$ along trajectories of $f$.
- **Relative Degree $r$:** Smallest integer such that $L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x_0) \neq 0$ at $x_0$.
- **Output Derivatives:** $y^{(i)} = L_f^i h(x)$ for $i < r$; $y^{(r)} = L_f^r h + L_g L_f^{r-1} h \cdot u$.
- **Linearizing Control Law:** $u = \dfrac{-L_f^r h(x) + v}{L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x)}$.
- **Result:** Closed-loop behaves as an $r$-th order pure integrator chain: $y^{(r)} = v$.
- **Input-State Linearization:** Achieved when $r = n$ (no internal dynamics remain).
- **Zero Dynamics:** Internal dynamics under $y \equiv 0$; denoted $\dot{\eta} = q(0, \eta)$.
- **Minimum Phase:** Zero dynamics asymptotically stable — required for closed-loop stability when $r < n$.
- **Non-Minimum Phase:** Unstable zero dynamics — I/O linearization alone is insufficient.
- **Frobenius Theorem:** Guarantees existence of output function $h(x)$ making system linearizable.
- **Lie Algebra Rank Condition:** $\text{rank}[g, ad_f g, \dots, ad_f^{n-1} g] = n$ for full controllability.
- **MIMO Extension:** Use decoupling matrix $E(x)$ with $E_{ij} = L_{g_j} L_f^{r_i - 1} h_i$; require $\det E \neq 0$.
- **Region of Validity:** Defined by $L_g L_f^{r-1} h(x) \neq 0$.
- **Design Procedure:** (1) Compute $f, g, h$ → (2) Compute Lie derivatives → (3) Find $r$ → (4) Construct $u$ → (5) Design $v$ using linear methods → (6) Check zero dynamics if $r < n$.
- **Comparison with Jacobian Linearization:** Exact & global vs. approximate & local.
- **Real Applications:** Robotic manipulators, quadrotors, CSTR reactors, autonomous vehicles.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Confusing $r$ with $n$, ignoring singularities, skipping zero-dynamics check, sign errors in Lie derivatives.
- **Auxiliary Controller Design:** $v = y_d^{(r)} - \sum_{i=1}^{r} k_i (y^{(i-1)} - y_d^{(i-1)})$ for tracking.
- **Stability Proof:** Use Lyapunov function $V = e^T P e$ with $P > 0$ solving $A^T P + P A = -Q$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to SLAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIA & MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to SLAM # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's (robot's) location within that map. It is formally a continuous-state estimation problem solved using recursive Bayesian filtering over the joint posterior distribution of robot pose and landmark locations. In rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, SLAM is defined as the problem of estimating the posterior probability density function: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ where: - $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_t\}$ — the history of robot poses (path/trajectory) - $m = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_N\}$ — the map consisting of $N$ landmarks - $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_t\}$ — the history of sensor observations/measurements - $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_t\}$ — the history of control inputs (odometry) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Explorer Imagine you are placed blindfolded inside an unfamiliar building. You are given a **pedometer** (gives approximate step count) and a **measuring tape** (lets you estimate distances to walls by touching them). - **Localization** is like asking: *"Where am I inside this building right now?"* - **Mapping** is like asking: *"What does the floor plan of this building look like?"* Now here is the paradox: to know where you are, you need a map. But to build a map, you need to know where you are. **This is the famous "Chicken-and-Egg" problem of SLAM**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **SLAM Solves the Chicken-and-Egg Paradox** by treating localization and mapping as a *single, jointly-coupled estimation problem*. As the robot moves, every new sensor observation simultaneously updates both the belief about its pose **and** the belief about landmark locations — making the two estimates self-reinforcing over time. ## 1.3 The Two Sub-Problems Inside SLAM | Sub-Problem | Definition | Sensors Typically Used | |---|---|---| | **Localization** | Estimating robot pose $x_t$ given map $m$ | GPS, IMU, Wheel Odometry | | **Mapping** | Building map $m$ given known pose $x_t$ | LiDAR, Camera, Sonar | | **SLAM (Joint)** | Estimating **both simultaneously** without prior knowledge | Fusion of proprioceptive + exteroceptive | ## 1.4 Why SLAM is Hard (KTU Board Perspective) > [!IMPORTANT] > SLAM is fundamentally hard because of three compounding difficulties: > 1. **Sensor Noise** — every measurement $z_t$ is corrupted by noise. > 2. **Motion Uncertainty** — wheel slippage and drift cause odometry errors to accumulate unbounded. > 3. **Data Association Ambiguity** — the robot must correctly identify "is this landmark the same one I saw 10 seconds ago?" This is known as the **correspondence problem**. The most commonly cited reference metric in SLAM literature is the **RMSE (Root Mean Square Error)** of pose and landmark estimates, often reported in **meters** or **radians**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of the SLAM Problem SLAM is recursively decomposed into two fundamental probabilistic models: ### Step 1 — The Motion Model (Prediction Step) The motion model describes how the robot's pose evolves under a control input $u_t$, conditioned on the previous pose $x_{t-1}$: $$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$ This captures the **kinematics** of the robot (e.g., for a differential-drive robot, how wheel velocities translate to $(x, y, \theta)$ updates). ### Step 2 — The Observation Model (Measurement / Update Step) The observation model describes the probability of receiving a sensor reading $z_t$ given the current pose and a known map: $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$ For a range-bearing sensor (like 2D LiDAR), $z_t = (r, \phi)$ where $r$ is range and $\phi$ is bearing angle to landmark $m_i$. ### Step 3 — Bayesian Recursive Filter The full SLAM posterior is computed recursively using Bayes' rule: $$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \propto p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$ This equation is the **mathematical heart of every SLAM algorithm** — EKF-SLAM, Particle Filter SLAM, and Graph SLAM all solve this integral approximately. ## 2.2 Algorithmic Families of SLAM (KTU High-Yield Classification) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board-Favorite Classification** — Examiners often ask students to compare these families: | Algorithm Family | Core Idea | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---| | **EKF-SLAM** | Linearize motion/observation via Taylor expansion; use Kalman filter | Mathematically rigorous, optimal for Gaussians | $O(N^2)$ complexity, fragile under non-linearity | | **FastSLAM (Rao-Blackwellized PF)** | Particle filter for pose + independent EKFs per landmark | Handles non-linearity, $O(N \log N)$ | Particle depletion, many particles needed | | **Graph-SLAM** | Pose graph; solve via sparse linear algebra | Globally consistent, efficient | Requires loop closure detection | | **ORB-SLAM / Visual SLAM** | Camera features (ORB), bundle adjustment | Low-cost sensors, rich data | Sensitive to lighting, scale ambiguity | | **LIO-SAM / LiDAR SLAM** | Iterated EKF on LiDAR-IMU fusion | High accuracy, robust in darkness | Heavy computation, expensive sensor | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all formulas** examiners can realistically test for a 14-mark question on SLAM fundamentals. Every equation here is mathematically verifiable. | # | Formula | Name | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Full SLAM posterior | Probability density | | 2 | $p(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ | Online SLAM posterior | Marginalized over past poses | | 3 | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Motion model | Gaussian noise typically $N(0, R_t)$ | | 4 | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Observation model | Gaussian noise typically $N(0, Q_t)$ | | 5 | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t(z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m))$ | EKF-SLAM mean update | $K_t$ is Kalman gain | | 6 | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain equation | $Q_t$ = measurement noise covariance | | 7 | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$ | EKF-SLAM covariance prediction | $R_t$ = process noise covariance | | 8 | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | EKF-SLAM covariance update | Standard Kalman form | | 9 | $G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\big\vert_{(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)}$ | Motion model Jacobian | Linearization of $g(\cdot)$ | | 10 | $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\big\vert_{(\mu_t, m_t)}$ | Observation model Jacobian | Linearization of $h(\cdot)$ | | 11 | $g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + G_t(x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1})$ | 1st-order Taylor expansion | EKF linearization | | 12 | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}$ | Root Mean Square Error | Standard accuracy metric | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > SLAM is **not just an academic exercise**. It is the foundational technology behind: > - **Autonomous Vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo) — LiDAR + camera SLAM for urban mapping > - **Warehouse Robotics** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch) — Graph-SLAM for inventory navigation > - **Augmented Reality (AR)** (Apple ARKit, Google ARCore) — Visual-Inertial SLAM for placing virtual objects > - **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance)** — Visual SLAM for extraterrestrial terrain > - **Drones / UAVs** — VIO (Visual-Inertial Odometry) for GPS-denied environments In every one of these applications, the core mathematical machinery is identical to the recursive Bayesian formulation discussed above. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Rule to the Recursive SLAM Filter We begin with the joint posterior over the current pose, map, and entire history: $$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ Applying the definition of conditional probability (Bayes' rule): $$ p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})\, p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} $$ **Step 1 — Markov assumption on observations**: The current measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and map $m$, not on the entire history. Thus: $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Bayes expansion**: $$ p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\, p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} $$ **Step 3 — Marginalize over the previous pose** $x_{t-1}$ using the law of total probability: $$ p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1} $$ **Step 4 — Combine Steps 2 and 3**, yielding the **full recursive SLAM filter**: $$ \boxed{p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}} $$ where $\eta = 1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ is the normalization constant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for KTU Board**: The recursive structure means that once $p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})$ is known, we can compute the next posterior through only the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ and observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$. **This is why SLAM algorithms can run in real-time.** ## 3.2 Derivation: EKF-SLAM Linearization (Jacobian Form) EKF-SLAM applies the Extended Kalman Filter to the nonlinear SLAM problem by linearizing around the current mean estimate via **first-order Taylor expansion**. ### Step 1 — Motion Model Function Let $g(x_{t-1}, u_t)$ be the nonlinear motion model that returns the predicted pose $\bar{\mu}_t$: $$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$ ### Step 2 — Linearize $g$ around $\mu_{t-1}$ via Taylor expansion: $$ g(x_{t-1}, u_t) \approx g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) + \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)} (x_{t-1} - \mu_{t-1}) + \text{higher-order terms} $$ **Step 3 — Drop higher-order terms (first-order approximation)** and define the motion Jacobian: $$ G_t \triangleq \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)} $$ This yields the **EKF prediction step**: $$ \bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) $$ $$ \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t\, \Sigma_{t-1}\, G_t^T + R_t $$ **Step 4 — Linearize the observation model** $h(x_t, m_i)$ which returns predicted measurement for landmark $m_i$: $$ h(x_t, m_i) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i) + \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i)} (x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) $$ **Step 5 — Define the observation Jacobian**: $$ H_t \triangleq \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i)} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the Kalman Gain**: $$ K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} $$ **Step 7 — Update mean and covariance with actual measurement** $z_t$: $$ \mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \big( z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i) \big) $$ $$ \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\, \bar{\Sigma}_t $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight**: The Jacobian matrices $G_t$ and $H_t$ carry units — for 2D pose $(x, y, \theta)$, $G_t$ has dimensions $3 \times 3$, and $H_t$ is $2 \times 3$ for range-bearing sensors. The full EKF-SLAM covariance $\Sigma_t$ grows as $3 + 2N$ dimensional, where $N$ = number of landmarks. ## 3.3 Worked Example: 2D Range-Bearing Observation Model For a landmark $m_i = (m_{i,x}, m_{i,y})$ at true position, the range $r$ and bearing $\phi$ observed from pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$ are: $$ \delta_x = m_{i,x} - x $$ $$ \delta_y = m_{i,y} - y $$ $$ q = \delta_x^2 + \delta_y^2 $$ $$ r = \sqrt{q} = \sqrt{\delta_x^2 + \delta_y^2} $$ $$ \phi = \text{atan2}(\delta_y, \delta_x) - \theta $$ These are the components of $h(x_t, m_i)$. The observation Jacobian $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ becomes: $$ H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{\delta_x}{\sqrt{q}} & -\frac{\delta_y}{\sqrt{q}} & 0 \\[4pt] \frac{\delta_y}{q} & -\frac{\delta_x}{q} & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ This Jacobian appears in **every KTU 2024 EKF-SLAM numerical**. Memorize the structure. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: SLAM System Pipeline The following block diagram captures the complete functional architecture of a generic SLAM system, mapping the data flow between sensor inputs, estimation modules, and outputs. ```mermaid flowchart LR sensorA[Wheel Odometry
and IMU] --> motionMod[Motion Model
g x_t-1 u_t] sensorB[LiDAR or Camera
Sensor Suite] --> obsMod[Observation Model
h x_t m] motionMod --> predict[Prediction Step
x_bar_t and Sigma_bar_t] obsMod --> dataAssoc[Data Association
and Matching] dataAssoc --> update[Measurement Update
Kalman Gain K_t] predict --> update update --> stateEst[State Estimate
mu_t and Sigma_t] stateEst --> mapOut[Robot Pose
x_t] stateEst --> landmarkOut[Landmark Map
m equals m_1 ... m_N] stateEst --> loopClosure[Loop Closure
Detection Module] loopClosure -->|Yes| graphOpt[Graph Optimization
Pose Graph Solver] graphOpt --> stateEst loopClosure -->|No| stateEst ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: EKF-SLAM Algorithm Flow This diagram details the iterative prediction-update cycle that is the backbone of every EKF-based SLAM implementation. ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Initialize at t equals 0
mu_0 and Sigma_0]) --> readCtrl[Read control input u_t
from odometry] readCtrl --> predictPose[Predict pose
mu_bar_t equals g of mu_t-1 u_t] predictPose --> predictCov[Predict covariance
Sigma_bar_t equals G_t Sigma_t-1 G_t^T plus R_t] predictCov --> readObs[Read sensor observation z_t
from LiDAR or Camera] readObs --> compH[Compute Jacobian H_t
of observation model] compH --> compK[Compute Kalman Gain
K_t equals Sigma_bar_t H_t^T times H_t Sigma_bar_t H_t^T plus Q_t inverse] compK --> updateMu[Update mean estimate
mu_t equals mu_bar_t plus K_t times z_t minus h] updateMu --> updateSigma[Update covariance
Sigma_t equals I minus K_t H_t times Sigma_bar_t] updateSigma --> storeMap[Add or update landmark in map m] storeMap --> checkLoop{Loop Closure
Detected?} checkLoop -->|Yes| graphOpt[Trigger Graph Optimization
and re-linearize] checkLoop -->|No| nextStep graphOpt --> nextStep[Increment t equals t plus 1] nextStep --> readCtrl ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: SLAM Problem Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD slamProblem[SLAM Problem] --> probForm[Probabilistic Formulation
p of x_t m given z and u] probForm --> onlineSLAM[Online SLAM
p of x_t m given z_1t u_1t] probForm --> fullSLAM[Full SLAM
p of x_1t m given z_1t u_1t] onlineSLAM --> ekfSLAM[EKF-SLAM
Gaussian Linearization] onlineSLAM --> pfSLAM[Particle Filter SLAM
FastSLAM] onlineSLAM --> graphSLAM[Graph-Based SLAM
Pose Graph Optimization] fullSLAM --> batchSLAM[Batch Optimization
Bundle Adjustment] ekfSLAM --> ext[Extended Kalman Filter
Taylor Series] pfSLAM --> rbp[Rao-Blackwellized
Particle Filter] graphSLAM --> frontEnd[Front End
Odometry and Loop Closure] graphSLAM --> backEnd[Back End
Sparse Matrix Solver] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams**: Notice how every block ultimately feeds back into the central "State Estimate" — this feedback loop is what makes SLAM **self-correcting**. As the robot moves, observations constrain both pose and map estimates simultaneously, reducing uncertainty over time. This is the *theoretical* guarantee: in EKF-SLAM, the landmark covariance monotonically decreases as more observations arrive. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define SLAM. Why is it considered a "chicken-and-egg" problem? List two common sensor combinations used in SLAM.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** SLAM stands for **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping**. It is the computational problem of constructing a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within that map. Formally, it estimates the posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. > **Chicken-and-Egg Explanation (1 mark):** It is called a chicken-and-egg problem because to localize the robot, a map is needed, but to build the map, the robot's pose must be known. SLAM solves this paradox by jointly estimating both quantities in a single coupled probabilistic framework. > **Sensor Combinations (1 mark):** > 1. **LiDAR + Wheel Odometry + IMU** — popular in autonomous vehicles. > 2. **Mono/Stereo Camera + IMU** — used in Visual-Inertial SLAM (e.g., ORB-SLAM3). --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between the motion model and the observation model in SLAM. Give the probabilistic form of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Motion Model (1.5 marks):** The motion model describes the probabilistic evolution of the robot's pose given a control input. It is given by $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$, where $x_t$ is the new pose, $x_{t-1}$ is the previous pose, and $u_t$ is the control input (e.g., wheel encoder reading). It captures odometry noise and is typically modeled as Gaussian: $x_t \sim \mathcal{N}(g(x_{t-1}, u_t), R_t)$. > **Observation Model (1.5 marks):** The observation model describes the probability of a sensor measurement given the current pose and map. It is given by $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$, where $z_t$ is the measurement (range $r$ and bearing $\phi$ for LiDAR). It is also typically Gaussian: $z_t \sim \mathcal{N}(h(x_t, m_i), Q_t)$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Formulate the SLAM problem as a probabilistic state estimation problem. Derive the recursive Bayesian filter that estimates the joint posterior $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Clearly state the Markov assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an EKF-SLAM system. Derive the EKF prediction and update equations, defining the Jacobian matrices $G_t$ and $H_t$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a): **Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark):** Given a mobile robot moving through an unknown environment, the SLAM problem is to estimate the joint probability distribution over the robot's pose history $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$, given all sensor observations $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$. $$ p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) $$ **Step 2 — Two Key Assumptions (2 marks):** > **Assumption 1 — Markov property on motion:** The current pose $x_t$ depends only on the immediately preceding pose $x_{t-1}$ and the control input $u_t$, not on the entire history: $$p(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$ > **Assumption 2 — Conditional independence of observations:** The current observation $z_t$ depends only on the current pose $x_t$ and the map $m$, not on previous observations: $$p(z_t \mid x_t, m, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$ **Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Rule (2 marks):** $$ p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\, p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})} $$ **Step 4 — Marginalize over the previous pose (2 marks):** $$ p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1} $$ **Step 5 — Final Recursive Filter:** $$ \boxed{p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta\, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\, dx_{t-1}} $$ This equation is solved recursively — given the posterior at time $t-1$, we can compute the posterior at time $t$ using only the motion model and observation model. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Step 1 — EKF-SLAM Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** EKF-SLAM consists of three main modules connected in a feedback loop: 1. **Motion Update Module** — applies $g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ to predict the next pose and propagates covariance using $G_t$. 2. **Observation Module** — compares predicted sensor readings $h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i)$ with actual measurements $z_t$. 3. **Kalman Filter Update Module** — computes Kalman gain $K_t$ and updates both pose and landmark estimates. **Step 2 — Prediction Step Derivation (2 marks):** $$ \bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) $$ $$ \bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t\, \Sigma_{t-1}\, G_t^T + R_t $$ where $R_t$ is the process noise covariance, and the motion Jacobian is: $$ G_t = \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)} $$ **Step 3 — Observation Linearization (2 marks):** $$ h(x_t, m_i) \approx h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i) + H_t(x_t - \bar{\mu}_t) $$ with the observation Jacobian: $$ H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i)} $$ **Step 4 — Update Step Derivation (1 mark):** Kalman Gain: $$ K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} $$ Mean and Covariance Update: $$ \mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \big( z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t, m_i) \big) $$ $$ \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t)\, \bar{\Sigma}_t $$ --- ### Question B (Option 2): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the EKF-SLAM algorithm for a 2D environment with range-bearing sensors. Clearly derive the observation model $h(x_t, m_i)$ for a landmark $m_i = (m_{i,x}, m_{i,y})$ observed from pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)$, and compute the Jacobian $H_t$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM algorithms with respect to computational complexity, handling of non-linearity, and map representation. Mention one real-world application of each. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a): **Step 1 — Define variables (1 mark):** Let the robot pose be $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ and the landmark position be $m_i = (m_{i,x}, m_{i,y})^T$. **Step 2 — Relative position (1 mark):** $$ \delta_x = m_{i,x} - x $$ $$ \delta_y = m_{i,y} - y $$ $$ q = \delta_x^2 + \delta_y^2 $$ **Step 3 — Observation model $h(x_t, m_i)$ (2 marks):** The range-bearing observation model returns: $$ h(x_t, m_i) = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{q} \\ \text{atan2}(\delta_y, \delta_x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} $$ where $r$ is the Euclidean distance to the landmark, and $\phi$ is the bearing angle relative to the robot's heading. **Step 4 — Jacobian $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ (3 marks):** Differentiating $r$ and $\phi$ with respect to $(x, y, \theta)$: $$ \frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{-\delta_x}{\sqrt{q}}, \quad \frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = \frac{-\delta_y}{\sqrt{q}}, \quad \frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\delta_y}{q}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = \frac{-\delta_x}{q}, \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -1 $$ Hence: $$ \boxed{H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{\delta_x}{\sqrt{q}} & -\frac{\delta_y}{\sqrt{q}} & 0 \\[4pt] \frac{\delta_y}{q} & -\frac{\delta_x}{q} & -1 \end{bmatrix}} $$ This is the canonical Jacobian used in every KTU EKF-SLAM derivation. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Aspect | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM | |---|---|---|---| | **Computational Complexity** | $O(N^2)$ per step | $O(M \log N)$ per step | $O(N^3)$ but sparse, effectively $O(N)$ | | **Handling of Non-linearity** | First-order linearization (fragile) | Particle filter handles arbitrary non-linearities | Iterative optimization, robust | | **Map Representation** | Single big Gaussian over all landmarks | Independent Gaussians per landmark (factorized) | Pose graph + landmark nodes | | **Memory** | $O(N^2)$ | $O(M \cdot N)$ | $O(N)$ sparse matrix | | **Best Use Case** | Small maps, indoor robots | Non-linear sensors, medium maps | Large-scale, loop closure heavy | **Real-World Applications (2 marks):** - **EKF-SLAM:** Used in small indoor service robots (e.g., iRobot Roomba with simplified EKF). - **FastSLAM:** Used in research vehicles with non-linear tire slip models; foundational in self-driving car research. - **Graph-SLAM:** Used in Google Cartographer, an open-source LiDAR SLAM system deployed in warehouse robots. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in SLAM questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the normalization constant $\eta$** in the recursive filter equation — examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this omission. > 2. **Confusing Online SLAM vs Full SLAM**: Online SLAM marginalizes over past poses $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, while Full SLAM keeps the entire trajectory $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. > 3. **Writing $H_t$ without showing the chain rule application** — the differentiation of $\phi = \text{atan2}(\delta_y, \delta_x) - \theta$ must be shown step-by-step. Skipping derivation loses **2 marks**. > 4. **Forgetting units**: $G_t$ is dimensionless when pose is in $(x, y, \theta)$ but range-bearing Jacobians have units of $\text{m}^{-1}$ and radians. State units when relevant. > 5. **Misnaming "EKF-SLAM" as just "Kalman Filter"** — it is an *Extended* Kalman Filter applied to SLAM. Examiners are strict about this terminology. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Introduction to SLAM** - **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping — joint estimation of pose and map. - **Chicken-and-egg problem**: To localize, need map; to map, need localization. SLAM solves this by joint Bayesian estimation. - **Core formulation**: $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — joint posterior over trajectory and map. - **Online SLAM**: $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — marginalizes over past poses. - **Two key probability models**: - Motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ - Observation model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ - **Bayes' rule** is the heart of SLAM, applied recursively. - **Markov assumptions**: Current pose depends only on previous pose + control; current observation depends only on current pose + map. - **EKF-SLAM** linearizes $g(\cdot)$ and $h(\cdot)$ via Taylor expansion, then applies Kalman filter. - **Jacobians**: $G_t = \partial g / \partial x$ (motion), $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$ (observation). - **Kalman gain** $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$. - **Covariance update**: $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$. - **Range-bearing observation**: $r = \sqrt{q}$, $\phi = \text{atan2}(\delta_y, \delta_x) - \theta$. - **Three SLAM families** (high-yield for KTU): EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM. - **Real-world uses**: Autonomous vehicles, AR/VR, drones, warehouse robots, Mars rovers. - **Key challenge**: Data association / loop closure detection. > **End of Module 4 — Introduction to SLAM Notes** | *PCRAT502 — Advanced Topics in AI & ML | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Sensor Modalities for SLAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensor Modalities for SLAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Sensor Modalities for SLAM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Sensor Modalities for SLAM** refers to the systematic classification, modeling, and characterization of physical measurement devices (sensors) that provide the raw perceptual data required by a **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** algorithm. Each modality describes *what physical phenomenon is being measured* (range, bearing, intensity, acceleration, angular velocity, etc.), *the underlying noise model*, and *the resulting observation function* `h(x, m)` that maps the robot state `x` and the map `m` to a measurement `z`.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot's Senses"

Imagine a blindfolded person dropped into an unfamiliar building and asked to draw a floor plan while reporting where they stand at every instant. To succeed, they rely on several "modalities":

| Human Sense | Robot Equivalent | What it measures |
|---|---|---|
| Hearing echo of a clap | Sonar / Ultrasonic | Distance via **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** |
| Touching walls with a stick | 2D / 3D **LiDAR** | Precise range & bearing via laser pulse |
| Eyes (one) | Monocular camera | Bearing only (no scale) |
| Eyes (two) | Stereo / RGB-D camera | Bearing + scale / depth |
| Inner ear (balance) | **IMU** (Gyro + Accel) | Angular velocity & linear acceleration |
| Step counter / wheel rotation | Wheel **odometry** | Relative displacement |
| Cell phone tower triangulation | **GPS / GNSS** | Global position (outdoor) |
| Whiskers / contact switch | Tactile / Bumper sensors | Binary contact events |

A SLAM system must pick the right *combination* of these "senses." A drone has no wheels (no odometry), so it must lean heavily on **LiDAR + IMU + GPS fusion**. A self-driving car uses **multi-beam LiDAR + stereo cameras + radar + wheel odometry + GPS**, fused through an Extended Kalman Filter or a factor graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCRAT502, Module 4):**
> Students must be able to (i) classify sensors into *proprioceptive / exteroceptive* and *active / passive*, (ii) write the **probabilistic observation model** for at least one range sensor and one vision sensor, and (iii) justify *sensor fusion* strategies for typical robotic platforms.

### 1.2 Classification of Sensors

A sensor modality in SLAM is uniquely described by **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Information Source** → *Exteroceptive* (perceives environment: LiDAR, camera, GPS) vs *Proprioceptive* (perceives self-motion: IMU, wheel odometry, joint encoders).
2. **Energy Source** → *Active* (emits energy: LiDAR, sonar, radar) vs *Passive* (only receives: monocular camera, thermometer).
3. **Output Type** → *Range-bearing* (LiDAR, stereo), *bearing-only* (monocular), *range-only* (sonar), *inertial* (IMU), *pose* (GPS, odometry).

> [!TIP]
> **Metric to memorize (Board Favorite):** The **Cramer–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** on the variance of an unbiased range estimator from a pulsed active sensor with bandwidth `B` and SNR `ρ` scales as `σ²_r ∝ 1 / (B² · ρ)`. Doubling the signal bandwidth *quarters* the range variance — this is why **wide-bandwidth LiDAR** beats narrow-bandwidth sonar in precision.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars: Exteroceptive vs Proprioceptive

- **Exteroceptive sensors** answer: *"What does the world look like from here?"* They produce measurements `z_t` that depend on both the robot pose `x_t` and the map `m`. The observation model is `z_t = h(x_t, m) + ε_t`, with `ε_t` typically modeled as zero-mean Gaussian `N(0, Q_t)`.
- **Proprioceptive sensors** answer: *"How have I moved since the last instant?"* They produce *control* or *odometry* inputs `u_t` that depend only on `x_{t-1}` and `x_t`. The motion model is `x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + δ_t`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The SLAM Coupling:**
> Exteroceptive measurements *correct* the drift introduced by integrating proprioceptive measurements. **LiDAR + IMU fusion** in modern Visual-Inertial-LiDAR Odometry (VILO) is the canonical example — the IMU provides smooth high-rate motion (≥200 Hz), while LiDAR provides low-drift absolute corrections (10 Hz).

### 2.2 LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)

**Operational Principle:** A laser pulse is emitted; the time `τ` for the back-scattered photon to return is measured. The range is computed as `r = (c · τ) / 2`, where `c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s` is the speed of light.

**Sub-modalities:**
- **1D / 2D LiDAR** — single rotating beam, gives a 2D scan `(r_i, θ_i)` per sweep.
- **3D Multi-beam LiDAR** — stack of beams (16, 32, 64, 128 layers) gives a 3D point cloud.
- **Solid-state LiDAR** — no rotating parts, MEMS-based or flash LiDAR.
- **Solid-state Flash LiDAR** — illuminates the entire scene in a single pulse.

**Noise Model:** The range measurement `z_r` is modeled as

`z_r = r_true + b_r + n_r`,  with `n_r ~ N(0, σ_r²)`, `b_r` is a (possibly range-dependent) bias.

The standard deviation `σ_r` is typically **2 cm – 3 cm** for automotive LiDAR and **0.5 cm – 1 cm** for surveying-grade units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `σ_r` is range-dependent:**
> For a pulsed ToF system, the timing jitter `σ_τ` dominates, so `σ_r = (c/2)·σ_τ`. For triangulation-style LiDAR, `σ_r ∝ r²` due to the angular uncertainty in beam steering. KTU questions often test which regime a given LiDAR falls under.

### 2.3 Cameras (Monocular, Stereo, RGB-D, Event-based)

**Pinhole Projection Model:** A 3D landmark `X = (X, Y, Z)` in the camera frame projects to pixel coordinates `(u, v)` via

`λ [u, v, 1]ᵀ = K · [X, Y, Z]ᵀ`

where `K` is the **intrinsic matrix** and `λ = Z` is the projective depth.

**Stereo Depth:** With baseline `b` and focal length `f`, depth from disparity `d = u_L − u_R` is

`Z = f · b / d`

**RGB-D:** Active IR projector + IR camera (structured light, e.g., Kinect v1) or ToF (Kinect v2, Intel RealSense). Provides a registered RGB image and a per-pixel depth map. **Range: 0.3 m – 5 m**, accuracy degrades sharply with distance.

**Event Camera:** Asynchronous; each pixel independently reports a brightness change with microsecond latency. High dynamic range (**>120 dB**), no motion blur. Used in **Event-based Visual Odometry (EVO)**.

### 2.4 IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)

The IMU is a **6-axis proprioceptive sensor** combining:
- **3-axis Accelerometer** — measures specific force `a_m = a_true − g + b_a + n_a` (gravity is *subtracted* in the navigation frame).
- **3-axis Gyroscope** — measures angular velocity `ω_m = ω_true + b_g + n_g`.

`b_a`, `b_g` are **time-varying biases** driven by **random walks** (Brownian motion), `n_a`, `n_g` are zero-mean white Gaussian noise.

IMU outputs are integrated at high rate (typically **200 Hz – 1000 Hz**) to produce pose changes:

`p(t + Δt) = p(t) + v(t)Δt + ½ a(t) Δt²`
`v(t + Δt) = v(t) + a(t) Δt`
`q(t + Δt) = q(t) ⊗ exp(½ ω(t) Δt)`  (quaternion integration)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Integration Drift:** Because IMU measurements are integrated twice (position) or once (orientation), even tiny biases cause **unbounded drift**. A standalone IMU can hold pose for only **a few seconds** before the error exceeds useful bounds. *This is exactly why IMU is always fused with a global sensor in SLAM.*

### 2.5 Wheel Odometry

Wheel encoders report wheel rotation counts `n_L`, `n_R`. For a differential-drive robot with wheel radius `r` and baseline `b` (distance between wheels):

`Δs = (Δs_R + Δs_L) / 2 = π r (n_R + n_L) / N_enc`
`Δθ = (Δs_R − Δs_L) / b`
`Δx = Δs · cos(θ + Δθ/2)`
`Δy = Δs · sin(θ + Δθ/2)`
`θ_new = θ + Δθ`

**Sources of error:** wheel slip, uneven floor, encoder quantization, wheel deformation. Odometry error grows **unbounded** with distance — roughly **1% – 5% of distance traveled** for a well-calibrated indoor robot.

### 2.6 GPS / GNSS

Absolute global position with accuracy of **3 m – 5 m** (single-frequency L1 C/A code) down to **<1 cm** (RTK-GNSS with carrier-phase). **Cannot work indoors**, under heavy canopy, or near tall buildings (multipath). Provides global reference to close the **loop closure** drift of LiDAR/visual SLAM.

### 2.7 Sonar / Ultrasonic

Active acoustic range sensor using ToF of an ultrasonic chirp (typically **40 kHz – 200 kHz**). Range `r = (v_s · τ) / 2` with `v_s ≈ 343 m/s`. Inexpensive but **wide beam (~30°)** leads to poor angular resolution. Used mainly in low-cost indoor robots (e.g., Roomba) and underwater SLAM.

### 2.8 Radar

Active RF sensor operating at **24 GHz / 77 GHz / 79 GHz**. **Weather-robust** (rain, fog, dust), measures **Doppler velocity directly**. Low angular resolution but high range. Used in **automotive safety** and increasingly in **radar-inertial SLAM**.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LiDAR ToF range | `r = (c · τ) / 2` | `c` = speed of light, `τ` = round-trip time | metres (m) |
| 2 | LiDAR measurement likelihood | `p(z_r \vert r) = N(z_r; r, σ_r²)` | `σ_r` = range std-dev | m |
| 3 | Sonar ToF range | `r = (v_s · τ) / 2` | `v_s ≈ 343` m/s sound speed | m |
| 4 | Pinhole projection | `λ [u,v,1]ᵀ = K [X,Y,Z]ᵀ` | `K` = intrinsic matrix | pixels |
| 5 | Pixel coordinates | `u = f_x X/Z + c_x` , `v = f_y Y/Z + c_y` | `f` = focal length, `c` = principal pt | pixels |
| 6 | Stereo depth | `Z = f · b / d` | `b` = baseline, `d` = disparity | m |
| 7 | Camera bearing-only | `θ = arctan2(X, Z)` | azimuth to landmark | rad |
| 8 | Accelerometer model | `a_m = a − g + b_a + n_a` | `g ≈ 9.81` m/s² gravity | m/s² |
| 9 | Gyroscope model | `ω_m = ω + b_g + n_g` | bias `b_g` drifts via random walk | rad/s |
| 10 | IMU pos. integration | `p_{k+1} = p_k + v_k Δt + ½ a_k Δt²` | `Δt` = IMU period (~5 ms) | m |
| 11 | Wheel odometry Δs | `Δs = π r (n_R + n_L) / N` | `N` = encoder ticks/rev | m |
| 12 | Wheel odometry Δθ | `Δθ = (Δs_R − Δs_L) / b` | `b` = wheelbase | rad |
| 13 | Odometry Δx, Δy | `Δx = Δs cos(θ + Δθ/2)` | `θ` = current heading | m |
| 14 | Observation model (general) | `z = h(x, m) + ε` , `ε ~ N(0, Q)` | `Q` = measurement covariance | varies |
| 15 | Range CRLB | `σ_r ≥ c / (2B \sqrt{2 SNR})` | `B` = signal bandwidth | m |
| 16 | Radar Doppler velocity | `v_r = (f_d λ_r) / 2` | `f_d` = Doppler shift, `λ_r` = wavelength | m/s |
| 17 | GPS pseudorange | `ρ = c (t_recv − t_send)` | satellite–receiver time diff | m |
| 18 | EKF update weight (Kalman gain) | `K = P Hᵀ (H P Hᵀ + Q)⁻¹` | innovation covariance | – |
| 19 | Information form | `Ω = Hᵀ Q⁻¹ H` , `ξ = Hᵀ Q⁻¹ z` | used in factor-graph SLAM | – |
| 20 | Sensor fusion (complementary) | `x̂ = α x̂_prop + (1−α) x̂_extero` | `α` = filter coefficient | – |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The single most-tested equation in this module is `z = h(x, m) + ε`. Examiners love to ask: *"Given the LiDAR observation model, derive the Jacobian `H_k = ∂h/∂x` at the current state."* Practice this — it is the heart of EKF-SLAM.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — LiDAR Range Measurement Likelihood

**Goal:** Show that, under independent additive Gaussian noise, the likelihood of a 2D-LiDAR scan `(r, θ)` observing a known landmark at polar coordinates `(r_true, θ_true)` is Gaussian.

**Setup:** Robot pose `x_R = (x, y, θ)`. Landmark `m_i = (m_{i,x}, m_{i,y})`. The *true* range and bearing are

$$
r_{\text{true}} = \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y)^2}
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{true}} = \operatorname{atan2}\!\big(m_{i,y} - y,\; m_{i,x} - x\big) - \theta
$$

**Step 1 — Define the observation function `h(x_R, m_i)`:**

$$
h(x_R, m_i) = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y)^2} \\[4pt]
\operatorname{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y,\; m_{i,x} - x) - \theta
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Add Gaussian noise:** The actual measurement is `z = h(x_R, m_i) + ε`, with

$$
\varepsilon = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_r \\ \varepsilon_\phi \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; Q) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\; \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_r^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\phi^2 \end{bmatrix}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the likelihood:**

$$
p(z \mid x_R, m_i) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{\vert Q \vert}} \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(z - h(x_R, m_i))^\top Q^{-1} (z - h(x_R, m_i))\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting `Q` and expanding (component form):**

$$
p(z \mid x_R, m_i) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_r \sigma_\phi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_r - r)^2}{2\sigma_r^2} - \frac{(z_\phi - \phi)^2}{2\sigma_\phi^2}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Jacobian `H_k = ∂h/∂x_R`** (needed for the EKF update):

$$
H_k = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_R} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
-\Delta x / r & -\Delta y / r & 0 \\[3pt]
\phantom{-}\Delta y / r^2 & -\Delta x / r^2 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where `Δx = m_{i,x} − x` and `Δy = m_{i,y} − y`. This Jacobian is exactly what the EKF-SLAM Jacobian-of-the-observation step requires. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stereo Depth from Disparity

**Setup:** Two cameras separated by baseline `b`, both with focal length `f` (in pixels). A 3D point `P = (X, Y, Z)` projects to pixel `u_L` in the left image and `u_R` in the right image. The two cameras are perfectly rectified so that the **epipolar lines are horizontal**.

**Step 1 — Write the projection for each camera:**

$$
u_L = f \frac{X}{Z} + c_x, \qquad u_R = f \frac{X - b}{Z} + c_x
$$

Here we use the standard convention: `X`-axis points along the baseline, `Z` is the optical axis.

**Step 2 — Define disparity `d` as the horizontal pixel offset:**

$$
d = u_L - u_R = f \frac{X}{Z} - f \frac{X - b}{Z} = f \frac{b}{Z}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for depth `Z`:**

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the depth uncertainty** (propagate the disparity noise `σ_d`):

$$
\sigma_Z = \left\vert \frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} \right\vert \sigma_d = \frac{f \cdot b}{d^2}\, \sigma_d = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot b}\, \sigma_d
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering insight:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_Z \;\propto\; \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot b}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Stereo depth variance grows **quadratically with distance** and is **inversely proportional** to baseline `b` and focal length `f`. This is why:
> - **Far-field (<1 % of baseline)** measurements are useless for stereo.
> - **Wide-baseline stereo rigs** (e.g., 1 m baseline on an autonomous car) are mandatory for outdoor SLAM.
> - **Sub-pixel disparity estimation** is critical: a `0.1 px` improvement in `σ_d` gives a `10×` improvement in depth precision.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wheel Odometry Motion Model

**Setup:** A differential-drive robot with wheel radius `r`, wheelbase `b`, and encoder resolution `N` ticks per revolution. Encoder readings `(n_L, n_R)` from time `t−1` to `t` give wheel displacements `(Δs_L, Δs_R)`.

**Step 1 — Convert encoder ticks to wheel arc lengths:**

$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi r}{N}\, n_L, \qquad \Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi r}{N}\, n_R
$$

**Step 2 — Average forward translation and differential rotation:**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}, \qquad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new pose `(x', y', θ')` from `(x, y, θ)`:** the robot translates by `Δs` along its body frame, which is rotated by `θ + Δθ/2` in the world frame (the midpoint angle accounts for the rotation during the arc).

$$
x' = x + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
y' = y + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta + \frac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta' = \theta + \Delta \theta
$$

**Step 4 — Probabilistic form (the `u_t` is *noisy*):** in EKF-SLAM, the control input `u_t = (Δs_L, Δs_R)ᵀ` is perturbed by Gaussian noise `ε_t ~ N(0, U_t)`. The covariance `U_t` is the *Jacobian of `f`* with respect to `u_t`, multiplied by the encoder noise covariance:

$$
U_t = J_u \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_L^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_R^2 \end{bmatrix} \cdot J_u^\top, \qquad J_u = \frac{\partial (x', y', \theta')}{\partial (n_L, n_R)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — IMU Preintegration (Tightly-Coupled Visual-Inertial SLAM)

**Setup:** Two camera keyframes at times `i` and `j`, with IMU samples between them at `Δt = 1/200 s`. The IMU provides `(a_k, ω_k)` at every tick. The goal is to express the *relative* motion between frames as a function of the IMU measurements *only* — this is called **preintegration** (Forster et al., 2017).

**Step 1 — Discrete integration of accelerometer and gyroscope (from `i` to `j`):**

$$
\Delta v_{ij} = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1} \Delta R_{ik}\,(a_k - b_{a,k})\, \Delta t
$$

$$
\Delta p_{ij} = \sum_{k=i}^{j-1}\!\left[ \Delta v_{ik}\,\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta R_{ik}\,(a_k - b_{a,k})\,\Delta t^2 \right]
$$

$$
\Delta R_{ij} = \prod_{k=i}^{j-1} \exp\!\big((\omega_k - b_{g,k})\,\Delta t\big)
$$

**Step 2 — The key insight:** the *change of bias* (linearization point) is **factored out** of the sums, so the sums are computed once and the bias update is just a linear correction:

$$
\Delta R_{ij}(b_g) \approx \Delta R_{ij} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\partial \Delta R_{ij}}{\partial b_g}\, \delta b_g\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Residual for non-linear optimization (factor graph):**

$$
r_\mathcal{I} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
2\,\mathrm{vec}\!\left(\Delta R_{ij}^\top R_i^\top R_j\right) \\[3pt]
R_i^\top (p_j - p_i - v_i \Delta t_{ij}) - \Delta p_{ij} \\[3pt]
R_i^\top (v_j - v_i - g \Delta t_{ij}) - \Delta v_{ij}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This 9-dimensional residual is added to the bundle-adjustment cost function along with visual reprojection residuals. ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for SLAM:** Preintegration lets the optimizer run at **camera frame rate (10–30 Hz)** instead of IMU rate (200–1000 Hz), giving a **20× – 100× speedup** while retaining full coupling. This is the foundation of **VINS-Mono**, **ORB-SLAM3 (visual-inertial)**, and **OKVIS**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Sensor Modalities

| Modality | Type | Range | Accuracy | Drift | Cost | Works Indoor | Works Outdoor | All-weather | SLAM Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2D LiDAR | Active / Extero | 0.1 – 30 m | ±2 cm | None (per scan) | $$ | ✓ | ✓ | Poor (rain/fog) | Hector-SLAM, GMapping |
| 3D LiDAR | Active / Extero | 0.5 – 200 m | ±3 cm | None | $$$$ | ✓ | ✓ | Poor | LOAM, LIO-SAM |
| Mono Camera | Passive / Extero | 0.5 m – ∞ | bearing only | Scale ambiguity | $ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (with HDR) | ORB-SLAM, DSO |
| Stereo Camera | Passive / Extero | 0.5 – 50 m | ±5 cm (near) | Low | $$ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | S-PTAM, VINS-Fusion |
| RGB-D Camera | Active+Passive / Extero | 0.3 – 5 m | ±1 cm (near) | Low | $ | ✓ | ✗ | Limited | KinectFusion, Kintinuous |
| IMU | Proprio | n/a | drift 1°–10°/min | High (unbounded) | $ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Always fused (EKF/Graph) |
| Wheel Odometry | Proprio | n/a | ±1 – 5 % of dist. | High (unbounded) | $ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Cartographer (2D) |
| GPS / GNSS | Extero | global | 3 m – 1 cm (RTK) | None | $$ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | Global loop closure |
| Sonar | Active / Extero | 0.1 – 10 m | ±1 cm | Low | $ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | Underwater SLAM |
| Radar | Active / Extero | 0.5 – 250 m | ±0.1 m (range) | Low | $$ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (best!) | Radar-SLAM (Navtech) |
| Event Camera | Passive / Extero | 0.5 m – ∞ | bearing | Low (no motion blur) | $$ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ (HDR) | EVT-VIO, ESVO |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Sensor Modalities for SLAM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SENSOR MODALITIES FOR SLAM"]:::root
    A --> B["EXTEROCEPTIVE\nPerceives the environment"]
    A --> C["PROPRIOCEPTIVE\nPerceives self-motion"]

    B --> B1["ACTIVE\nEmits energy"]
    B --> B2["PASSIVE\nReceives ambient energy"]

    B1 --> B1a["LiDAR\nLaser pulse ToF"]
    B1 --> B1b["Sonar\nUltrasonic chirp"]
    B1 --> B1c["Radar\nRF Doppler"]
    B1 --> B1d["RGB-D IR projector\nStructured light / ToF"]

    B2 --> B2a["Monocular camera\nBearing only"]
    B2 --> B2b["Stereo camera\nBearing + disparity"]
    B2 --> B2c["Event camera\nAsync brightness change"]
    B2 --> B2d["GNSS / GPS\nSatellite ranging"]

    C --> C1["IMU\nAccel + Gyro"]
    C --> C2["Wheel encoders\nOptical / Hall"]
    C --> C3["Joint encoders\nArm manipulators"]

    B1a --> S1["SLAM Algorithm\nEKF / Graph / Particle"]
    B1b --> S1
    B1c --> S1
    B1d --> S1
    B2a --> S1
    B2b --> S1
    B2c --> S1
    B2d --> S1
    C1 --> S1
    C2 --> S1
    C3 --> S1

    S1 --> O1["POSE ESTIMATE\nx y theta"]
    S1 --> O2["MAP\nLandmarks / Occupancy / Point cloud"]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multi-Sensor SLAM Front-End

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENS["RAW SENSOR LAYER"]
        LIDAR["3D LiDAR\n10 Hz"]
        CAM["Stereo Camera\n30 Hz"]
        IMU["6-Axis IMU\n200 Hz"]
        ODOM["Wheel Encoders\n100 Hz"]
        GPS["RTK-GNSS\n5 Hz"]
    end

    subgraph PRE["PREPROCESSING"]
        LPF["Deskewing\nMotion compensation"]
        FEAT["Feature extraction\nORB / SIFT / SuperPoint"]
        INTP["IMU preintegration\nForster 2017"]
        OKF["Wheel odom integration"]
        GEOD["Geodetic to ENU\nconversion"]
    end

    subgraph FUS["FUSION LAYER"]
        EKF["EKF Predict\nIMU at 200 Hz"]
        UPD["EKF Update\nLiDAR + GPS at 10 Hz"]
        GRAPH["Factor graph\nBundle adjustment\ncamera rate"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUTS"]
        POSE["Robot pose\nx y z roll pitch yaw"]
        MAPM["Map\nPoint cloud / Landmarks"]
        LC["Loop closure\ndetection"]
    end

    LIDAR --> LPF --> EKF
    CAM --> FEAT --> GRAPH
    IMU --> INTP --> EKF
    IMU --> INTP --> GRAPH
    ODOM --> OKF --> EKF
    GPS --> GEOD --> UPD

    EKF --> UPD
    UPD --> POSE
    GRAPH --> POSE
    UPD --> MAPM
    POSE --> LC
    LC --> UPD
```

### 4.3 Information-Flow Diagram — Why We Need Sensor Fusion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["Single sensor limitation"]:::warn
    P --> P1["IMU only\nDrift grows quadratically\nin time, t^2 error"]
    P --> P2["LiDAR only\nNo motion between scans\nCannot predict at high rate"]
    P --> P3["Camera only\nScale ambiguity monocular\nDrift on textureless walls"]
    P --> P4["GPS only\nFails indoors\nMultipath in urban canyons"]

    F["FUSION"]:::good
    F --> F1["IMU + LiDAR = LIO\nHigh rate + low drift"]
    F --> F2["Camera + IMU = VIO\nVisual scale + inertial smoothness"]
    F --> F3["LiDAR + Camera = LIVO\nGeometric + semantic"]
    F --> F4["IMU + LiDAR + GPS = LIO-GPS\nGlobal reference + local precision"]
    F --> F5["Camera + IMU + GPS = VINS-GPS\nLow cost full pose"]

    classDef warn fill:#5a1f1f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef good fill:#1f5a2f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing a Sensor Stack for a Robotic Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Environment?"}:::q
    Q1 -->|Indoor| Q2{"Floor type?"}
    Q1 -->|Outdoor urban| Q3{"Budget?"}
    Q1 -->|Off-road| Q4{"Visibility?"}

    Q2 -->|Smooth| R1A["2D LiDAR + Wheel Odom + IMU\nGMapping / Cartographer"]
    Q2 -->|Uneven| R1B["3D LiDAR + IMU\nLOAM / LIO-SAM"]

    Q3 -->|Low| R2A["Stereo Camera + IMU + GPS\nORB-SLAM3-VI"]
    Q3 -->|High| R2B["128-layer LiDAR + Stereo + Radar + IMU + RTK-GPS\nFull-stack autonomous"]

    Q4 -->|Clear| R3A["Stereo + LiDAR + IMU + GPS\nMulti-modal SLAM"]
    Q4 -->|Fog / dust| R3B["Radar + IMU + GPS\nRadar-inertial SLAM"]

    classDef q fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a *node* in the information pipeline. The arrows show the *temporal/causal* flow of data. Notice that the **IMU** appears as an input to both the **EKF predict step** *and* the **factor graph** — this is the hallmark of **tightly-coupled fusion**, as opposed to *loosely-coupled* fusion where the IMU runs an independent filter whose output is later averaged with the LiDAR/camera filter.

---
