<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Classify the following sensors used in SLAM as *active/passive* and *proprioceptive/exteroceptive*: (i) 2D LiDAR, (ii) Monocular camera, (iii) IMU, (iv) RTK-GNSS, (v) Wheel encoder, (vi) Radar.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sensor | Active / Passive | Proprioceptive / Exteroceptive |
|---|---|---|
| 2D LiDAR | **Active** (emits laser pulse) | **Exteroceptive** |
| Monocular camera | **Passive** (ambient light) | **Exteroceptive** |
| IMU | **Passive** (no emission) | **Proprioceptive** |
| RTK-GNSS | **Active** (uses satellite signals) | **Exteroceptive** |
| Wheel encoder | **Passive** (no emission) | **Proprioceptive** |
| Radar | **Active** (emits RF) | **Exteroceptive** |

> **Examiner's note (3-mark weight):** A correct 6-row table with both classifications filled gets full marks. Half-marks if only one axis is answered.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**With the help of a suitable equation, explain the observation model of a 2D range-bearing LiDAR sensor. State the standard form of the likelihood.**

**Model Answer:**
The LiDAR measures range `r` and bearing `φ` to landmark `m_i` from pose `x_R = (x, y, θ)`:

$$
z = h(x_R, m_i) + \varepsilon = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y)^2} \\
\mathrm{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y,\; m_{i,x} - x) - \theta
\end{bmatrix} + \varepsilon
$$

Likelihood (assuming zero-mean Gaussian noise with covariance `Q = diag(σ_r², σ_φ²)`):

$$
p(z \mid x_R, m_i) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_r \sigma_\phi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_r - r)^2}{2\sigma_r^2} - \frac{(z_\phi - \phi)^2}{2\sigma_\phi^2}\right)
$$

Typical values: **σ_r = 0.02 m**, **σ_φ = 0.5° = 0.0087 rad** for a Hokuyo-class 2D LiDAR.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **stereo depth equation** `Z = f·b / d` from first principles, and show that the depth uncertainty grows as `σ_Z ∝ Z² / (f·b)`. Discuss the engineering implications for the design of a stereo SLAM rig on an autonomous car.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **monocular, stereo, and RGB-D cameras** as SLAM sensors across the four axes: (i) output type, (ii) scale observability, (iii) range and accuracy, (iv) computational cost. Identify the algorithmic implication of monocular scale ambiguity on initialization.

**Model Solution to (a):**

Starting from the rectified pinhole projection of a 3D point `P = (X, Y, Z)` into the left and right cameras with baseline `b`:

$$
u_L = f \frac{X}{Z} + c_x, \qquad u_R = f \frac{X - b}{Z} + c_x
$$

Disparity is the horizontal offset: `d = u_L − u_R = f·b / Z`, hence

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. `d` and using error propagation:

$$
\sigma_Z = \left\vert \frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} \right\vert \sigma_d = \frac{f \cdot b}{d^2} \sigma_d = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot b} \sigma_d
$$

**Engineering implications:**

- **Wide baseline required for outdoor SLAM.** For `Z = 50 m` and `f = 1000 px`, we need `b ≥ 0.5 m` to keep `σ_Z / Z < 1 %`.
- **Sub-pixel disparity estimation** is non-negotiable; `σ_d = 0.1 px` gives 10× better depth than `σ_d = 1 px`.
- **Calibration critical.** A 1 % error in baseline `b` causes 1 % bias in *every* depth — therefore stereo rigs must be re-calibrated after any mechanical shock.
- **Textureless regions** (white walls, sky) have ill-defined `d` → SLAM must degrade gracefully or use IMU.

**Model Solution to (b):**

| Axis | Monocular | Stereo | RGB-D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output | Bearing only (u, v) | Bearing + disparity (u, v, d) | Bearing + depth (u, v, Z) |
| Scale observability | **Not observable** from images alone; needs external metric prior | Observable (via `b`) | Observable (via IR) |
| Range & accuracy | ∞ range, infinite uncertainty (no scale) | 0.5–50 m, `σ_Z ∝ Z² / (f·b)` | 0.3–5 m, `σ_Z ≈ 1–2 cm` |
| Computational cost | Lowest (feature match only) | Medium (rectification + matching) | Lowest (sensor gives depth) |

**Algorithmic implication of monocular scale ambiguity:** Initialization of monocular SLAM (e.g., ORB-SLAM, DSO) cannot recover absolute scale. Two common fixes are (i) initialize scale from a known target / checkerboard, or (ii) **tightly couple with an IMU** — gravity direction breaks the gauge freedom and preintegration provides metric scale within one trajectory.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **wheel odometry motion model** for a differential-drive robot, starting from the encoder counts `(n_L, n_R)`, wheel radius `r`, and wheelbase `b`. Write down the Jacobian of the update function with respect to the control input — this is required for the EKF prediction step.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **IMU measurement model** for accelerometer and gyroscope. Define "bias random walk" and "white noise," and explain why a standalone IMU cannot serve as the only sensor in a long-duration SLAM system. What is the typical solution adopted in modern VIO pipelines?

**Model Solution to (a):**

Step 1 — Encoder counts to wheel arcs:

$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi r}{N}\, n_L, \qquad \Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi r}{N}\, n_R
$$

Step 2 — Average translation and differential rotation:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}, \qquad \Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}
$$

Step 3 — Pose update with midpoint angle:

$$
x' = x + \Delta s \cos\!\big(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\big)
$$

$$
y' = y + \Delta s \sin\!\big(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\big)
$$

$$
\theta' = \theta + \Delta \theta
$$

Step 4 — Jacobian of the update with respect to control `(Δs_R, Δs_L)` (or directly `(n_R, n_L)`):

$$
V_t = \frac{\partial (x', y', \theta')}{\partial (n_R, n_L)} = 
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\pi r}{N} \cos(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) - \frac{\pi r}{N}\frac{\Delta s}{b} \sin(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) & 
\frac{\pi r}{N} \cos(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) + \frac{\pi r}{N}\frac{\Delta s}{b} \sin(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) \\[4pt]
\frac{\pi r}{N} \sin(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) + \frac{\pi r}{N}\frac{\Delta s}{b} \cos(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) & 
\frac{\pi r}{N} \sin(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) - \frac{\pi r}{N}\frac{\Delta s}{b} \cos(\theta + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}) \\[4pt]
\frac{2\pi r}{N b} & -\frac{2\pi r}{N b}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The pose covariance is then updated as `P_{t+1} = F_t P_t F_tᵀ + V_t U_t V_tᵀ`, where `U_t = diag(σ_L², σ_R²)` is the encoder noise covariance.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Accelerometer model** (measures specific force, *not* free acceleration):

$$
a_m(t) = R_{WB}(t)^\top \big(a_W(t) - g_W\big) + b_a(t) + n_a(t)
$$

where `R_{WB}` is the rotation from world to body, `g_W ≈ [0, 0, 9.81]ᵀ` is gravity, `b_a` is the **accelerometer bias** and `n_a ~ N(0, σ_a² I)` is **white Gaussian noise**.

**Gyroscope model:**

$$
\omega_m(t) = \omega(t) + b_g(t) + n_g(t)
$$

**Bias random walk:** The biases are *not constant* — they evolve as **Brownian motion** (integrated white noise):

$$
\dot{b}_a(t) = n_{ba}(t), \qquad \dot{b}_g(t) = n_{bg}(t), \qquad n_{ba} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{ba}^2 I),\; n_{bg} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{bg}^2 I)
$$

So `b_a` and `b_g` are **state variables** that must be estimated on-line, not constants.

**Why IMU alone fails:** Integrating `ω` gives orientation error growing linearly with time; integrating `a` twice gives position error growing as `~t³` (for random walk on bias). After ~10 s, a MEMS-grade IMU has position error in the **tens of meters** — useless for SLAM.

**Solution in modern VIO (VINS-Mono, ORB-SLAM3-VI, OKVIS):** **Tightly-coupled visual-inertial fusion.** Visual observations (camera) provide *absolute* constraints (no drift) on the scale and position; the IMU provides *smooth high-rate* motion prediction. The two are coupled inside a **single optimization problem** (factor graph / sliding-window filter) with the IMU preintegration residual `r_𝓘` and the visual reprojection residual `r_𝓒` minimized jointly. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing exteroceptive vs active/passive** — these are *two independent axes*. A GPS is exteroceptive AND active. Most mistakes happen here.
> 2. **Forgetting units in stereo depth** — examiners explicitly deduct for missing `f` (px) and `b` (m) units. A dimensionless answer gets 0.
> 3. **Skipping the Jacobian** — in EKF-SLAM, a question that mentions "observation model" almost certainly wants `H = ∂h/∂x` next. Write it explicitly.
> 4. **Not addressing drift in IMU answer** — saying "IMU measures acceleration" without mentioning *bias random walk* and *unbounded drift* loses 2 of the 7 marks in part (b).
> 5. **Mixing coordinate frames** — pinhole projection expects coordinates in the *camera* frame, not the world frame. Always state the frame explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sensor modality** = a sensor's *physical principle + mathematical observation model + noise characteristics*.
- ✅ **Three classification axes:** exteroceptive vs proprioceptive, active vs passive, range-bearing vs bearing-only vs inertial vs pose.
- ✅ **LiDAR range equation:** `r = c·τ/2`, accuracy ~2 cm, dominant error source is timing jitter.
- ✅ **Pinhole camera equation:** `λ [u, v, 1]ᵀ = K [X, Y, Z]ᵀ`; monocular SLAM has *scale ambiguity*.
- ✅ **Stereo depth:** `Z = f·b / d` and **`σ_Z ∝ Z² / (f·b)`** — quadratic error growth with distance.
- ✅ **RGB-D:** excellent short-range (≤5 m) but fails outdoors; ideal for indoor Kinect-style SLAM.
- ✅ **IMU model:** `a_m = a − g + b_a + n_a`, `ω_m = ω + b_g + n_g`; biases evolve as **random walks**.
- ✅ **IMU integration drift:** position error grows as `~t³`; must always be fused with a global sensor.
- ✅ **IMU preintegration (Forster 2017):** keeps optimization at camera frame rate, not IMU rate.
- ✅ **Wheel odometry model:** `Δs = πr(n_R + n_L)/N`, `Δθ = (Δs_R − Δs_L)/b`; error ≈ 1–5 % of distance.
- ✅ **GPS pseudorange:** `ρ = c·Δt`; carrier-phase RTK gives cm-level; fails indoors.
- ✅ **Sonar range:** `r = v_s·τ/2`; wide beam, low angular resolution; for low-cost indoor & underwater.
- ✅ **Radar:** Doppler velocity `v_r = f_d·λ_r/2`; *best* all-weather; low angular resolution.
- ✅ **Observation model (universal):** `z = h(x, m) + ε`, `ε ~ N(0, Q)`.
- ✅ **Jacobian of range-bearing observation:** `H = [[−Δx/r, −Δy/r, 0], [Δy/r², −Δx/r², −1]]`.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion rationale:** *proprioceptive* sensors drift → *exteroceptive* sensors correct drift.
- ✅ **Tightly-coupled fusion** (e.g., VINS-Mono) > *loosely-coupled* (separate filters averaged later).
- ✅ **Sensors that are *active* + *exteroceptive*:** LiDAR, Sonar, Radar, RGB-D, GNSS receivers.
- ✅ **Sensors that are *passive* + *proprioceptive*:** IMU, wheel encoders, joint encoders.
- ✅ **The most-tested KTU equation this module:** the **stereo depth `Z = f·b / d`** and the **LiDAR observation Jacobian `H_k`**.
- ✅ **KTU Examiner's mantra:** "Write the model, write the noise, write the Jacobian — in that order."

> **End of Module 4 — Sensor Modalities for SLAM Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Framework for SLAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Framework for SLAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of SLAM

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the computational problem of constructing or updating a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously keeping track of an agent's (robot's) location within that map. The **probabilistic framework for SLAM** treats both the robot pose and the map elements as random variables governed by probability distributions, thereby explicitly modeling the **uncertainty** inherent in sensor measurements and actuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Probabilistic Formulation (KTU Syllabus Anchor):**
> The fundamental objective of probabilistic SLAM is to estimate the joint posterior probability density over the robot trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$, given all sensor observations $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$:
> $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

Where:
- $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_t\}$ — the **robot trajectory** (poses at each time step)
- $m$ — the **map** (collection of landmark positions or occupancy grid)
- $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_t\}$ — the **sensor measurements** (observations)
- $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_t\}$ — the **odometry / control inputs** (motion commands)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Explorer in a Dark Museum"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Callout:** Imagine a person (the robot) placed inside a large, completely dark museum with a dim flashlight. They cannot see the walls directly, but the flashlight reflects off paintings, pillars, and doorways (these reflections are the **observations $z_t$**). The person walks using a slightly faulty pedometer (the **odometry $u_t$**). As they walk, they must remember:
> 1. **Where they are** (localization).
> 2. **What they have seen and where** (mapping).
> 
> Every step they take introduces uncertainty in their position, but every reflection they observe provides a clue that *corrects* both their position belief and the map belief. The **probabilistic framework** is the mathematical "notebook" the person uses to keep track of *how confident* they are about every position and every landmark, refining these confidences as new evidence arrives.

## 1.3 Why Probabilistic? — The Uncertainty Problem

Robots operate in the real world where:
- **Sensor noise** corrupts measurements ($z_t$ is stochastic).
- **Actuator noise** corrupts motion ($x_t$ is stochastic).
- **Unknown environments** mean the map must be *inferred*, not given.

A purely deterministic approach fails because errors **accumulate without bound** (drift). Probability provides a mathematically rigorous way to fuse noisy information streams.

## 1.4 Key Probabilistic Components

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | Robot pose at time $t$ (position + orientation) | meters, radians |
| $m_i$ | $i$-th landmark position in map | meters |
| $u_t$ | Control input between $t-1$ and $t$ | m/s, rad/s |
| $z_t$ | Observation of a landmark at time $t$ | meters, radians |
| $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | **Motion model** (transition probability) | probability density |
| $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | **Observation model** (measurement likelihood) | probability density |
| **$\Sigma$** | Covariance matrix representing pose/map uncertainty | $m^2, m\cdot rad, rad^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Almost every SLAM algorithm in the syllabus is a different *implementation strategy* for the same posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — EKF-SLAM uses a Gaussian assumption, Particle Filter SLAM uses Monte Carlo sampling, and Graph SLAM optimizes a sparse graph of constraints.

## 1.5 The Three Fundamental SLAM Distributions

1. **Perceptual model** $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ — How a landmark looks from a given pose.
2. **Motion model** $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ — How the robot moves given a control input.
3. **The joint posterior** $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — The answer SLAM seeks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Recursive Bayesian Formulation of SLAM

The joint posterior is computed **recursively** using two alternating steps at every time step $t$:

### Step 1 — **Prediction (Time Update)**
Uses the motion model to propagate the previous belief through the motion:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

### Step 2 — **Measurement Update (Correction)**
Uses the observation model and Bayes' rule to correct the prediction:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

The denominator is a **normalizing constant** (often dropped in implementation):

$$p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int \int p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_t \, dm$$

## 2.2 The Three Classic Implementations

### A. EKF-SLAM (Extended Kalman Filter SLAM)
- **Assumption:** All distributions are **Gaussian**, all models are **linearizable**.
- State vector grows with map size: $\mu_t \in \mathbb{R}^{3+2N}$ for $N$ landmarks (2D pose + 2D landmarks).
- Pros: Optimal under Gaussian-linear assumptions. Cons: $O(N^2)$ cost, brittle to data-association errors.

### B. Particle Filter SLAM (FastSLAM)
- **Assumption:** The map can be **Rao-Blackwellized** — once the trajectory is known, landmarks are conditionally independent.
- Uses $M$ particles to represent $p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$; each particle carries its own EKF per landmark.
- Complexity: $O(M \log N)$ — far more scalable.

### C. Graph SLAM
- **Assumption:** The full trajectory is a graph; map and poses are nodes, observations/odometry are edges.
- Solves an **offline nonlinear least-squares** problem over all constraints.
- Complexity dominated by sparse linear-algebra solvers.

## 2.3 Key KTU Formulas / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **SLAM posterior (online)** | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Single-time-step form |
| **SLAM posterior (full)** | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Full-trajectory form |
| **Motion model** | $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | First-order Markov |
| **Observation model** | $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | Conditional on pose + map |
| **Bayes prediction** | $\bar{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Chapman–Kolmogorov |
| **Bayes update** | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \cdot \bar{\text{bel}}(x_t)$ | $\eta$ = normalizer |
| **EKF prediction** | $\bar{\mu}_t = g(u_t, \mu_{t-1})$ ; $\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^\top + R_t$ | $G_t$ = Jacobian of $g$ |
| **EKF update** | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| **EKF correction** | $\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t))$ ; $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t$ | |
| **FastSLAM factorization** | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(m_i \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$ | Rao–Blackwellization |
| **Graph SLAM objective** | $x^* = \arg\min_x \sum_t \Vert e_{u,t}(x_{t-1}, x_t) \Vert^2_{\Sigma} + \sum_{i,j} \Vert e_{z,i,j}(x_i, m_j) \Vert^2_{\Sigma}$ | Sum of squared errors |
| **Information matrix sparsity** | $\Omega = J^\top J$ where $J$ is sparse Jacobian | Graph is sparse |
| **Marginal posterior (landmark)** | $p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_{1:t}, m_i \mid \cdot) \, dx_{1:t} \, dm_{\neg i}$ | Integration step |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Boards:** When asked to "derive" the SLAM update, examiners expect you to **begin from the Bayes rule** $p(A\mid B) = \frac{p(B\mid A) p(A)}{p(B)}$, apply it to $A = (x_t, m)$ and $B = (z_t)$, then identify the motion model and observation model. Memorizing the final formula without showing the Bayes derivation loses 3–4 marks.

## 2.4 Why Probabilistic SLAM Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Self-driving cars use variants of EKF-SLAM and Graph SLAM to localize within HD maps.
> - **AR/VR Headsets:** Probabilistic SLAM powers inside-out tracking (Apple ARKit, Meta Quest).
> - **Warehouse Robotics:** Amazon Kiva robots use FastSLAM-style algorithms for inventory navigation.
> - **Planetary Rovers:** NASA's Mars rovers run Graph SLAM–based batch optimization for terrain mapping.

## 2.5 The Three Pillars of SLAM Posterior Structure

1. **Temporal Markov property** — Current state depends only on the previous state (not the entire history).
2. **Conditional independence of landmarks given the trajectory** — This is the *Rao-Blackwellization* insight.
3. **Sparse information matrix** — Only nearby measurements are correlated — exploited by Graph SLAM solvers.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The SLAM Posterior from First Principles (Full Trajectory)

**Goal:** Derive $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ using the chain rule of probability and Bayes' theorem.

### Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t} \mid x_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}$$

### Step 2 — Since the denominator does not depend on $x_{1:t}$ or $m$, it acts as a normalizer $\eta$:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t} \mid x_{1:t}, m) \cdot p(x_{1:t}, m)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the chain rule to the joint likelihood $p(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t} \mid x_{1:t}, m)$:

$$p(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t} \mid x_{1:t}, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{t} p(z_k \mid x_{1:k}, m, z_{1:k-1}, u_{1:k}) \cdot p(u_k \mid x_{1:k}, m, z_{1:k-1}, u_{1:k-1})$$

### Step 4 — Apply the **Markov assumption** (current measurement depends only on current pose and map, current control depends only on the previous pose):

$$p(z_k \mid x_{1:k}, m, z_{1:k-1}, u_{1:k}) = p(z_k \mid x_k, m)$$

$$p(u_k \mid x_{1:k}, m, z_{1:k-1}, u_{1:k-1}) = p(u_k \mid x_{k-1})$$

### Step 5 — Substitute back:

$$p(z_{1:t}, u_{1:t} \mid x_{1:t}, m) = \prod_{k=1}^{t} p(z_k \mid x_k, m) \cdot p(u_k \mid x_{k-1})$$

### Step 6 — Re-arrange the product to make the time-recursive structure visible:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(x_0) \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{t} \big[ p(x_k \mid x_{k-1}, u_k) \cdot p(z_k \mid x_k, m) \big] \cdot p(m)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This product form is the heart of online SLAM.** Every new control $u_k$ and every new observation $z_k$ multiplies the posterior by a single factor. The full posterior at time $t$ is the prior times the running product of all motion and observation likelihoods.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Recursive Bayes Filter for Online SLAM

**Goal:** Show that the full-trajectory posterior can be updated *online* using only the previous posterior at time $t-1$.

### Step 1 — Marginalize the trajectory $x_{1:t-1}$ from the previous posterior:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = \int p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{1:t-1}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the prediction formula by marginalizing out $x_{t-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \cdot p(x_{t-1}, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **prediction step** — it propagates uncertainty through the motion model.

### Step 3 — Apply Bayes' rule to introduce the new observation $z_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Define the normalization constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= \int \int p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \cdot p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_t \, dm
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical two-step Bayes filter for SLAM.** Every online SLAM algorithm (EKF, particle filter, etc.) is a different parameterization of these two integrals.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EKF-SLAM Update Equations

**Goal:** Derive the EKF correction equations for SLAM.

### Step 1 — Assume the prediction step yields a Gaussian:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) &= \mathcal{N}(\bar{\mu}_t, \bar{\Sigma}_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The state vector is $\mu_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t, m_{1,x}, m_{1,y}, m_{2,x}, m_{2,y}, \ldots]^\top$.

### Step 2 — Linearize the nonlinear observation model $h(\cdot)$ via first-order Taylor expansion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(\mu_t) &\approx h(\bar{\mu}_t) + H_t (\mu_t - \bar{\mu}_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mu_t} \bigg\vert_{\bar{\mu}_t}$ is the **Jacobian** evaluated at the predicted mean.

### Step 3 — The observation likelihood in Gaussian form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(z_t \mid x_t, m) &= \mathcal{N}(h(\mu_t), Q_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Combine the two Gaussians (prediction $\times$ likelihood) using the standard Kalman update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_t &= \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top (H_t \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1} \quad \text{(Kalman gain)} \\[4pt]
\mu_t &= \bar{\mu}_t + K_t \big( z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t) \big) \quad \text{(mean update)} \\[4pt]
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H_t) \bar{\Sigma}_t \quad \text{(covariance update)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "innovation" $z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t)$ is the surprise** — the discrepancy between what the sensor saw and what the model predicted. $K_t$ weights this surprise against the relative certainty of the model vs. the sensor.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Rao-Blackwellization — The Foundation of FastSLAM

**Goal:** Show that the SLAM posterior factorizes as a product of a trajectory posterior and independent landmark posteriors.

### Step 1 — Start from the SLAM posterior and condition on the trajectory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \cdot p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the identity $p(A, B) = p(A) p(B \mid A)$ applied to $A = x_{1:t}$ and $B = m$.

### Step 2 — Assume landmarks are **conditionally independent given the trajectory**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}) &= \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(m_i \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})
\end{aligned}
$$

This assumption is what makes SLAM tractable — without it, a single map with $N$ landmarks has a joint Gaussian of dimension $2N$.

### Step 3 — Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) &= p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(m_i \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This factorization is why FastSLAM works.** The trajectory is estimated with particles, and each particle carries $N$ small EKFs (one per landmark). Complexity drops from $O(N^2)$ to $O(M \log N)$ where $M$ is the number of particles.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Information Form and Sparsity of Graph SLAM

**Goal:** Show that the Graph SLAM information matrix $\Omega$ is sparse.

### Step 1 — The Graph SLAM objective is a least-squares minimization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= \arg\min_x \sum_{k} \Vert z_k - h_k(x) \Vert^2_{\Sigma_k}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Linearize around the current estimate $x^0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_k(x) &\approx h_k(x^0) + H_k (x - x^0)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and group the quadratic terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= \arg\min_x \sum_{k} (z_k - h_k(x^0) - H_k(x - x^0))^\top \Sigma_k^{-1} (z_k - h_k(x^0) - H_k(x - x^0))
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — The minimum of the quadratic form satisfies the normal equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Big( \sum_k H_k^\top \Sigma_k^{-1} H_k \Big) (x^* - x^0) &= \sum_k H_k^\top \Sigma_k^{-1} (z_k - h_k(x^0))
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Define the **information matrix** $\Omega$ and information vector $\xi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega &= \sum_k H_k^\top \Sigma_k^{-1} H_k \quad \text{(sparse!)} \\[4pt]
\xi &= \sum_k H_k^\top \Sigma_k^{-1} (z_k - h_k(x^0)) \quad \text{(vector)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve the sparse linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* &= x^0 + \Omega^{-1} \xi
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sparsity explanation:** Each constraint $h_k$ involves only the two poses it connects, so its Jacobian $H_k$ has nonzeros in only two columns. The resulting $\Omega$ is a **band-like sparse matrix** — sparse Cholesky factorization solves it in $O(N)$ time instead of $O(N^3)$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SLAM Algorithms

| Algorithm | Posterior Representation | Computational Complexity | Key Strength | Key Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EKF-SLAM** | Single big Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ over pose+map | $O(N^2)$ per step | Mathematically optimal under Gaussian assumption | $O(N^2)$ cost, fragile data association |
| **FastSLAM 1.0** | $M$ particles × $N$ independent Gaussians | $O(M \log N)$ | Highly scalable | Particle depletion problem |
| **FastSLAM 2.0** | Improved proposal distribution in particle filter | $O(M \log N)$ | Better particle diversity than 1.0 | Still uses particles |
| **Graph SLAM** | Sparse information matrix $\Omega$ | $O(N)$ sparse solve | Globally consistent, robust | Not online (batch) without sliding window |
| **ORB-SLAM** (visual) | Feature-based Graph SLAM with covisibility | $O(N \log N)$ | Real-time, robust visual | Needs texture, lighting |
| **LIDAR SLAM (LOAM)** | Iterative closest point + pose graph | $O(N)$ per sweep | High accuracy in 3D | Expensive sensor |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Probabilistic SLAM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Belief bel x0] --> B[Prediction Step]
    B --> C[Apply Motion Model p x_t given x_t-1, u_t]
    C --> D[Propagated Belief bar bel x_t]
    D --> E{New Observation z_t?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Compute Observation Likelihood p z_t given x_t, m]
    F --> G[Bayes Update with Normalizer eta]
    G --> H[Updated Belief bel x_t]
    E -- No --> H
    H --> I[Store Pose and Map Estimate]
    I --> J{Algorithm Type?}
    J --> K1[EKF SLAM: Update Single Gaussian]
    J --> K2[FastSLAM: Update M Particles with N EKFs]
    J --> K3[Graph SLAM: Add Constraint to Graph]
    K1 --> L[Output Pose and Map]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[Next Time Step t plus 1]
    M --> B
```

## 4.2 Bayes Filter Loop for SLAM (Recursive Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Prior bel x_t-1] --> Q[Prediction Integral]
    U[Control u_t] --> Q
    Q --> R[Predicted Belief bar bel x_t]
    R --> S[Multiply by Observation Likelihood]
    O[Observation z_t] --> S
    S --> T[Normalize by Evidence]
    T --> N[Posterior Belief bel x_t]
    N --> O2[Output Pose and Map]
    N --> P
```

## 4.3 Rao-Blackwellization Factorization (FastSLAM Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Joint SLAM Posterior p x_t, m given z, u] --> B[Factorize via Chain Rule]
    B --> C[Trajectory Posterior p x_t given z, u]
    B --> D[Map given Trajectory p m given x_t, z]
    C --> E[Particle Filter: M Particles]
    D --> F[Conditional Independence of Landmarks]
    F --> G1[Landmark 1 EKF]
    F --> G2[Landmark 2 EKF]
    F --> G3[Landmark N EKF]
    G1 --> H[Product of N Gaussians]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    E --> I[Full FastSLAM State]
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Graph SLAM Structure (Nodes and Edges)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0((Pose x_0)) ---|Motion Edge u_1| N1((Pose x_1))
    N1 ---|Motion Edge u_2| N2((Pose x_2))
    N2 ---|Motion Edge u_3| N3((Pose x_3))
    N1 -.->|Observation Edge z_a| M1((Landmark m_1))
    N2 -.->|Observation Edge z_b| M1
    N3 -.->|Observation Edge z_c| M2((Landmark m_2))
    N2 -.->|Observation Edge z_d| M2
```

## 4.5 EKF-SLAM Covariance Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Sigma_t-1: 3x3 Robot Block] --> B[Expand to 3+2N x 3+2N]
    B --> C[Predict Step: G_t Sigma_t-1 G_t transpose plus R_t]
    C --> D[Compute Kalman Gain K_t]
    D --> E[Innovation z_t minus h mu_t]
    E --> F[Update Mean mu_t and Covariance Sigma_t]
    F --> G{New Landmark Observed?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Augment State Vector and Covariance]
    G -- No --> I[Reuse Existing Landmark Block]
    H --> J[New Sigma_t with 3 plus 2N plus 2]
    I --> F
```

## 4.6 SLAM Algorithm Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need a SLAM Algorithm?] --> B{Environment Size?}
    B -- Small, Static --> C[EKF-SLAM]
    B -- Large, Online --> D{Linearizable?}
    B -- Large, Offline --> I[Graph SLAM]
    D -- Yes --> E[FastSLAM 2.0]
    D -- No --> F[UKF-SLAM]
    B -- Visual, Real-time --> G[ORB-SLAM]
    B -- 3D LIDAR --> H[LOAM / LIO-SAM]
```
