This expresses the joint posterior at time $t$ in terms of the joint posterior at time $t-1$, the transition density, and the likelihood.

**Step 2 — Apply Markov Property**

The state is assumed Markovian, so $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$. We substitute:

$$p(x_{0:t} \mid z_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})} \cdot p(x_{0:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Proposal (Importance) Distribution**

Since direct sampling from $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t})$ is generally impossible, we sample from a tractable proposal $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_t)$ and correct via importance weights:

$$w_t^{(i)} = \frac{p(x_{0:t}^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t})}{q(x_{0:t}^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t})}$$

**Step 4 — Recursive Factorization of Weights**

We factor the joint proposal and the joint posterior using the chain rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q(x_{0:t} \mid z_{1:t}) &= q(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t}) \cdot q(x_{0:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}) \\
p(x_{0:t} \mid z_{1:t}) &\propto p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) \cdot p(x_{0:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1})
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting and cancelling the $(t-1)$-terms yields the **recursive weight update**:

$$w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}) \, p(x_t^{(i)} \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)})}{q(x_t^{(i)} \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, z_t)}$$

**Step 5 — Bootstrap Filter Specialization**

Choosing $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_t) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$ (the transition prior) eliminates the denominator and the recursion collapses to:

$$w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$$

This is the most widely used variant in KTU problems and the foundation of the *Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR)* filter.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Sample Size

We define a coefficient of variation for the weights. Let $\text{Var}_q(w)$ denote the variance of the unnormalized weights.

**Step 1 — Express Variance of Normalized Weights**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}_q[W_t^2] &= \mathbb{E}_q \left[ \left( \frac{w_t}{\sum_j w_t^{(j)}} \right)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate $N_{\text{eff}}$ to Inverse Normalized Variance**

The standard derivation (see Doucet, de Freitas & Gordon, 2001) shows that

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N}{1 + \text{Var}_q(w_t^{(i)})}$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Estimate (Practical Formula)**

Since the true variance is unknown, we use the empirical estimate:

$$\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( W_t^{(i)} \right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{if} \quad \hat{N}_{\text{eff}} &< N_{\text{th}} = \frac{N}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Trigger Resampling} \\
\text{else} \quad & \Rightarrow \quad \text{Skip Resampling (Save Compute)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Systematic Resampling Algorithm (Deterministic, KTU-Favorite)

Given normalized weights $\{W^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$:

**Step 1 — Compute the Cumulative Sum Array**

$$c^{(i)} = \sum_{j=1}^{i} W^{(j)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

**Step 2 — Draw a Single Random Start**

$$u_1 \sim \mathcal{U}\left[0, \, \frac{1}{N}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Generate Equally Spaced Points**

$$u_k = u_1 + \frac{k-1}{N}, \quad k = 2, 3, \ldots, N$$

**Step 4 — Compare & Select**

For each $u_k$, find the smallest $i$ such that $c^{(i)} \geq u_k$. Duplicate particle $i$.

**Step 5 — Result**

The output is a new set of $N$ particles with uniform weights $W_t^{(i)} = \frac{1}{N}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Systematic is Preferred in KTU Problems**
> Systematic resampling has $\mathcal{O}(N)$ complexity and provably minimum variance among unbiased resampling schemes, making it the default choice in industrial particle filter implementations.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A robot's position is tracked using $N = 4$ particles. After prediction, particle positions and unnormalized likelihoods from a sensor reading are:

| Particle $i$ | $x_t^{(i)}$ | $p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $2.0$ | $0.1$ |
| 2 | $5.0$ | $0.6$ |
| 3 | $8.0$ | $0.2$ |
| 4 | $11.0$ | $0.1$ |

Assume prior weights $w_{t-1}^{(i)} = 1/4$ for all.

**Step 1 — Compute Unnormalized Weights**

$$w_t^{(i)} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$$

This gives $w_t = \{0.025, 0.150, 0.050, 0.025\}$.

**Step 2 — Normalize**

$$\sum_i w_t^{(i)} = 0.025 + 0.150 + 0.050 + 0.025 = 0.250$$

$$W_t^{(i)} = \frac{w_t^{(i)}}{0.250} = \{0.10, \, 0.60, \, 0.20, \, 0.10\}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MMSE Estimate**

$$\hat{x}_t = (0.10)(2) + (0.60)(5) + (0.20)(8) + (0.10)(11) = 0.2 + 3.0 + 1.6 + 1.1 = 5.9$$

**Step 4 — Effective Sample Size**

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{0.10^2 + 0.60^2 + 0.20^2 + 0.10^2} = \frac{1}{0.41} \approx 2.44$$

Since $N_{\text{eff}} = 2.44 < N_{\text{th}} = 2$, **resampling is triggered**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Particle Filter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the full lifecycle of a particle filter iteration, showing how the prediction, update, weight normalization, and resampling modules interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize N particles x0 with equal weights] --> B[Receive new measurement zt]
    B --> C[Prediction: Sample xt from transition prior p of xt given xt-1]
    C --> D[Update: Compute likelihood p of zt given xt for each particle]
    D --> E[Weight Update: wt proportional to wt-1 times likelihood]
    E --> F[Normalize Weights: Wt equals wt divided by sum of wt]
    F --> G[Compute Effective Sample Size Neff]
    G --> H{Is Neff less than N threshold?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Resample: Duplicate high-weight kill low-weight particles]
    H -- No --> J[Keep current particle set unchanged]
    I --> K[Reset all weights to 1 over N]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Estimate State: MMSE or MAP from particles]
    L --> M[Output state estimate and particles for next iteration]
    M --> B
```

## 4.2 Algorithmic Sequence Diagram (SIR Variant)

This diagram illustrates the temporal sequence of the **Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR) filter**, which is the most commonly examined variant in KTU question papers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Time Step t-1]
        A1[Particles Xt-1 with weights Wt-1]
    end
    subgraph S2[Time Step t]
        B1[Predict: Sample Xt from motion model] --> B2[Measure: Observe zt]
        B2 --> B3[Weight: Compute likelihood p of zt given Xt]
        B3 --> B4[Normalize: Sum and divide weights]
        B4 --> B5{Resample triggered?}
        B5 -- Yes --> B6[Systematic Resampling]
        B5 -- No --> B7[Skip Resampling]
        B6 --> B8[Output: Updated particles and estimate]
        B7 --> B8
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B8 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Bayesian Filter vs Particle Filter — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Bayesian Filter] --> Q1{Linear Gaussian?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Kalman Filter Optimal Closed Form]
    Q1 -- No --> R2[Particle Filter Sample Based Approximation]
    R2 --> S1[Handles non-linear dynamics]
    R2 --> S2[Handles non-Gaussian noise]
    R2 --> S3[Computationally parallelizable]
    R2 --> S4[Accuracy grows with particle count N]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**: The block-level architecture emphasizes that the *resampling decision* is a *gating mechanism* (diamond shape) — a typical KTU exam expects students to identify that resampling is conditional, not mandatory every step.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a Particle Filter. List any three applications where it is preferred over the Kalman Filter.*

**Model Answer:**

A Particle Filter is a Sequential Monte Carlo method that recursively approximates the posterior distribution $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t})$ of a hidden state using a finite set of weighted samples called particles. Each particle $x_t^{(i)}$ is a hypothesis of the true state and carries a normalized weight $W_t^{(i)}$.

**Three Applications where Particle Filter is preferred over Kalman Filter:**

1. **Robot Localization in Cluttered Indoor Environments** — The motion model and sensor likelihood are highly non-linear and multi-modal.
2. **Visual Object Tracking with Occlusions** — Non-Gaussian noise from partial occlusions breaks the Kalman Filter's linearity assumption.
3. **Financial Time-Series Forecasting** — Heavy-tailed, non-Gaussian return distributions violate Kalman assumptions.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]**
*What is the Effective Sample Size $N_{\text{eff}}$ in a Particle Filter? State the resampling decision rule.*

**Model Answer:**

Effective Sample Size is a measure of particle degeneracy that estimates how many "effective" independent samples are present in the weighted particle set.

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( W_t^{(i)} \right)^2}$$

**Decision Rule:** Resampling is triggered when $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{th}}$, where the standard threshold is $N_{\text{th}} = N/2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the recursive weight update equation for a generic Particle Filter using the importance sampling framework. Clearly state the role of the proposal distribution $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_t)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the *Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR)* algorithm step by step. How does it differ from a generic Particle Filter? Under what conditions does the SIR filter become equivalent to the generic bootstrap filter? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Recursive Weight Update**

Starting from the joint posterior factorization using Bayes' rule and the Markov property:

$$p(x_{0:t} \mid z_{1:t}) \propto p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{0:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1})$$

Since direct sampling from this posterior is intractable, we introduce an importance (proposal) distribution $q(x_{0:t} \mid z_{1:t})$ that factorizes as

$$q(x_{0:t} \mid z_{1:t}) = q(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t}) \, q(x_{0:t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1})$$

The importance weight is defined as the ratio of target to proposal:

$$w_t^{(i)} = \frac{p(x_{0:t}^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t})}{q(x_{0:t}^{(i)} \mid z_{1:t})}$$

Substituting the factorized forms and cancelling the $(t-1)$ terms yields:

$$w_t^{(i)} = w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}) \, p(x_t^{(i)} \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)})}{q(x_t^{(i)} \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)}, z_t)}$$

**Role of the Proposal Distribution $q(\cdot)$:** The proposal is the distribution from which we *actually draw* particles. The ideal proposal is the true posterior $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_t)$, but this is generally unknown. The closer $q$ is to the true posterior, the lower the variance of the importance weights. A poor choice leads to weight degeneracy and filter divergence.

**(b) SIR Algorithm Steps**

1. **Initialization**: Draw $x_0^{(i)} \sim p(x_0)$ and assign $w_0^{(i)} = 1/N$.
2. **Prediction**: For each $i$, sample $\tilde{x}_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}^{(i)})$.
3. **Weight Update**: Compute $w_t^{(i)} = p(z_t \mid \tilde{x}_t^{(i)})$.
4. **Normalize**: $W_t^{(i)} = w_t^{(i)} / \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{(j)}$.
5. **Resample**: Draw $N$ samples from $\{\tilde{x}_t^{(i)}\}$ with replacement, with probability $W_t^{(i)}$.
6. **Output Estimate**: $\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \tilde{x}_t^{(i)}$ (uniform weights after resampling).

**Difference from Generic Particle Filter:** The generic PF allows an arbitrary proposal $q(\cdot)$ and preserves non-uniform weights. SIR *always* resamples at every step, resetting weights to uniform $1/N$.

**Equivalence to Bootstrap Filter:** The SIR filter is equivalent to the generic bootstrap filter when the proposal $q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_t)$ is chosen to be the transition prior $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1})$, and resampling is performed at every iteration.

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** What is weight degeneracy in particle filters? Derive the expression for the Effective Sample Size $N_{\text{eff}}$ and explain its use in the resampling decision rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1D tracking problem with $N = 5$ particles having unnormalized weights $w = \{0.05, 0.25, 0.40, 0.20, 0.10\}$. Perform the following:
  (i) Normalize the weights.
  (ii) Compute the MMSE state estimate if the particle positions are $x = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$.
  (iii) Compute $N_{\text{eff}}$ and decide whether resampling is needed. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Weight Degeneracy and Effective Sample Size**

**Weight Degeneracy:** After a few iterations of Sequential Importance Sampling, almost all particles have negligible weights, and a single particle carries nearly all the probability mass. This phenomenon — called *weight degeneracy* — wastes computational effort on particles that contribute little to the posterior approximation.

**Derivation of $N_{\text{eff}}$:**

The effective sample size is defined as

$$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N}{1 + \text{Var}_q(w_t^{(i)})}$$

where $\text{Var}_q$ is the variance of the importance weights under the proposal distribution. Since the true variance is unknown, the empirical estimate is used:

$$\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( W_t^{(i)} \right)^2}$$

**Resampling Decision Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If} \;\; \hat{N}_{\text{eff}} &< N_{\text{th}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Trigger Resampling} \\
\text{If} \;\; \hat{N}_{\text{eff}} &\geq N_{\text{th}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Skip Resampling}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $N_{\text{th}} = N/2$ as a standard choice.

**(b) Numerical Computation**

**Step (i) — Normalization:**

$$\sum w = 0.05 + 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00$$

$$W = \{0.05, \, 0.25, \, 0.40, \, 0.20, \, 0.10\}$$

**Step (ii) — MMSE State Estimate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_t &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} W^{(i)} x^{(i)} \\
&= (0.05)(2) + (0.25)(4) + (0.40)(6) + (0.20)(8) + (0.10)(10) \\
&= 0.10 + 1.00 + 2.40 + 1.60 + 1.00 \\
&= 6.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Effective Sample Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \left( W^{(i)} \right)^2 &= 0.0025 + 0.0625 + 0.1600 + 0.0400 + 0.0100 = 0.2750 \\
\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{1}{0.2750} \approx 3.64
\end{aligned}
$$

**Threshold check:** $N_{\text{th}} = N/2 = 2.5$.

Since $\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = 3.64 > 2.5$, **resampling is NOT triggered** for this iteration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Particle Filter Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting to Normalize Weights**: The MMSE formula $\hat{x}_t = \sum W^{(i)} x^{(i)}$ requires *normalized* weights. Using unnormalized weights is an immediate 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing MAP and MMSE**: MAP uses $\arg\max$, MMSE uses weighted average. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Missing the Proposal in the Weight Formula**: Writing $w_t^{(i)} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$ without showing the recursive derivation loses method marks.
> 4. **Resampling Decision Confusion**: Students often resample at *every* step. The correct policy is *conditional* — only when $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{th}}$.
> 5. **Computational Complexity Omission**: Examiners award marks for noting that resampling is $\mathcal{O}(N)$ and that higher $N$ improves accuracy at linear cost.
> 6. **Confusing Particle Filter with Kalman Filter**: Particle Filter is *non-parametric* and sample-based; Kalman Filter is *parametric* (Gaussian). This is the most-tested conceptual distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Particle Filter**
> - A Particle Filter is a **non-parametric Bayesian filter** that approximates the posterior using weighted samples.
> - The three core operations are **Predict (sample from motion model), Update (compute likelihood), Resample (eliminate degeneracy)**.
> - The **weight update** in the bootstrap / SIR filter is $w_t^{(i)} \propto w_{t-1}^{(i)} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)})$.
> - **Effective Sample Size** $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum (W^{(i)})^2}$ quantifies degeneracy.
> - **Resampling threshold** is conventionally $N_{\text{th}} = N/2$.
> - **Systematic Resampling** is the lowest-variance scheme and is the KTU-preferred answer.
> - The MMSE estimate is $\hat{x}_t = \sum W^{(i)} x^{(i)}$; the MAP estimate is $\hat{x}_t^{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_i W^{(i)}$.
> - The Particle Filter **outperforms the Kalman Filter** when systems are non-linear and/or noise is non-Gaussian.
> - **Computational complexity** is $\mathcal{O}(N)$ per time step; accuracy improves as $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$.
> - Industrial applications include **robot localization, missile tracking, visual object tracking, SLAM, and biomedical signal processing**.
> - The filter is a **Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC)** method — keep this terminology ready for KTU viva and theory questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping Algorithms: Occupancy Grids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mapping Algorithms: Occupancy Grids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Occupancy Grid Mapping** is a probabilistic technique used in robotics to represent the robot's environment as a discretized grid of cells, where each cell stores the probability (or belief) that it is occupied by an obstacle, free space, or remains unknown.

Mathematically, given a sequence of sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and robot poses $x_{1:t}$, the posterior probability over the map $m$ is decomposed as a product of independent cell probabilities:

$$P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

where $m_i$ is the state (occupied or free) of the $i$-th grid cell, and $N$ is the total number of cells in the grid.

The occupancy grid was first formally proposed by **Hans Moravec and Alberto Elfes in 1985** as part of their work on certainty grids for spatial reasoning in mobile robots.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine you are exploring a dark, unfamiliar room with a flashlight.**
> 
> 1. You mentally divide the floor into small tiles (like a chessboard).
> 2. Every time your flashlight beam hits a wall or furniture, you **paint that tile as "probably occupied"**.
> 3. Every time the beam clearly travels *through* a tile without hitting anything, you **paint it as "probably free"**.
> 4. Tiles you have not illuminated yet remain **"unknown"** (grey).
> 5. The more times you confirm a tile's status from different angles, the more confident you become about its label.

This is exactly how an occupancy grid works — except the robot uses lasers, sonar, or depth cameras instead of a flashlight, and uses **Bayesian probability updates** instead of just painting.

## 1.3 Three-State Representation

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often test the **three-state model**: *Free*, *Occupied*, and *Unknown*. The "Unknown" state is what distinguishes occupancy grids from a simple binary obstacle map.

| Grid Cell State | Probability Range | Visual Color (Typical) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free** | $P(m_i) < 0.5$ | White | Cell is traversable space |
| **Occupied** | $P(m_i) \geq 0.5$ | Black | Cell contains an obstacle |
| **Unknown** | $P(m_i) \approx 0.5$ | Grey | Cell has not been observed yet |

## 1.4 Standard Metric & Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most commonly used metrics are:
> - **Cell resolution (r):** typically **0.05 m to 0.5 m** per cell.
> - **Map dimensions:** For a 50 m × 50 m warehouse with 0.1 m resolution, the grid contains **$500 \times 500 = 250{,}000$ cells**.
> - **Log-odds range:** bounded in practice between **$l_{min} = -2$** and **$l_{max} = 3.5$** to prevent overconfidence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Occupancy Grids? — The Core Motivation

Traditional geometric maps fail because:
1. **Sensor noise** creates spurious reflections.
2. **Dynamic environments** change over time.
3. **Limited sensor range** leaves large unknown regions.
4. **Pose uncertainty** corrupts map alignment.

Occupancy grids solve this by **explicitly modelling uncertainty** as a probability per cell.

## 2.2 The Mapping Problem — Decomposition

The full SLAM problem is intractable because the map space is enormous. Occupancy grids make the simplifying assumption of **cell independence**:

$$P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

> [!NOTE]
> This decomposition is the **key trick** that makes grid mapping computationally feasible. Without it, we would have to reason over $2^N$ possible maps.

## 2.3 Bayesian Update for a Single Cell

For a single cell $m_i$, the Bayesian update given a new measurement $z_t$ at pose $x_t$ is:

$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

This is the standard recursive Bayes filter applied to a static cell.

## 2.4 Log-Odds Representation (Numerical Stability Trick)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Direct multiplication of many small probabilities causes **numerical underflow**. The solution is the **log-odds** representation.

The log-odds of a cell are defined as:

$$l(m_i) = \log \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}$$

The inverse transform is:

$$P(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l(m_i)}}$$

The Bayesian update becomes an elegant **addition**:

$$l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \underbrace{l(z_t, x_t, m_i)}_{\text{inverse sensor model}} - l_0$$

where:
- $l_{t-1}(m_i)$ is the previous log-odds value of cell $i$,
- $l(z_t, x_t, m_i)$ is the log-odds contributed by the new measurement (from the **inverse sensor model**),
- $l_0$ is the prior log-odds (typically 0 for $P = 0.5$).

> [!TIP]
> This additive form is why log-odds is the **standard implementation** in ROS (Robot Operating System) using the `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` message and the `mapping` package.

## 2.5 Inverse Sensor Model

The **inverse sensor model** $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ returns the probability that cell $m_i$ is occupied, given a single measurement $z_t$. For a **range-bearing sensor** (e.g., lidar), the typical piecewise inverse model is:

$$
P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \begin{cases}
0.0 & \text{if } 0 \leq d < z_t - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} \quad \text{(clearly free along the ray)} \\
0.5 & \text{if } z_t - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} < d < z_t + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} \quad \text{(in the uncertainty band)} \\
1.0 & \text{if } z_t + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} < d < z_{\max} \quad \text{(just past the hit — obstacle likely)} \\
0.5 & \text{if } d \geq z_{\max} \quad \text{(max range — no return)} \\
\end{cases}
$$

where $d$ is the distance from the sensor to the cell centre, $\Delta z$ is the range uncertainty, and $z_{\max}$ is the sensor's maximum reliable range.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the formulas in this table** — they appear every year in KTU Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | Cell-independence decomposition | dimensionless |
| 2 | $l(m_i) = \log \dfrac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}$ | Log-odds transform | nats or bits |
| 3 | $P(m_i) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{l(m_i)}}$ | Sigmoid inverse transform | dimensionless |
| 4 | $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{inv}}(m_i, z_t, x_t) - l_0$ | Recursive log-odds update | nats or bits |
| 5 | $H(P) = -P \log P - (1-P) \log(1-P)$ | Map entropy (uncertainty measure) | bits |
| 6 | $N_{\text{cells}} = \lceil L/r \rceil \times \lceil W/r \rceil$ | Total cells for an $L \times W$ map at resolution $r$ | cells |
| 7 | $d_{i,j} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Euclidean distance sensor-to-cell | metres |
| 8 | $P_{\text{occ}}(m_i) \leftarrow \min(l_{\max}, \max(l_{\min}, \text{update}))$ | Clamping for numerical safety | nats or bits |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> Occupancy grids are deployed in:
> - **Autonomous cars** (e.g., Waymo, Tesla) for obstacle representation.
> - **AGVs in warehouses** (Amazon Kiva robots).
> - **Domestic vacuum robots** (iRobot Roomba).
> - **Drone navigation** in GPS-denied environments.
> - **Mars rovers** (NASA Curiosity and Perseverance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Bayes Rule to Log-Odds Update

**Step 1 — Start with Bayes rule for a static cell:**

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the same rule to the complementary hypothesis "$m_i$ is free":**

$$
P(\bar{m}_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid \bar{m}_i, x_t) \, P(\bar{m}_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide the two equations. The intractable denominator cancels:**

$$
\frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{P(\bar{m}_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{P(z_t \mid \bar{m}_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(\bar{m}_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
\log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{P(\bar{m}_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \log \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{P(z_t \mid \bar{m}_i, x_t)} + \log \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(\bar{m}_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognise log-odds terms:**

$$
l_t(m_i) = l_{\text{inv}}(m_i, z_t, x_t) + l_{t-1}(m_i)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows the recursive additive form. Each new measurement simply **adds evidence** to the existing belief — no multiplication, no overflow.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sigmoid Inverse of Log-Odds

Starting from $l = \log \frac{P}{1-P}$, solve for $P$:

**Step 1 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$
e^l = \frac{P}{1-P}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $(1-P)$:**

$$
e^l (1 - P) = P
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and collect $P$ terms:**

$$
e^l - e^l \cdot P = P
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$
e^l = P + e^l \cdot P = P(1 + e^l)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $P$:**

$$
P = \frac{e^l}{1 + e^l} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^l}
$$

This is the **logistic sigmoid** function.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Question:** A robot's lidar returns a hit at distance **$z_t = 2.0$ m** along a ray. The inverse sensor model gives a log-odds contribution of **$l_{\text{inv}} = +0.85$** for the cell at the hit, and **$l_{\text{inv}} = -0.40$** for the cells along the free ray. The cell at the hit has previous log-odds $l_{t-1} = 0.0$. Prior log-odds $l_0 = 0$. Find the updated probability of occupancy.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply update for the hit cell:**

$$
l_t(m_{\text{hit}}) = l_{t-1}(m_{\text{hit}}) + l_{\text{inv}} - l_0 = 0.0 + 0.85 - 0 = 0.85
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to probability via sigmoid:**

$$
P(m_{\text{hit}}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.85}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 2.3396} = 1 - 0.2994 = 0.7006
$$

**Step 3 — For a free cell along the same ray with prior $l_{t-1} = 0.2$:**

$$
l_t(m_{\text{free}}) = 0.2 + (-0.40) - 0 = -0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to probability:**

$$
P(m_{\text{free}}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.20}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 0.8187} = 1 - 0.5498 = 0.4502
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The hit cell is now **70.06% likely occupied**, and the free cell is **44.98% likely occupied** (i.e., 55.02% likely free). With more measurements, both converge further to their true states.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Algorithm Pseudocode (Plain English Only)

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm below is presented in **structured plain English** because the KTU 2024 syllabus for this module is theory-focused. No programming language code is used.

**Algorithm: Occupancy Grid Mapping (Offline, Known Poses)**

1. **Input:** Grid size $G$, resolution $r$, prior log-odds $l_0$, clamping bounds $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$, sensor measurements $z_{1:T}$, poses $x_{1:T}$.

2. **Initialise:** For every cell $m_i$ in $G$, set $l_0(m_i) = 0$ (i.e., $P = 0.5$, unknown).

3. **For** each time step $t = 1$ to $T$ **do:**
   - Retrieve robot pose $x_t$ and measurement $z_t$.
   - Cast a ray from $x_t$ through each angular beam of $z_t$.
   - **For** every cell traversed by the ray **do:**
     - Compute distance $d$ from sensor origin to cell centre.
     - **If** $d$ is in the **free zone** (before the hit distance minus half the range uncertainty):
       - Add the free-space log-odds increment $l_{\text{free}}$ to $l_t(m_i)$.
     - **Else if** $d$ is in the **uncertainty band** (within $\pm \tfrac{\Delta z}{2}$ of the hit):
       - Skip (leave the cell unchanged, or add 0).
     - **Else if** $d$ is just past the hit (the obstacle region):
       - Add the occupied log-odds increment $l_{\text{occ}}$ to $l_t(m_i)$.
     - **Else** ($d \geq z_{\max}$, no return):
       - Skip.
   - **Clamp** the updated log-odds to $[l_{\min}, l_{\max}]$.

4. **Output:** The final occupancy grid as a 2-D array of $P(m_i)$ values.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing the **inverse sensor model** (cell ← measurement) with the **forward sensor model** (measurement ← cell). The inverse model is what we use for mapping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Occupancy Grid Mapping — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Pose x_t] --> C[Ray Casting Module]
    B[Sensor Measurement z_t] --> C
    C --> D[For each cell along ray]
    D --> E{Compute distance d}
    E --> F{d in free zone?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Add l_free to cell]
    F -->|No| H{d in hit band?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Skip cell]
    H -->|No| J{d in obstacle band?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Add l_occ to cell]
    J -->|No| L[Beyond z_max, skip]
    G --> M[Log-Odds Update: l_t = l_t-1 + l_inv]
    K --> M
    M --> N[Clamp to l_min, l_max]
    N --> O[Store in Grid Array]
    O --> P[Occupancy Grid Map]
```

## 4.2 Bayesian Update Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prior P m_i] --> C[Multiply by P z_t given m_i x_t]
    B[Measurement z_t] --> C
    C --> D[Normalize by evidence P z_t]
    D --> E[Posterior P m_i given z_t x_t]
    E --> F[Convert to log-odds]
    F --> G[Add to running sum]
    G --> H[Clamp log-odds]
    H --> I[Convert back via sigmoid]
    I --> J[Updated Grid Cell]
```

## 4.3 Cell State Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cell m_i] --> B{Log-odds value l?}
    B -->|l less than threshold_neg| C[FREE - white]
    B -->|threshold_neg ≤ l ≤ threshold_pos| D[UNKNOWN - grey]
    B -->|l greater than threshold_pos| E[OCCUPIED - black]
    C --> F[Published Map]
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

## 4.4 Sensor Ray — Inverse Model Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Sensor at x_t] --> F1[Free Zone: 0 to z_t minus Δz/2]
    F1 --> U[Uncertainty Band: z_t ± Δz/2]
    U --> O[Obstacle Zone: just past hit]
    O --> MX[Beyond z_max: skip]
```

> [!TIP]
> In an actual ray diagram (which cannot be rendered in Mermaid without breaking the syntax rules), the sensor sits at the left, the free zone is the long stretch until the hit, then a narrow uncertainty band, then the occupied cell cluster, then empty/unknown beyond $z_{\max}$. Memorize this layout for the exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: Define occupancy grid mapping. Why is the cell-independence assumption important?**

**Model Answer:**

Occupancy grid mapping is a probabilistic technique that decomposes a robot's environment into a finite set of equally sized cells, where each cell stores the probability that it is occupied by an obstacle, free, or unknown, given all sensor measurements and robot poses up to time $t$.

The cell-independence assumption states that $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$. This assumption is **crucial** because it converts an intractable joint estimation problem (which has $2^N$ possible maps) into $N$ independent scalar estimation problems that can be updated independently and efficiently. Without this assumption, mapping would be computationally infeasible for any realistic environment.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the log-odds representation in occupancy grid mapping? Mention one advantage.**

**Model Answer:**

The log-odds representation transforms the occupancy probability $P(m_i)$ of a cell into a logarithmic ratio:

$$l(m_i) = \log \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}$$

The Bayesian update rule becomes a simple **addition**: $l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{inv}}(m_i, z_t, x_t) - l_0$.

**Advantage:** It converts multiplicative probability updates into additive log-odds updates, which (1) avoids numerical underflow when many small probabilities are multiplied, and (2) makes the implementation computationally efficient. Additionally, it is easy to **clamp** the log-odds to a finite range to prevent overconfidence.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: With neat mathematical derivations, explain the Bayesian formulation of occupancy grid mapping. Also explain the log-odds update rule with a suitable example.** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Bayesian Formulation (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Statement:** Given sensor measurements $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ and known robot poses $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_t\}$, estimate the map $m$.

**Step 2 — Apply the cell-independence assumption:**

$$
P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})
$$

**Step 3 — For each cell, apply Bayes rule recursively:**

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

**Step 4 — The term $P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)$ is the inverse sensor model** — it answers "given this measurement, how likely is this cell to be the source of the return?"

**Step 5 — The recursive form allows incremental updates** as new measurements arrive, without reprocessing the entire history.

#### Part (b) — Log-Odds Update with Example (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define log-odds:**

$$
l(m_i) \triangleq \log \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive additive update rule** (shown in Section 3.1):

$$
l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{\text{inv}}(m_i, z_t, x_t) - l_0
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:** Suppose a cell has prior $P = 0.5$ ($l_0 = 0$). Three consecutive lidar hits contribute $l_{\text{inv}} = 0.7$ each. Then:

- After update 1: $l_1 = 0 + 0.7 - 0 = 0.7 \Rightarrow P_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{0.7}} = 0.668$
- After update 2: $l_2 = 0.7 + 0.7 = 1.4 \Rightarrow P_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{1.4}} = 0.802$
- After update 3: $l_3 = 1.4 + 0.7 = 2.1 \Rightarrow P_3 = 1 - \frac{1}{1+e^{2.1}} = 0.891$

The probability of occupancy rises monotonically and asymptotically toward 1.0 as more evidence accumulates. **Log-odds clamping** at $l_{\max} = 3.5$ prevents this from exceeding 1.0 exactly.

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]

**Q: Describe the inverse sensor model for a range-bearing sensor used in occupancy grid mapping. How does multi-sensor fusion improve grid quality?** (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Inverse Sensor Model for Range-Bearing Sensor (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

A range-bearing sensor (e.g., 2D lidar) returns distance $z_t$ and angle $\theta$. The inverse sensor model specifies $P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ for each cell along the ray.

**Standard piecewise inverse model** (with four zones):

$$
P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } 0 \leq d < z_t - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} \quad \text{(clearly free)} \\
0.5 & \text{if } z_t - \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} < d < z_t + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} \quad \text{(hit uncertainty)} \\
1 & \text{if } z_t + \tfrac{\Delta z}{2} < d < z_{\max} \quad \text{(occupied past hit)} \\
0.5 & \text{if } d \geq z_{\max} \quad \text{(beyond range)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-step meaning:**

1. **Zone 1 — Free zone (0 to $z_t - \Delta z/2$):** The ray clearly traversed this cell without hitting anything, so it is **free** ($P = 0$).
2. **Zone 2 — Uncertainty band ($\pm \Delta z/2$):** The hit could be anywhere in this band, so we leave the cell **unknown** ($P = 0.5$).
3. **Zone 3 — Occupied zone (just past hit):** Cells just past the measured range are most likely to be the obstacle, so $P = 1$.
4. **Zone 4 — Beyond $z_{\max}$:** No measurement info, so cell remains unknown.

> [!NOTE]
> The **range uncertainty $\Delta z$** captures sensor noise and grows with distance in real lidars (typically $\Delta z \propto d$).

#### Part (b) — Multi-Sensor Fusion (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

Single sensors suffer from limitations:
- Lidar: fails on glass and mirrors.
- Sonar: poor angular resolution, specular reflections.
- Camera: lighting-dependent, no depth directly.
- IMU/odometry: drifts over time.

**Fusion strategy:** Treat each sensor as an independent measurement source and apply the **log-odds update sequentially**:

$$
l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + \sum_{k=1}^{K} l_{\text{inv}}^{(k)}(m_i, z_t^{(k)}, x_t) - l_0
$$

where $k$ indexes the $K$ different sensors at time $t$.

**Benefits of fusion:**
1. **Complementary strengths** — Sonar detects glass, lidar detects thin obstacles, cameras add semantic labels.
2. **Reduced uncertainty** — Multiple confirmations reduce variance of $P(m_i)$.
3. **Faster convergence** — A cell reaches its final occupancy belief with fewer time steps.
4. **Robustness** — If one sensor fails (e.g., sunlight blinds camera), the others still update the grid.

In practice, fusion is implemented as a **plug-in architecture** in ROS, where each sensor publishes a separate `OccupancyGrid` update, and a central node combines them using the additive log-odds rule.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing forward and inverse models** — The forward model $P(z \mid m, x)$ predicts measurements from a known map. The inverse model $P(m \mid z, x)$ infers the map from measurements. **Occupancy grid mapping uses the inverse model.** Losing 2 marks here is very common.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the prior $l_0$** — The full update equation includes $-l_0$. Many students write $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{inv}}$ and lose marks.
> 
> 3. **Wrong sigmoid formula** — Writing $P = \frac{1}{1+e^{-l}}$ is equivalent and acceptable, but mixing it up with the wrong sign costs marks. Practice both forms.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the cell-independence assumption** — Always mention this assumption explicitly. It is the *foundation* of why occupancy grids are computationally feasible.
> 
> 5. **Not clamping log-odds** — In a 14-mark question about implementation, forgetting to mention clamping at $l_{\min}, l_{\max}$ is a guaranteed deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Occupancy Grid Mapping**

- **Definition:** A probabilistic, discretized 2-D (or 3-D) map where each cell stores $P(\text{occupied})$.
- **Cell independence:** $P(m \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \prod_{i} P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ — the key simplifying assumption.
- **Three states:** Free, Occupied, Unknown — *never* just two.
- **Bayesian update:** Recursive Bayes filter applied per cell.
- **Log-odds transform:** $l = \log \frac{P}{1-P}$ — converts multiplication to addition.
- **Update rule:** $l_t = l_{t-1} + l_{\text{inv}} - l_0$.
- **Inverse sigmoid:** $P = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^l}$.
- **Inverse sensor model zones:** Free, Uncertainty band, Occupied, Beyond range.
- **Clamping bounds:** Typically $l_{\min} = -2$, $l_{\max} = 3.5$.
- **Inventors:** Moravec and Elfes (1985) — remember this name for viva questions.
- **Common use cases:** Autonomous cars, warehouse AGVs, vacuum robots, Mars rovers.
- **Limitation:** Assumes known robot poses — extension to full SLAM needed for unknown poses.
- **3-D extension:** OctoMap uses octrees for variable-resolution 3-D occupancy grids.
- **Sensors used:** 2-D/3-D lidar, sonar arrays, stereo cameras, depth cameras (e.g., Kinect, RealSense).
- **Output format:** In ROS, `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid` with values in $\{-1, 0, 100\}$ for unknown, free, occupied.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Landmark-Based Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Landmark-Based Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Landmark-Based Map** (also called a **Feature-Based Map** or **Topological–Metric Hybrid Map**) is a compact representation of the environment used in robotics and autonomous systems, in which the environment is abstracted into a collection of **distinct, identifiable, and geometrically meaningful entities** known as **landmarks**. Each landmark is stored as a parametric feature (point, line segment, corner, or higher-order geometric primitive) along with an associated uncertainty covariance, rather than as a dense grid of occupancy values.

In the formal SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) literature, the landmark-based map $\mathcal{M}$ is defined as the set:

$$\mathcal{M} \;=\; \{\, m_i = (\, x_i,\; y_i,\; \Sigma_i \,) \;\mid\; i = 1, 2, \dots, N \,\}$$

where $m_i$ is the $i$-th landmark, $(x_i, y_i)$ is its estimated 2D position in a global reference frame, and $\Sigma_i \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$ is the **posterior covariance matrix** quantifying localization uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Landmark-based maps are the *foundational representation paradigm* for EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM. The student must clearly distinguish them from **occupancy grid maps**, which store a discretized probability of cell occupancy $p(m_{cell})$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist in a new city **without GPS**. Instead of memorizing every street and building (dense map), you only remember a few key landmarks — the *Eiffel Tower*, the *Main Railway Station*, and the *City Museum*. With just these three reference points and a rough estimate of "how far and in which direction" they are from your current location, you can always reorient yourself and navigate. This is **exactly** what a landmark-based map does for a mobile robot.

| Tourist (Human) | Robot (SLAM Agent) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Recognizable monuments | Visually salient features (corners, SIFT/SURF keypoints) |
| Mental estimate of distance/bearing | Range-bearing sensor measurements $z_t = (r, \theta)$ |
| "I think the station is 500 m North" | Gaussian estimate $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ |
| Updated by walking | Updated by motion + measurement models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Landmark-based maps are *sparse, parametric, and probabilistic*. Their computational efficiency grows linearly with the number of landmarks $N$, whereas dense metric maps scale with the *area* of the environment. This makes landmark-based maps the **preferred choice for large-scale outdoor SLAM**.

### 1.3 Core Properties (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

The following standardized metrics appear in KTU exam questions and must be memorized:

- **Time Complexity of EKF-SLAM Update:** $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ per measurement, where $N$ is the number of landmarks in the state vector.
- **Time Complexity of FastSLAM Update:** $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot \log N)$, where $M$ is the number of particles.
- **Landmark Observability Condition:** A landmark must be re-observed from **at least two distinct robot poses** to become *fully observable* and localizeable in 2D.
- **Data Association Confidence Threshold (Mahalanobis Distance):** $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.991$ for 2D landmarks at a 95% confidence gate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The 5-Stage Landmark SLAM Loop

The landmark-based mapping pipeline executes in a continuous loop. Each stage is enumerated below with the underlying *why* and *how*.

**Stage 1 — Sensor Data Acquisition**
- **Why:** All mapping begins with raw observations. Without data, the posterior cannot be updated.
- **How:** The robot's exteroceptive sensors (LiDAR, monocular/stereo camera, sonar) capture a scan $z_t$ at time $t$. Proprioceptive sensors (wheel odometry, IMU) provide the control input $u_t$.

**Stage 2 — Feature Extraction & Landmark Detection**
- **Why:** Raw sensor data is high-dimensional and noisy. We need compact, repeatable descriptors.
- **How:** Algorithms such as **SIFT (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform)**, **SURF**, **ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF)**, or geometric corner detectors (**Harris, Shi-Tomasi**) extract salient keypoints. For LiDAR, line/plane segment extractors (e.g., **Line Segment Detector**) are used.

**Stage 3 — Data Association**
- **Why:** The robot must decide *which* observed feature corresponds to *which* landmark in the map. False associations cause catastrophic filter divergence.
- **How:** Compute the **Mahalanobis distance** $D_M$ between the predicted observation $\hat{z}_t$ and each candidate landmark observation, and apply a **gating threshold** (typically the $\chi^2$ gate):

$$D_M = (\, z_t - \hat{z}_t \,)^T \, S^{-1} \, (\, z_t - \hat{z}_t \,) \;\leq\; \gamma$$

where $S$ is the innovation covariance. If $D_M \leq \gamma$, the observation is accepted as a match; otherwise, a **new landmark** is initialized.

**Stage 4 — State Prediction (Motion Update)**
- **Why:** The robot has moved; we must propagate the belief forward in time.
- **How:** Apply the non-linear motion model $f(\cdot)$ with control input $u_t$:

$$\mu_t^{-} = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \quad ; \quad \Sigma_t^{-} = F_t \, \Sigma_{t-1} \, F_t^T + Q_t$$

where $F_t = \partial f / \partial x$ is the Jacobian and $Q_t$ is process noise covariance.

**Stage 5 — Measurement Update (Correction Step)**
- **Why:** The observation of a landmark corrects the drift accumulated in Stage 4.
- **How:** Compute the **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ and update both the robot pose and landmark positions:

$$K_t = \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T \, (\, H_t \, \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T + R_t \,)^{-1}$$

$$\mu_t = \mu_t^{-} + K_t \, (\, z_t - h(\mu_t^{-}) \,) \quad ; \quad \Sigma_t = (\, I - K_t \, H_t \,) \, \Sigma_t^{-}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Landmark Map Definition | $\mathcal{M} = \{m_i = (x_i, y_i, \Sigma_i)\}$ | $i = 1 \dots N$, positions in meters (m), $\Sigma_i$ in $\text{m}^2$ | $N$ must be finite; assume Gaussian noise |
| Range-Bearing Observation | $z = [r, \phi]^T$ | $r$ in m, $\phi$ in radians (rad) | $r > 0$, $0 \leq \phi < 2\pi$ |
| Observation Model (2D) | $r = \sqrt{(x_l - x_r)^2 + (y_l - y_r)^2}$ | $(x_r, y_r)$ robot pose | Line-of-sight, no occlusion |
| Bearing Model | $\phi = \text{atan2}(y_l - y_r,\; x_l - x_r) - \theta_r$ | $\theta_r$ robot heading in rad | Bounded wrap-around at $\pm \pi$ |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ | Innovation $\nu = z - \hat{z}$, $S \in \mathbb{R}^{2\times 2}$ | $D_M \leq \chi^2_{d, \alpha}$ |
| EKF-SLAM Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $N$ = # landmarks | Holds per measurement update |
| FastSLAM Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ | $M$ = # particles | Tree-based landmark storage |
| Kalman Gain | $K_t = \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ | $H_t$ Jacobian of $h(\cdot)$ | Requires $S$ to be invertible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Landmark-based maps power the perception stacks of **autonomous vehicles (Waymo, Tesla FSD)**, **drone swarms in GPS-denied environments**, **Mars rovers (NASA's Curiosity & Perseverance)**, and **warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** from Amazon Kiva and Fetch Robotics. Their sparse nature allows real-time operation on embedded ARM-based SoCs (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Orin).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EKF-SLAM Measurement Update for a 2D Point Landmark

**Problem Setup:** A robot at pose $\xi_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^T$ observes a landmark at true global position $l = (l_x, l_y)^T$. The observation is a range-bearing tuple $z_t = (r, \phi)^T$ corrupted by zero-mean Gaussian noise $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$, where $R = \text{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\phi^2)$.

We seek to derive the full EKF update that simultaneously corrects *both* the robot pose *and* the landmark position.

---

**Step 1 — Write the Non-Linear Observation Model**

The range is the Euclidean distance from robot to landmark, and the bearing is the angle of the landmark *relative to the robot's heading*:

$$r = \sqrt{(l_x - x_t)^2 + (l_y - y_t)^2} \;+\; \varepsilon_r$$

$$\phi = \text{atan2}(\, l_y - y_t,\; l_x - x_t \,) \;-\; \theta_t \;+\; \varepsilon_\phi$$

In compact vector form:

$$z_t \;=\; h(\xi_t, l) \;+\; \varepsilon_t$$

---

**Step 2 — Define the Augmented State Vector**

EKF-SLAM stacks the robot pose and *all* $N$ landmarks into a single high-dimensional state vector $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^{(3+2N) \times 1}$:

$$\mu_t \;=\; \Big[\, x_t,\; y_t,\; \theta_t,\; m_{1,x},\; m_{1,y},\; \dots,\; m_{N,x},\; m_{N,y} \,\Big]^T$$

The covariance $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{(3+2N) \times (3+2N)}$ is correspondingly large. The block relating the $i$-th landmark to the robot is denoted $\Sigma_{rr, l_i l_i}$.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the Observation Jacobian $H_t$**

The Jacobian of $h(\cdot)$ with respect to the augmented state must capture partial derivatives w.r.t. *both* the robot pose and the landmark. The first three columns (robot pose) are:

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial x_t} = \frac{-(l_x - x_t)}{r} = -\frac{\Delta x}{r}$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial y_t} = \frac{-(l_y - y_t)}{r} = -\frac{\Delta y}{r}$$

$$\frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta_t} = 0$$

For the bearing:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_t} = \frac{\Delta y}{r^2} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y_t} = \frac{-\Delta x}{r^2} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta_t} = -1$$

Stacking these into the full Jacobian:

$$H_t \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -\Delta x / r & -\Delta y / r & 0 & \dots & \Delta x / r & \Delta y / r & \dots \\ \Delta y / r^2 & -\Delta x / r^2 & -1 & \dots & -\Delta y / r^2 & \Delta x / r^2 & \dots \end{bmatrix}$$

The non-zero entries at columns corresponding to the observed landmark $j$ are the **negatives** of the robot-partial entries — a direct consequence of the $\pm \Delta$ symmetry.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Innovation Covariance $S_t$**

The innovation covariance captures the total uncertainty in the *predicted* observation:

$$S_t \;=\; H_t \, \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T \;+\; R_t$$

where $R_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_r^2, \sigma_\phi^2)$ is the sensor noise covariance.

---

**Step 5 — Compute the Kalman Gain $K_t$**

The Kalman gain $K_t$ has dimensions $(3+2N) \times 2$ and is computed as:

$$K_t \;=\; \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1} \;=\; \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T \, (\, H_t \, \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T + R_t \,)^{-1}$$

---

**Step 6 — Apply the State and Covariance Update**

$$\mu_t \;=\; \mu_t^{-} \;+\; K_t \cdot \nu_t \quad \text{where} \quad \nu_t = z_t - h(\mu_t^{-})$$

$$\Sigma_t \;=\; (\, I - K_t \, H_t \,) \, \Sigma_t^{-}$$

---

**Step 7 — Verify Computational Cost**

The dominant operation is the inversion of $S_t$, which is a fixed $2 \times 2$ matrix (independent of $N$). However, the matrix products $H_t \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T$ involve the $(3+2N) \times (3+2N)$ covariance matrix, giving an asymptotic complexity of:

$$T(n) \;=\; \mathcal{O}((3+2N)^2) \;\approx\; \mathcal{O}(N^2)$$

This quadratic growth is the **EKF-SLAM computational bottleneck** and the primary motivation for FastSLAM's $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ approach.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Landmark vs. Occupancy Grid Maps

| Property | Landmark-Based Map | Occupancy Grid Map |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Representation | Sparse set of features $m_i$ | Dense grid of $p(m_{cell})$ |
| Storage Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | $\mathcal{O}(A / r^2)$ for area $A$, resolution $r$ |
| Update Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ (EKF), $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ (FastSLAM) | $\mathcal{O}(A / r^2)$ per scan |
| Suitable For | Large-scale, structured environments | Small-scale, unstructured environments |
| Path Planning Integration | Requires post-processing into graph | Direct via search algorithms (A*, D*) |
| Uncertainty Modeling | Explicit Gaussian $\Sigma_i$ | Per-cell Bernoulli probability |
| KTU Typical Question | EKF-SLAM derivation, data association | Inverse sensor model, log-odds update |
| Famous Implementations | ORB-SLAM, LSD-SLAM, RTAB-Map | OctoMap, Cartographer (hybrid) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EKF-SLAM Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **block-level functional architecture** of an EKF-SLAM system, showing how sensor inputs flow through the prediction-correction loop and how the landmark map is incrementally constructed and updated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Inputs] --> B[Odometry u_t]
    A --> C[LiDAR or Camera z_t]
    B --> D[Motion Model f mu, u]
    C --> E[Feature Extraction ORB SIFT]
    E --> F[Data Association Mahalanobis Gate]
    F --> G{Match Found?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Innovation nu_t]
    G -- No --> I[Initialize New Landmark]
    I --> J[Augment State Vector]
    H --> K[Kalman Gain K_t]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    D --> L[Predicted State mu_t minus]
    L --> M[Innovation Covariance S_t]
    M --> K
    K --> N[State Update mu_t, Sigma_t]
    N --> O[Landmark Map M]
    O --> P[Pose Estimate xi_t]
    P --> Q[Next Time Step t plus 1]
    Q --> A
```

### 4.2 Landmark Data Association Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed Feature z_t] --> B[Predict Observation z_hat]
    B --> C[Compute Mahalanobis D_M]
    C --> D{D_M le chi2 gate}
    D -- Yes --> E[Associate with Existing Landmark m_j]
    D -- No --> F[Create New Landmark m_new]
    E --> G[Update Filter EKF Step]
    F --> H[Initialize Covariance Sigma_new]
    H --> G
```

### 4.3 Landmark Map Structure — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    R[Robot Pose xi_t] -- range r --> L1[Landmark m1]
    R -- range r --> L2[Landmark m2]
    R -- range r --> L3[Landmark m3]
    R -- range r --> Ln[Landmark mN]
    L1 -.uncertainty Sigma1.-> U1[Ellipse Covariance 2D]
    L2 -.uncertainty Sigma2.-> U2[Ellipse Covariance 2D]
    L3 -.uncertainty Sigma3.-> U3[Ellipse Covariance 2D]
    Ln -.uncertainty SigmaN.-> UN[Ellipse Covariance 2D]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a landmark-based map. List any four desirable properties of a good landmark for SLAM applications.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A landmark-based map is a sparse, parametric representation of the environment consisting of a finite set of geometric features $m_i = (x_i, y_i, \Sigma_i)$, where $(x_i, y_i)$ is the landmark position and $\Sigma_i$ is the associated uncertainty covariance.

Four desirable properties:
1. **Distinctiveness** — easily distinguishable from other landmarks via feature descriptors.
2. **Stability** — invariant to changes in lighting, viewpoint, and weather.
3. **Abundance** — sufficiently many landmarks should be observable in the environment.
4. **Static** — landmarks should not move over time (or their motion must be predictable).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for the four properties — total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is data association in landmark-based SLAM? State the Mahalanobis distance formula used for gated nearest-neighbor association.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Data association** is the process of matching a newly observed sensor feature $z_t$ to an existing landmark $m_j$ in the map, or declaring it as a new landmark if no match exists. It is critical because false associations lead to catastrophic filter divergence.

The **Mahalanobis distance** is:

$$D_M = (\, z_t - \hat{z}_t \,)^T \, S^{-1} \, (\, z_t - \hat{z}_t \,)$$

The observation is associated with landmark $j$ if $D_M \leq \gamma$, where $\gamma = \chi^2_{d,\alpha}$ is the chi-squared gate (e.g., $\gamma = 5.991$ for 2D landmarks at 95% confidence).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for gating condition — total 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester]**

**(a)** Derive the EKF-SLAM measurement update equations for a 2D point landmark observed by a range-bearing sensor. Clearly state the observation model, the Jacobian matrix, the innovation covariance, the Kalman gain, and the final state and covariance update. *(**7 Marks**)*

**(b)** Explain the data association problem in landmark-based SLAM. Discuss the **nearest-neighbor** and **joint compatibility branch and bound (JCBB)** methods, and compare their computational complexity. *(**7 Marks**)*

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) EKF-SLAM Measurement Update Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Observation Model.** For a robot at pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ observing landmark $l = (l_x, l_y)$:

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_x - x_t)^2 + (l_y - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_y - y_t,\; l_x - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Jacobian Computation.** Let $\Delta x = l_x - x_t$, $\Delta y = l_y - y_t$, and $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$. The full Jacobian w.r.t. the augmented state $\mu_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t, m_{1,x}, m_{1,y}, \dots]^T$ is:

$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\Delta x / r & -\Delta y / r & 0 & \dots & \Delta x / r & \Delta y / r & \dots \\ \Delta y / r^2 & -\Delta x / r^2 & -1 & \dots & -\Delta y / r^2 & \Delta x / r^2 & \dots \end{bmatrix}$$

The first three columns correspond to the robot pose, and the non-zero entries at the observed landmark's columns are the negatives of the robot-partials.

**Step 3 (1 mark): Innovation Covariance.**

$$S_t = H_t \, \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T + R_t$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Kalman Gain.**

$$K_t = \Sigma_t^{-} \, H_t^T \, S_t^{-1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): State and Covariance Update.**

$$\mu_t = \mu_t^{-} + K_t \, (z_t - h(\mu_t^{-})) \quad ; \quad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t^{-}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Computational Complexity Discussion.** The matrix products scale as $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ where $N$ is the number of landmarks, because $\Sigma_t \in \mathbb{R}^{(3+2N) \times (3+2N)}$. Only the $2 \times 2$ inversion of $S_t$ is constant-time.

---

**(b) Data Association Methods (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Data association is the process of matching each observed feature $z_t$ in the current sensor scan to either an existing landmark in the map $\mathcal{M}$ or a new landmark.

**Nearest-Neighbor (NN) Method (2 marks):**
- For each observation $z_t$, compute the Mahalanobis distance $D_M$ to *every* existing landmark.
- Assign the observation to the landmark with the *minimum* $D_M$, provided $D_M \leq \gamma$ (the gating threshold, e.g., $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.991$).
- If no landmark passes the gate, initialize a new landmark.
- **Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(N)$ per observation, $N$ landmarks, single hypothesis.

**Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB) Method (3 marks):**
- JCBB evaluates *combinations* of observation-to-landmark pairings, not just individual matches.
- It uses a **branch-and-bound** search over the assignment hypothesis space.
- A hypothesis is *jointly compatible* if **all** observation-landmark pairs in it individually satisfy the Mahalanobis gate and the resulting joint innovation covariance $S_{joint}$ remains well-conditioned.
- The bound prunes branches whose partial joint innovation cost already exceeds the current best.
- **Complexity:** Worst-case $\mathcal{O}(N^k)$ for $k$ observations, but pruning drastically reduces it in practice.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Nearest Neighbor | JCBB |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hypothesis Space | Single best match | Combinatorial search |
| Robustness to Clutter | Low | High |
| Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | Worst-case exponential, pruned in practice |
| Use Case | Sparse, low-clutter environments | Dense, cluttered environments |

---

**Question B [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the EKF-SLAM algorithm with a clear block diagram. Discuss why the computational complexity of EKF-SLAM is $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ and how FastSLAM overcomes this limitation. *(**7 Marks**)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **landmark-based maps** with **occupancy grid maps**. Tabulate at least six distinguishing parameters. Discuss one real-world application where landmark-based maps are preferred and justify your answer. *(**7 Marks**)*

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) EKF-SLAM Algorithm & Complexity (7 Marks):**

**EKF-SLAM Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**

1. **Initialization:** Set $\mu_0 = \xi_0$, $\Sigma_0 = 0_{3+2N}$ (all zeros for an empty map).
2. **Prediction:** Apply the motion model — $\mu_t^{-} = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$ and $\Sigma_t^{-} = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$.
3. **Observation:** Acquire range-bearing measurement $z_t$ to landmark $j$.
4. **Association:** Compute $D_M$ and gate-match $z_t$ to landmark $j$ or initialize new landmark.
5. **Update:** Compute $K_t = \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$, then $\mu_t = \mu_t^{-} + K_t (z_t - h(\mu_t^{-}))$ and $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t^{-}$.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1** above. The student should sketch the *Prediction–Correction loop* with explicit labels: Motion Update, Observation, Data Association, Kalman Gain, State Update, Map Augmentation.

**Complexity Discussion (2 marks):**
- The covariance matrix $\Sigma_t$ has dimensions $(3+2N) \times (3+2N)$.
- The product $H_t \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T$ involves the full covariance, yielding $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ per step.
- For $N \to 10^6$ landmarks (city-scale SLAM), this becomes computationally infeasible.
- **FastSLAM** reduces complexity to $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ by factoring the SLAM posterior using a **Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF)** — each particle maintains an independent EKF for *each* landmark stored in a balanced tree.

---

**(b) Landmark vs. Occupancy Grid Maps (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):** (See table in Section 3.2 of these notes. The student should reproduce at least six rows.)

**Key Distinctions (2 marks):**
- Landmark maps are **sparse and parametric**; occupancy grids are **dense and non-parametric**.
- Landmark maps store Gaussian estimates; grids store Bernoulli probabilities per cell.
- Landmark maps scale with the *number of features*; grids scale with the *physical area*.

**Real-World Application (2 marks):**
**Autonomous Driving in Urban Environments** (e.g., Waymo, Cruise, Tesla FSD). Landmark-based maps are preferred because:
- The environment is **large-scale** (entire city, area $\sim 10^9 \text{ m}^2$), making dense grids infeasible.
- Distinctive features (traffic signs, building corners, lane markings) are abundant and **easily detectable** by cameras and LiDAR.
- Localization must be **real-time** ($\leq 100$ ms) on embedded compute platforms; sparse updates are computationally tractable.
- The map must support **loop closure** detection across re-visited areas, which is naturally handled by landmark-based descriptors (e.g., ORB, NetVLAD).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Landmark Map Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing observation model with state model** — students often write the Jacobian w.r.t. the *state* but forget the sign convention. The landmark partials are *positive* of the robot-partials, not the same sign.
> 2. **Forgetting the noise covariance $R_t$** in the innovation covariance $S_t = H \Sigma H^T + R$. Omitting $R_t$ gives a Kalman gain that is unstable for high-noise sensors.
> 3. **Not stating the chi-squared gate value explicitly** — Examiners expect $\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.991$ to be quoted, not just the word "threshold."
> 4. **In data association, students write NN algorithm but skip JCBB** — both must be explained for full 7 marks.
> 5. **Complexity claims without justification** — stating "EKF-SLAM is $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$" without referencing the $(3+2N) \times (3+2N)$ covariance matrix size loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Real-world application lacks quantitative justification** — "landmark maps are used in self-driving cars" is too vague. Always cite *area scale*, *latency constraint*, and *sensor modality*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **landmark-based map** is a sparse, parametric set $\mathcal{M} = \{m_i = (x_i, y_i, \Sigma_i)\}$ representing distinguishable environmental features.
- The **observation model** for a 2D point landmark uses range $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ and bearing $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta_t$.
- The **Jacobian $H_t$** has the sign-flipped structure: robot-partials are $-\Delta x/r, -\Delta y/r$ for range; landmark-partials are $+\Delta x/r, +\Delta y/r$.
- The **innovation covariance** is $S_t = H_t \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T + R_t$ — *never omit $R_t$*.
- The **Kalman gain** is $K_t = \Sigma_t^{-} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$, dimensions $(3+2N) \times 2$.
- **Data association** uses the **Mahalanobis distance** $D_M = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ with a **chi-squared gate** ($\chi^2_{2, 0.95} = 5.991$ for 2D).
- **EKF-SLAM complexity** is $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ due to the large covariance matrix; **FastSLAM** reduces this to $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ via Rao-Blackwellized particle filtering.
- **NN (Nearest Neighbor)** is $\mathcal{O}(N)$ but fragile; **JCBB (Joint Compatibility BB)** is robust but exponentially complex in the worst case.
- Landmark maps are preferred for **large-scale outdoor SLAM** (autonomous vehicles, planetary rovers, drone swarms); occupancy grids suit **small-scale indoor SLAM** (domestic vacuum robots, warehouse AMRs).
- The **state augmentation step** (initializing a new landmark) is the only point at which the state dimension increases, and it is critical to set the initial covariance $\Sigma_{new} = J \Sigma_{robot} J^T + R$ properly.
- **Loop closure** in landmark-based SLAM is detected by descriptor matching (e.g., ORB or Bag-of-Words) and triggers a graph optimization (pose graph SLAM / bundle adjustment) that **globally** corrects the map.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SLAM for Dynamic Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT502 - ADVANCED TOPICS IN ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE & MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** SLAM for Dynamic Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** in the context of **Dynamic Environments** refers to the extended class of SLAM algorithms that can construct and continuously update a consistent map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating the ego-pose (position and orientation) of the sensor/robot, under the explicit assumption that a significant proportion of the perceived scene contains **moving entities** (humans, vehicles, animals, movable furniture, etc.) whose motion must be detected, filtered, or modeled rather than treated as static landmarks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRAT502 – Module 4):**
> *"SLAM in dynamic environments extends classical SLAM by integrating dynamic-object detection, semantic segmentation, and motion-consistency checks so that the front-end of the SLAM pipeline discards or down-weights observations belonging to independently moving objects (IMOs), thereby preserving the integrity of the static background map."*

### 1.1.1 Standard Notation Used Across the Module

Let the following symbols be defined as the standard **KTU 2024 Scheme notation** for this module:

- $\mathcal{X}_k$ — Robot pose (state vector) at discrete time step $k$
- $u_k$ — Control input (odometry/IMU) applied between $k-1$ and $k$
- $z_k$ — Sensor observation (LiDAR, RGB-D, monocular image) at time $k$
- $m_i$ — The $i^{th}$ static landmark in the map
- $\mathcal{M}_k = \{m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_N\}$ — The set of all static map features up to time $k$
- $\mathcal{D}_k$ — The set of *dynamic* features/objects at time $k$
- $P(\mathcal{X}_k, \mathcal{M}_k \mid z_{1:k}, u_{1:k})$ — Joint posterior over trajectory and static map

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The standard static-world SLAM posterior assumes $\mathcal{D}_k = \emptyset$. Dynamic SLAM **explicitly partitions** the observation into $\mathcal{M}_k$ and $\mathcal{D}_k$, then estimates the posterior **conditional only on the static set**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Tourist in a Moving Hall of Mirrors"

Imagine you are a **blindfolded tourist** dropped into a large **fashion mall** at night. You must:

1. **Build a mental map** of the mall (where the pillars, escalators, and shop counters are).
2. **Track your own location** within that map.
3. Avoid being confused by the **dozens of shoppers walking past you**, the **rolling luggage carts**, and the **automatic sliding doors** that constantly open and close.

> If you tried to use those walking shoppers as "landmarks" (as classical SLAM would), you would conclude (falsely) that the entire mall is warping around you. Your map would be a chaotic mess.

> **The Dynamic SLAM solution** is exactly what a smart tourist does: **mentally subtract the moving people** ("they don't belong to the building") and use **only the pillars, walls, and floors** as anchors for your map. The tourists are *detected* (by their motion), *discarded* (as unreliable landmarks), and *ignored* for localization — though they may still be *tracked* as a separate task (pedestrian detection).

This is, in essence, the philosophy of **SLAM for Dynamic Environments**.

---

## 1.3 Why Standard SLAM Fails in Dynamic Worlds

| Standard SLAM Assumption | Reality in Dynamic Environments |
|---|---|
| The world is **rigid** and **time-invariant** | People, vehicles, furniture move continuously |
| All feature correspondences are caused by **ego-motion** | A large fraction of optical flow is caused by **object motion** |
| The map, once built, can be reused | The map of dynamic regions must be continuously **updated or pruned** |
| Outliers are sparse and uniformly distributed | Dynamic objects produce **structured, correlated outliers** (entire regions of the image) |
| Data association is solved by RANSAC on a few outliers | Classical RANSAC breaks down when **>50 % of the scene is moving** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** A common student mistake is to confuse *sensor noise* (random, low-magnitude, Gaussian-like) with *dynamic outliers* (structured, large-magnitude, spatially coherent). Dynamic SLAM must combat the **latter**, and that requires a fundamentally different front-end pipeline.

---

## 1.4 High-Level Taxonomy of Dynamic SLAM Approaches

The modern literature (and the KTU 2024 syllabus) groups dynamic SLAM methods into **four major paradigms**:

1. **Geometric / Motion-Consistency Methods** — Use epipolar geometry, scene flow, or multi-view geometry to flag points that do not obey the static-scene motion model. (Examples: Moving Consistency Check, REMODE)
2. **Semantic-Segmentation-Based Methods** — Use a pre-trained deep neural network (Mask R-CNN, YOLACT, SegNet) to mask out known dynamic object classes (people, cars, cyclists) *before* the SLAM front-end. (Examples: DS-SLAM, DynaSLAM, MaskFusion)
3. **Probabilistic / Bayesian Methods** — Maintain a per-feature probability of being static vs. dynamic and weight its contribution to the pose graph accordingly. (Examples: Dynamic SLAM using EM, Cotter et al.)
4. **Hybrid / Multi-Modal Methods** — Combine the above with depth cues, optical flow, IMU pre-integration, or LiDAR clustering for robustness. (Examples: VDO-SLAM, ReFusion, MID-Fusion)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "classify Dynamic SLAM approaches," remember the mnemonic **G-S-P-H** → **G**eometric, **S**emantic, **P**robabilistic, **H**ybrid. This single line can fetch you 2 marks instantly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dynamic SLAM Problem — Formal Statement

The classical static SLAM posterior is:

$$
P(\mathcal{X}_{0:k}, \mathcal{M} \mid z_{1:k}, u_{1:k})
$$

Dynamic SLAM **augments the state** to include the set of dynamic objects $\mathcal{D}_k$ and their individual trajectories:

$$
P(\mathcal{X}_{0:k}, \mathcal{M}, \mathcal{D}_{0:k} \mid z_{1:k}, u_{1:k})
$$

The system must then **jointly** estimate:
- The robot trajectory $\mathcal{X}_{0:k}$
- The static background map $\mathcal{M}$
- The **dynamic object states** (position, velocity, shape) $\mathcal{D}_{0:k}$

### 2.1.1 Why This Is Hard — The Three Open Problems

1. **Detection-Then-Estimate vs. Estimate-Then-Detect Chicken-and-Egg:** You need a map to detect movers; you need to detect movers to build a clean map.
2. **Data Association Across Frames for Movers:** A person in frame $k$ must be linked to the *same* person in frame $k+1$, despite large appearance and pose changes.
3. **Computational Tractability:** Jointly tracking the ego-pose, the static map, *and* every moving entity is exponential in the number of objects.

---

## 2.2 Operational Pipeline of a Modern Dynamic SLAM System

Below is the standard 5-stage processing chain used by state-of-the-art systems (e.g., **DynaSLAM**, **DS-SLAM**, **VDO-SLAM**). This is the single most important diagram you will encounter in Module 4.

### Stage 1 — Sensor Data Acquisition
- Input: RGB frame $I_k$, Depth frame $D_k$, optional IMU $u_k$ and/or LiDAR scan.

### Stage 2 — Semantic Segmentation (Deep Learning Front-End)
- A CNN (Mask R-CNN, YOLOv8-Seg, SegFormer) produces per-pixel class labels.
- Pixels belonging to *potentially movable* classes (person, car, bicycle, dog, cat) are flagged.
- Output: A binary **dynamicity prior mask** $S_k(x,y) \in \{0, 1\}$.

### Stage 3 — Geometric Verification (Epipolar / Scene-Flow Check)
- For every candidate feature, compute its **reprojection error** against the static-scene motion model.
- If the residual is greater than a threshold $r_{th}$, the feature is flagged as a **geometric outlier**.
- Output: A **motion-consistency mask** $G_k(x,y) \in \{0, 1\}$.

### Stage 4 — Mask Fusion
- Combine the two masks. A pixel is declared *truly dynamic* if either:
  $$S_k(x,y) = 1 \quad \text{AND} \quad G_k(x,y) = 1 \quad \text{(strict mode)}$$
  $$\text{or} \quad S_k(x,y) = 1 \quad \text{OR} \quad G_k(x,y) = 1 \quad \text{(lenient mode)}$$
- A *potentially dynamic object* (PDO) is the connected component of such pixels.

### Stage 5 — Pose Optimization and Mapping
- The static pixels (those with $S_k = 0$ **and** $G_k = 0$) are used to:
  1. Extract ORB/SIFT features
  2. Match them with the map
  3. Solve the Perspective-n-Point (PnP) problem for ego-pose
  4. Run Bundle Adjustment
- The dynamic pixels are **either discarded** (foreground subtraction mode) **or handed to a separate tracker** (object SLAM mode).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every formula in this table.** It covers approximately **70 % of the numerical/analytical marks** for this topic in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Variable Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static-SLAM Joint Posterior | $P(\mathcal{X}_{0:k}, \mathcal{M} \mid z_{1:k}, u_{1:k})$ | Probability of trajectory + map given all observations and controls |
| 2 | Dynamic-SLAM Augmented Posterior | $P(\mathcal{X}_{0:k}, \mathcal{M}, \mathcal{D}_{0:k} \mid z_{1:k}, u_{1:k})$ | Includes dynamic-object trajectories in the state |
| 3 | Pinhole Camera Projection | $s\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K [R \mid t] \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $K$ = intrinsic matrix; $(R,t)$ = ego-motion; $s$ = scale (depth) |
| 4 | Epipolar Constraint | $x_2^{\top} F x_1 = 0$ | $F$ = fundamental matrix; $x_1, x_2$ = corresponding points in two views |
| 5 | Sampson Distance (Geometric Outlier Score) | $d = \dfrac{(x_2^{\top} F x_1)^2}{(Fx_1)_1^2 + (Fx_1)_2^2 + (F^{\top}x_2)_1^2 + (F^{\top}x_2)_2^2}$ | First-order approximation to the geometric error. $d > \tau \Rightarrow$ dynamic |
| 6 | Optical Flow Constraint | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | $I_x, I_y$ = spatial gradients; $I_t$ = temporal gradient; $(u,v)$ = flow |
| 7 | Scene Flow Decomposition | $\vec{SF} = \vec{F}_{ego} + \vec{F}_{obj}$ | Total flow = flow due to ego-motion + flow due to object motion |
| 8 | 3D Point Reprojection Error | $e_i = \pi(\mathcal{X}_k, m_i) - z_{k,i}$ | $\pi(\cdot)$ = projection function; minimized via Bundle Adjustment |
| 9 | Static-Feature Probability Update | $p_s^{(k+1)} = (1 - \alpha) p_s^{(k)} + \alpha \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{consistent at } k+1]$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate; $\mathbb{1}$ = indicator function |
| 10 | Dynamic-to-Static Threshold | $\text{label}(m_i) = \begin{cases} \text{static} & \text{if } p_s^{(k)} \geq \theta \\ \text{dynamic} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | $\theta$ typically $\in [0.5, 0.9]$ |
| 11 | Weighted Bundle Adjustment Cost | $\min_{\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{M}} \sum_k \sum_i w_{k,i} \cdot \rho(e_{k,i}^{\top} \Sigma_{k,i}^{-1} e_{k,i})$ | $w_{k,i}$ = feature weight; $\rho(\cdot)$ = Huber/Cauchy robust kernel |
| 12 | IoU for Mask Evaluation | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert S_k \cap G_k \vert}{\vert S_k \cup G_k \vert}$ | Intersection-over-Union of semantic and geometric masks |
| 13 | Hamming Distance for ORB Matching | $d_{ORB} = \sum_{j=1}^{256} \text{XOR}(b_1^j, b_2^j)$ | Used in feature matching, must be below a threshold for inliers |
| 14 | ATE / RPE (Trajectory Error) | $\text{ATE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \Vert \text{trans}(\mathcal{X}_k^{-1}\mathcal{X}_k^{\text{gt}}) \Vert^2}$ | Absolute Trajectory Error — gold standard for SLAM evaluation |
| 15 | EM Convergence Bound | $\log P(z \mid \theta^{(t+1)}) \geq \log P(z \mid \theta^{(t)})$ | EM is used in probabilistic Dynamic SLAM for joint estimation |

> [!NOTE]
